spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Overview of the Course
Private equity (PE) funds have a sizeable and growing influence on worldwide capital
markets. In this course, we examine the PE industry, with a particular focus on the US
Leveraged Buyout industry – why has it been so successful at attracting capital, and
what do firms do to generate returns for themselves and their investors. That includes
not only finance, but also the strategic and operational factors that create a successful
investment. Topics include:
● The Private Equity industry – its history, structure, players and adjacent
industries
● What kind of company makes an attractive private equity investment?
● How do private equity funds add value to their investments (operational
improvement, management incentives, balance sheet)
● What do private equity funds do when ‘things go wrong’?
● How do private equity funds create successful exits from their investments?
The course is designed to be relevant not only to students considering a career in
private equity, but to those in related careers: investment banking, commercial banking,
fund placement and line management of leveraged companies.
Learning Goals
At the conclusion of this course, the student should be able to understand:
● Why has PE become such a major force in global capital markets?
● How does the PE industry work?
● What are the common themes that define and create PE successes?
● What do PE firms do when ‘things go wrong?’
● What are the differences and commonalities between leveraged buyouts and
venture capital (VC)?
● How can I make better lending and investment decisions by thinking like a PE
pro?
spans.label: DESCRIP

Grading

spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Take-private deal proposal & short pitch. In recent years, Private Equity firms have
increasingly turned to public markets as a deal source. Rather than buying companies in
the private market, they instead choose to take a public company private. In 2022, for
instance, Vista Equity Partners and Elliott Management teamed up to acquire
cloud-computing company Citrix Systems. This short pitch will be graded as part of
your take-private deal proposal.
For this assignment you will be assigned (by Tues, Jan 30) to a team of 4-6 students.
Your team is required to put together a pitch deck presenting a take-private deal
proposal to the investment committee at your private equity firm. The deck should
include approximately 12 - 18 slides (plus appendix materials if needed). Include the
following points:
● Company to be acquired and acquisition price range
● Industry overview
○ What makes this industry attractive for PE?
○ Competitor analysis
○ Industry trends
○ Drivers, Risks
● Comparable deals (comps), if applicable
● Value creation strategy/Deal Thesis
○ There are many different ways to create value. What makes most sense
here?
4

■ Are there any comparables, i.e. other companies that have pursued
similar or related strategies? Other PE deals in this sector that you
think provide guideposts for what can be done?
● Exit strategy
● LBO model with assumptions
● MOIC and IRR calculations
Your team will also prepare a no more than 7
minute pitch to present in class. You can designate two team members to present.
Choose 3-5 slides from your full pitch deck to present the highlights and key points
underlying your idea.
spans.label: ASSIGN

Academic Integrity. Sanctions for academic dishonesty can include failing grades
and/or suspension from the university.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Class Sessions
WED, JAN 15
Introduction to Private Equity Funds
In this introductory lecture we will answer the following questions, which will provide
the foundation for our remaining sessions:
o Why do we have a ‘Private Equity’ industry? Where did it come from and what
is its unique role in capital markets? How does it differ from other family run or
other private businesses?
5

o What is the structure of the global Leveraged Buyout (LBO) industry? How is
the same and how is it different in major geographies around the world?
o Where does Private Equity money come from? Why do limited partners invest?
What are they trying to achieve?
o Given the above, what are the motivations for a PE investor? Where are they
convergent and divergent vs. their limited partners and financing sources?
TUES (Brandeis Monday), JAN 21
What makes an attractive Private Equity Deal?
Case: Hubbardton Forge (A) (via MOODLE)
<> Hubbardton Forge LBO model due <>
Bunker Hill Capital is considering an acquisition of Hubbardton Forge, a lighting
company. Is Hubbardton Forge a “good business”? What valuation would you put on
the business? What are your primary risks and concerns?
We will introduce the class to ‘LBO math’, using a real LBO deal as an example,
answering the following:
o What types of businesses make attractive LBO investments and why?
o How does LBO financing produce such high returns?
o What are the similarities and differences between an attractive LBO and an
attractive corporate M&A transaction?
We will also establish the basic ‘Private Equity Success Formula’ that we will explore in
subsequent classes, using the example of Hubbardton Forge:
Buy Right + Add Value + Exit Well = Attractive PE Returns
WED, JAN 22
Jon Ginns, ACON Investments - Guest Speaker
MON, JAN 27
Add Value & Growth Equity - Exit or No Exit?
Case: Marlin Outdoor (via MOODLE)
We will go over the Marlin Outdoor case == Birmingham Capital has the opportunity to
take a quick gain on its Marlin Outdoor investment. Should it sell now, or hold on for
the hope of a higher return later? Read the case and come to class prepared to discuss.
6

WED, JAN 29
Add Value Approaches / Strategics
We will explore how private equity funds add value to their portfolio companies,
discussing:
o How are PE firms organizing themselves to add value to their portfolios?
o What are the most important tools that a PE owner will use to add value?
o How can acquisitions add value to a portfolio company?
o How do PE firms incent their managers to add value?
o What governance structures do funds employ to provide oversight to their
portfolio company investments?
MON, FEB 3
Hugh MacArthur, Bain & Company PE practice - Guest Speaker
WED, FEB 5
Exit or Add Value?
Case: Construction Partners (via MOODLE)
<> Construction Partners writeup due <>
Guest Speaker: Mike McKay, Bain and Company. CPI has been an awesome
investment, but – is it time to exit? Or recap? Or IPO? Wow – so many choices!
MON, FEB 10
Buy and Build / Dividend Recaps
The need to exit and the positioning for exit is a critical difference between a PE buyer
and a corporate buyer. We will address the following:
o What are the major opportunities for exit, and when is each most appropriate?
o What are the specific things that a private equity owner will do to position a
portfolio company for attractive sale?
o When is the ‘optimal’ time for exit? How do PE funds manage the ongoing
tension between ‘declaring victory’ and getting cash back to investors vs.
continuing to compound capital and hope for a larger payday later?
7

What is a dividend recap, and when is it an appropriate part of an exit strategy?
WED, FEB 12
Riddhish Rege, JLC Infrastructure - Guest Speaker.
MON, FEB 24
Alumni Day / Take-private deal presentations
<> Full deal proposals due noon, today <>
Today, each team will have no more than 7 minutes to present their take-private deal
proposal. Use this time efficiently to emphasize what makes this a good deal. Please do
not run over the 7 minute maximum time allotment. Your grade will be reduced if you
do.
WED, FEB 26
Alumni Day / Take-private deal presentations continued
Today we will welcome a few Fin231 alumns who are now working in PE. They will
share their story and you will have an opportunity to ask questions about their work
and how they got to where they are.
MON, MAR 3
Venture Capital
Case: ShopKeep - Taking Back Mainstreet through Payments and Points of Sale. (via
MOODLE)
<> ShopKeep short answers due <>
Guest Speaker: Brian Hirsch, Tribeca Venture Capital
WED, MAR 5
Final Exam (70 minutes) - in class.

spans.text:
Chemistry 11A covers a wide array of topics, embracing aspects of descriptive, as well as
quantitative, chemistry. No prior study of chemistry is assumed. The course begins by looking at
the atomic foundation of matter, the elements, and the organization of the Periodic Table, working
its way up to an examination of how atoms are bonded together to form larger units of matter.
Students who complete this course will gain understanding of the laws that govern the behavior of
matter in the gas phase, as well as knowledge of the major types of chemical reactions and how to
represent them. A strong focus is put on learning methods of creative problem-solving—using the
material to develop the critical thinking tools essential to solving unfamiliar problems—skills that
carry students far beyond the confines of the classroom. When taken in conjunction with
Chemistry 11B and the associated lab courses (Chemistry 18A and 18B), this course meets the
general, analytic, and inorganic requirements of medical, dental, and veterinary schools.
Success in this four-credit course assumes that students will spend a minimum of 9 hours of study time
per week in preparation for class (readings, problem sets, recitations, SI sessions).
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to
• make qualitative and quantitative predictions about the results of chemical reactions.
• predict chemical and physical properties of matter based on models of molecular structure.
• think both critically (analytically) and creatively about a variety of approaches to problem-
solving.
• view problems as a maze to which there may be more than one equally accurate, if not
efficient, solution.
• decide between possible approaches to a problem and choose the most efficient.
• let go of their dependence on paradigms to solve problems and replace paradigmatic
memorization with true analytical thinking.
• explain succinctly what “chemistry” is to a person who has never studied the subject and
argue convincingly why chemistry matters to everyone.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Textbook: Tro, Nivaldo J. Chemistry, A Molecular Approach. Pearson Prentice Hall, 4th ed., 2017.
For your convenience, the relevant chapters from the textbook, as well as the appendices, data tables, glossary, and
index, are posted as PDFs on the Moodle course page.
Calculators: You must have a scientific calculator for assessments. It does not need to be a
graphing calculator, but it must have log functions. Cell phone calculators will not be permitted
under any circumstances.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
ATTENDANCE at all lectures and at least one recitation section a week is a mandatory part of
CHEM 11A.
Lectures: CHEM 11A is an in-person class and lectures begin at 10:10 AM. I know that for
some of you this seems impossibly early, but your physical presence is important for you and
for me.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Since attentive listening is crucial to understanding the lectures, laptops and other electronic
Internet devices ARE NOT PERMITTED. Electronic devices are a distraction to your fellow
students as well as to you (whether you think so or not). Distractions interfere with an effective
learning environment.

spans.label: ACCOM

spans.label: ATTEND

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: I expect students to participate in at least one recitation session per\nweek, but you are welcome to attend as many additional sessions as you find useful.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Lecture recordings: A lot of information gets packed into every lecture in CHEM 11!\nRecordings of the lectures are made available for review purposes only. They are NOT an\nacceptable substitute for physical attendance! Lectures in CHEM 11A will not be “live-streamed.”
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Supplemental Instruction: Supplemental Instruction (SI) is a wonderful opportunity for
you to process the course material in small groups that maximize your active participation.
Sessions are conducted by SI Leaders—students who have completed CHEM 11 successfully
and who know what it takes to get the grades that you want. By engaging actively in these short
(one-hour) collaborative learning sessions, you will be able to digest as much material as it
would take you two and a half hours to study on your own! SI sessions are scheduled in the
evenings and on weekends to minimize conflicts with other course obligations. It is our goal to
make these sessions as convenient for you to attend as possible. While attendance at SI sessions is
entirely voluntary, results from past semesters indicate that, on average, students who attend SI sessions earn
higher test scores and semester grades than those who do not.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
PROBLEM SETS: Problems for each chapter are posted on Moodle. I have assigned a large
number of problems of graduated difficulty to help you learn the material. Answers to the assigned
problems are posted on Moodle. The problem sets will not be collected or graded, but the pretest
and test problems on which you will be graded are based on the assigned cumulative problems
from the textbook. remember to request the problem for a recitation session so that I can help you
understand and conquer it!
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
READINGS: I expect you to read every chapter thoroughly, and to work through the sample
problems in the text as you go along. Plan to read each chapter before the lectures on the chapter
begin. The only way to keep up is to stay ahead! For your convenience, the relevant chapters
from the textbook, as well as the appendices, data tables, glossary, and index, are posted as PDFs
on the Moodle course page.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
PRETESTS AND PEER EVALUATIONS: There are 5 group-work sessions in CHEM 11A, each
scheduled to occur one week before the test on the same material. During these sessions, your
group will complete and submit a “pretest.” The pretest will include questions and problems from
each of the two chapters that will be covered on the full test the following week. Some form of
the pretest questions WILL be on the actual test, so it will be well worth your time to
prepare for these group-work sessions thoroughly!
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: The pretests are collectively worth 10% of your\nsemester grade. I will drop your lowest pretest score before calculating your final average.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: The group-work sessions begin promptly at 9:35 AM. Arrival to a session later than\n9:40 will result in an automatic zero on the pretest. There are no exceptions to this\npolicy.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
After the initial 40 minutes, you will work with your assigned group of 4-5 students for a 30-minute
collaboration period. During that time, you will discuss solutions with your group, share your
collective knowledge, and revise your answers. No textbooks, notes, or Internet sources are
allowed.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: *Following the inclusive practice of “Universal Design in Education (UDE),” the 40 minutes allowed to all students\ntakes into consideration that some students have extended time accommodations (up to double time) on assessments.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Peer Evaluations: For each pretest, you will select one paper to submit for grading to represent
the collective work of your group. Your group is free to choose the submitter and vary who submits
for the group from week to week. For any working group, however, there is nothing more
frustrating than having members who do not contribute at all and still get full credit for the efforts
of their peers. To encourage you to prepare for the pretests and make a strong contribution to
your group’s effort, every student will submit an end-of-semester evaluation, on Moodle, of all
group members. The average score you receive from your peers will count for 5% of your overall
course grade for the semester. Completing the evaluations of yourself and your peers will count
for an additional 5% of your semester grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: TESTS AND FINAL EXAM (“ASSESSMENTS”): In CHEM 11A there are five 75-minute tests and a\nthree-hour final exam
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: , collectively worth 80% of your semester grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
YOUR GRADE WILL BE CALCULATED USING THE BEST SCORES ON FIVE OF THE
SIX ASSESSMENTS! I will automatically drop the lowest score on one assessment, even if that
lowest score is on the final exam! If you have not missed any of the regular chapter tests
during the semester and are satisfied with your grade, you do not need to take the
final exam in CHEM 11A!!!
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
MISSED TESTS AND PRETESTS: THERE ARE ABSOLUTELY NO MAKE-UPS FOR PRETESTS
OR TESTS, NO EXCEPTIONS. Under certain restricted circumstances (see below) an excused
absence from a test may be granted. Travel plans, family obligations, etc. are not valid excuses
and will result in a zero on the missed test. Excused absences will normally not be granted for missed group-
work sessions. I drop the lowest pretest score to allow for the possibility that a student might be sick or absent on one
of the Fridays that we have scheduled group work.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Circumstances qualifying for an excused test absence:
• an incapacitating illness or injury in which the student is hospitalized, under medical care, or sufficiently
debilitated as to be unable to perform basic academic tasks. [Colds, headaches, or other such mild complaints
that result in your feeling less than 100% are not considered “incapacitating.” It is your responsibility to get
enough rest and take care of yourself in such a way as to enable you to meet all course requirements. To qualify
for an excused absence, an illness must be severe or contagious enough to warrant absence from classes and
restriction from all other social and group events.]
• extraordinary circumstances over which the student has no control such as a death in the family.
• participation in a varsity game or mandatory travel to a varsity game (not practices or club sports).
Requests for an excused absence must be made in writing before the scheduled test (if possible),
and in no event later than 24 hours after the test has been administered. All requests must
be accompanied by documentation supporting the reason for the absence. After the
24-hour deadline, a missed test will be considered UNEXCUSED and will earn a score of zero.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
A zero
score from one missed assessment will be dropped before the final grade is calculated. If you sleep through one 75-
minute test during the semester, for example, that zero (presumably your lowest score!) would be dropped, but you
would have to take the entire final exam as one of the five tests from which your semester grade is calculated.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
In the case of an EXCUSED absence from a 75-minute test, the corresponding part
of the final exam will serve as a make-up. The score that you earn on the
corresponding part of the final exam (see below) will substitute for the score on the
missed test.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
ACCOMMODATIONS: Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all
students, and, as your instructor, I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability
accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-
736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find
helpful student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might
be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can so that I
can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate test accommodations,
ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before the test.
Students sometimes receive approval for academic accommodations later in the semester, which
is fine. Note, however, that no accommodations can be made retroactively.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
OPTIONAL EXTRA CREDIT AND REDEMPTION POINTS (GRADE BOOSTERS!)
______________________________________________________________________________
Optional “Redemption Point Challenge” Assignments: No student’s semester grade
should ride on the outcome of a single test. Stress and bad days can affect test performance
regardless of how carefully and thoroughly a student has prepared. The “Redemption Point
Challenge” is an optional extra-credit assignment that will be posted after each of the five tests. It
is an opportunity to delve into the material from a previous test and earn up to 10 points.*
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Earned\npoints will be added to your test score and the new score will replace your original score. In most\ncases this will allow you to boost your performance by one-half to a one full letter grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
*Redemption points are not “automatic.” The assignments are graded carefully; points are awarded only for complete
work that reflects thorough learning. Students whose original test score is 82% or higher can earn up to half the
points lost. For example, if you get a 92 on the test, you can earn up to ½ of the 8 lost points, for a final score of
96.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Redemption Points Earned on the Cumulative Final Exam: You can earn more points
back by performing well on the final exam!
The cumulative final exam is divided into five parts, each part corresponding to one of the five 75-
minute tests. If your score on a test (after redemption points have been added) is less than 100%,
you have the chance to “redeem” up to half the remaining points by doing well on the
corresponding section of the final. If your percent score on a section of the final is better than your
percent score on the corresponding test, your test score will be replaced by the average of your
original score and your score on the corresponding section of the final. (There is no penalty if you
do worse on the final than on the original test.)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Graded components of the course are weighted as follows:
Tests/Final Exam (5 out of 6) 80%
Pretests (4 out of 5) 10%
Peer Evaluation Score 5%
Completion of Peer Evaluations 5%
Grading in CHEM 11 is non-competitive. Grades are based solely on individual student
achievement and are independent of class average. (That is, there is no such thing as a “scale” or
a “curve” in my class, so do not even ask!) The letter grade ranges you see in the table below are
larger targets to aim for than you may be used to from high school or other classes. You do not
need to be “perfect” to get a good letter grade on your transcript! I value deep learning and in
order to do that well, you need some room to make mistakes with harder material, rather than get
high scores by repeating simple tasks.
Grade Percentage of Grade Percentage of
Total Points Total Points
A 87 – 100 C 51 – 58
A- 82 – 86 C- 46 – 50
B+ 77 – 81 D+ 41 – 45
B 69 – 76 D 33 – 40
B- 64 – 68 D- 28 – 32
C+ 59 – 63 E < 28
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Academic honesty:
Academic integrity is central to the mission of educational excellence at Brandeis University. University policy on
academic honesty may be found in your Student Handbook under “Rights and Responsibilities.” While students are
Page 7

Syllabus, CHEM 11A Fall 2024
encouraged to study together and discuss subject matter inside and outside the classroom, tests and the final exam must
be your own individual effort. Cheating is unfair to your fellow students, to the instructor, and to yourself. You might
think that in a class of this size sharing information with a friend during an exam goes unnoticed. It doesn’t. Nearly
every year students are caught trying to cheat.* The consequences are embarrassing and time-consuming, and the
incident remains part of your permanent undergraduate record. Violations of University policies on academic integrity,
described in Section Three of “Rights and Responsibilities,” will be reported to the Brandeis Student Rights and
Community Standards Office. A first offense will result in a zero on the assignment. Subsequent offenses may result
in failure in the course, suspension, or expulsion from the university.
*Cheating includes copying or sharing information with a fellow student on a test or exam, or consulting any written
or electronic materials or online sources of information during groupwork sessions, tests, or the final exam.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Course format and continuity\nClasses will be conducted in-person, and in-person attendance is a part of the grade (see “Course\nRequirements” section below). In the event of campus closures, classes will meet on Zoom.

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
This course serves as an introduction to Psychology. We will cover many of the subfields of Psychology,
with a focus on scientific research into the human mind. You will be challenged to hone your critical
thinking skills through the course material, written assignments, and active class participation. What I
would most like for you to gain from this course is 1) an appreciation of the breadth of the field of
Psychology, and 2) the ability to critically evaluate findings from the behavioral sciences that you will
encounter in the media and everyday life.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Credit Hours:\nSuccess in this 4-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of nine\nhours per week in preparation for class (readings, assignments, preparation for the exams, etc.).

Teaching Assistant assignment and communication
Each student is assigned as indicated above to a graduate student Teaching Assistant (TA) according to the
first letter of their first name, as it is listed in Workday. Graduate student TA’s will hold regular weekly
Student Hours on Zoom at the indicated links. Graduate student TA’s are primarily responsible for grading
exams and assignments. Undergraduate student TA’s are primarily responsible for recording attendance.
Both graduate and undergraduate TA’s are available to discuss and/or clarify any course material or
course policies. Students may contact their assigned TA by email any time, and the TA will respond as
promptly as they can. Keep in mind that you have three options for contacting people with questions: 1)
Professor Howard, 2) your assigned graduate student TA, 3) your assigned undergraduate student TA.

will be 30 lecture days that count towards your attendance grade. You get 3 “free” unexcused absences
for the semester, meaning the final attendance grade will be out of 27 rather than 30. If you need to miss
more than 3 lectures, please notify your assigned undergraduate TA with a documented excuse.

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I want
to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations, you will need to
work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS) (781-736-3470, access@brandeis.edu). You can find helpful
student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you
might be eligible for support from SAS. If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please
provide me with a copy as soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of
accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you should provide
the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Important Policies and Resources
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which may
include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures
related to academic integrity. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they
apply to a specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or
assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to
the Department of Student Rights and Community Standards.
Exams for this course are open book, but you may not work collaboratively with other people to complete
exams. Any work submitted for academic credit must by your own, and must be unique for this course. Do
not submit work that presents the ideas of others as one’s own, recycles your own work across courses,
fails to properly cite sources, and/or lifts sentences or ideas from the works of others (including
textbooks, websites, or other students). If you wish to bring in an idea or a quote from an outside source
and are uncertain how to cite them, please ask the instructor or teaching assistants how to reference it.
Additional citation and research assistance can be found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Classroom Health and Safety
• Register for the Brandeis Emergency Notification System. Students who receive an emergency
notification while attending class should notify their instructor immediately. In the case of a life-
threatening emergency, call 911. As a precaution, review this active shooter information sheet.
• Brandeis provides this shuttle service for traveling across campus or to downtown Waltham,
Cambridge and Boston.
• On the Brandeis campus, all students, faculty, staff and guests are required to observe the university's
policies on physical distancing and mask-wearing to support the health and safety of all classroom
participants. Review up to date COVID-related health and safety policies regularly.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Personal reactions to topics
Material covered in Psychology courses such as this one can bring up personal feelings and reactions that
you may not know how to handle. I will rarely give trigger warnings, as you should be prepared to deal
with sensitive topics in the field of Psychology. If needed, please make use of the counseling services
available on campus at the Brandeis Counseling Center. For anonymous peer counseling, call the Brandeis
Counseling & Rape Crisis hotline: 6-TALK.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Course Materials/Books/Apps/Equipment
If you are having difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your Student
Financial Services or Academic Services advisor to discuss possible funding options, including vouchers for
purchases made at the Brandeis Bookstore.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Moodle\nMoodle is the Brandeis learning management system. Login using your UNET ID and password. For LATTE\nhelp, contact Library@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Library
The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis students, faculty
and staff. Librarians and Specialists from Research & Instructional Services, Public Services, Archives & Special
Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios, MakerLab, AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab are
available to help you through consultations and workshops.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Brandeis Undergraduate Group Study (BUGS)
BUGS is a program in the Office of Academic Services open to all Brandeis undergraduates which offers
tutoring at no cost to students. Group Study Leaders are undergraduates who have successfully
completed the relevant course(s), are recommended by faculty, and have been trained by Academic
Services. These peer tutors lead sessions to review course material, discuss study strategies, and help
prepare for assessments.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Privacy
To protect your privacy in any case where this course involves online student work outside of Brandeis
password-protected spaces, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias. You must share the pseudonym/
alias with me and any teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may
submit such work directly to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Student Support
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. If a student, faculty, or
staff member wants to learn more about support resources, the Support at Brandeis webpage offers a
comprehensive list that includes these staff colleagues you can consult, along with other support
resources:
• The Care Team
• Academic Services (undergraduate)
• Graduate Student Affairs
• Directors of Graduate Studies in each department, School of Arts & Sciences
• Program Administrators for the Heller School and International Business School
• University Ombuds
• Office of Equal Opportunity.

Tue & Fri | 9:35AM – 10:55AM
Room: Lemberg Academic Center 054
Aldo Musacchio
(Pronouns: He, him, his)
Professor of International Business
Teaching assistant:
Yllka Sela (yllkasela@brandeis.edu)
Contact information
Office: Lemberg 252
Email: <aldom@brandeis.edu> (best way to contact me)
Office hours: By appointment (email me to set time)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Purpose of the Course
This course teaches students the basic principles of competitive and corporate strategy,
focusing mostly on business models and competitive dynamics in technology companies. The
course also dives into the basics of innovation, competing with platforms, and competition
among firms that rely on social strategy. Additional topics covered include corporate
diversification and M&As in technology companies.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: *The course has a quant component. Students will be required to turn in assignments intended\nto teach them advanced excel skills and data visualization with Tableau.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Who should be taking this course?
· students interested in strategy, consulting, entrepreneurship, and global
management
· students interested in the challenges startup entrepreneurs face when launching
new products or services
· students interested in a career in sales, data, business analysis, investment
banking, etc…
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals and Outcomes
1. You will learn the basics of strategic management; you will understand how
strategists and CEOs tackle the main challenges they face when selling a new product or
service, when they compete with other firms, and when they acquire new firms,
2. You will develop oral communication and presentation skills,
3. You will learn to think critically about business models,
4. You will learn the basics of two-sided platforms (e.g., such as Uber) and you will
acquire tools to help you develop ideas using such platforms,
5. You will develop advanced MS Excel skills and basic data visualization using
Tableau,
6. Because the course has a strong focus on team work and decision-making with
limited information, you will learn to:
a. Use limited information to crack business cases (very useful for job
interviews)
b. Recognize personality traits that may handicap you in your future career
and identify your own biases, stemming from both your background and your
culture;
c. Write short business memos about a variety of topics using simple
frameworks that strategists worldwide use;
d. Work with a team under pressure; and,
e. Develop skills to prepare power points and presentations for investors or for
boards of directors
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: strategy
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisite: BUS 10a & BUS 152a (recommended)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Expected workload
Students should expect to invest 9 hours/week outside of class on the course.
Questions to prepare for each session will be posted on Latte. There are also a variety of
assignments that require work throughout the semester.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Materials for the Course
The course relies mostly on Harvard Business School case studies, which are available for
purchase at TBD. Course packs are comprised of copyrighted materials, so please respect
intellectual property by purchasing your own copy of the materials (Through the Harvard page
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: we keep track of who buys the materials). The cases change every year, so make sure to get the\nmost up to date case packet.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
For those of you who prefer to have a way to study the material all in one place, I strongly
recommend the following textbook. We won’t use it directly in class because case studies are
more fun, but MOST of the material we discuss in the first part of the course (the basics of
strategy) is there:
Completely optional textbook if you want to see a different style of putting the pieces together:
Hill, C. W., Jones, G. R., & Schilling, M. A. Strategic management: Theory & cases: An integrated
approach. Cengage Learning. [Look for latest edition] [We won’t use it or refer to it in class]
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
COMPONENTS OF THE GRADE
Here is a breakdown of how we are going to grade all the different assignments of the course.
Participation 30%
2 Excel assignments + Tableau homework 15% (5% each)
Midterm exam (written, in class) 20%
Written assignments 20%
Team presentations (5% 1st round, 10% the 2nd) 15%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
GRADING GUIDE
Grades in Bus 160a are VERY different from what you may have experienced in other courses at
Brandeis University. All of the work is graded relative to your peers. That is, it is all based on
rankings of performance for each of the assignments. Why is this the case? Well, because that is
how companies operate. In the real world only a few people get A’s. Nowadays, people are
competing fiercely in the workplace to be the best and there are lists with rankings for
promotions. Your supervisors will evaluate you in relation to your peers. So, take this course as
your first contact with the harsh reality of the workplace.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
How I calculate the final Grade:
At the end, I will add up everything so that the maximum grade you can get is 100. I will then
use these scores to rank students and to do a distribution of letter grades following a forced-
curve (e.g., something like this 2 A, 6 A-, 8 B+, 8B, 4 B-, 2 C+, 1 D, etc…). The mean should be
centered around B, but the distribution can get stretched out to the right if people don’t turn in
their assignments or miss too many sessions. In those cases, the letter grades can go all the way
to the Ds and Fs. So please do not assume you won’t get a bad grade. Not turning in a written
assignment or not participating in the team project gives you an automatic D and missing
multiple assignments can set you back with an F.
Class Participation (30%)
Class participation will add 30 points in total to your final score. According to your ranking you
will be assigned into 4 buckets:
Rank
Points towards your final score
Top 10% 30 points
Top 20-40% 25 points
Top 40-66% 20 points
Top Bottom third 15 points
Those who misuse technology, >2 unexcused absences, etc. 0 points
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: The ranking is based on two scores, one score keeps track of student participation in every\nsession and assigns a grade. The second score is based on quality of the comments. That is, the
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
participation score is extremely high for people who provide insights that help everyone see the
main points of a discussion or who are actively participating to illustrate a point or do a complex
calculation, those people who systematically have high quality comments have a higher overall
score and a better ranking.
I will share the ranking of students according to participation halfway through the course.
Please don’t get too comfy with the ranking then because the rankings are dynamic and change
every day until the last day of class.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Midterm exam (20%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
The midterm exam is IN CLASS. It will be based on the cases read until that day and on things
discussed in class. On the day of the exam there will be a set of questions you will need to
answer in blue books by hand, no laptops or electronics allowed. The questions will be fair and
short enough to complete in 80 minutes.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: You cannot bring printed notes, cases or reading\nsummaries, or any other auxiliary notes. Phones, computers, and all your bags will be in the\nfront of the room.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: You won’t need to memorize anything; the questions are made for you to think and apply what\nyou have learned so far. There will be a prep session with the TA before the exam in which\nstudents will go over a similar case analysis.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
The midterm exam is to give you an idea of how you are doing in class. If you get a terrible grade
in the midterm, it is unlikely that you will be able to handle the individual writeup and final
presentation, so a discussion with the professor is recommended at this point to see if it is
necessary to drop the course.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: All assignments are graded on a check mark/half check mark/ no check mark basis.\nNot turning in an assignment gives you zero points, but also adds a red flag—I use this to decide\nwhere to put people in the subjective rankings as well, whether someone deserves a D, etc.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Final Team Presentations (15%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
There are two presentations of the final project. Teams of 2-3 should send a proposal for the
final project as soon as we cover the innovation section of the course. The professor has to
approve your topic before you develop it. The deliverable is an amazing presentation in teams
and a deck of slides. Please try to use CANVA, it’s so much better than Google Slides or
PowerPoint.
TEAMS of 2-3 people MAX
Deliverable: an amazing Canva presentation. I strongly encourage you to learn to use CANVA,
it’s so much better than Google Slides or PowerPoint. Using ChatGPT’s Canva tool is also
strongly recommended.
ASSIGNMENT:
1. Business model analysis using whatever is necessary to understand the competitive
advantage of your firm in 1 slide!!! (Five Forces, Competitor Analysis, Cost Analysis, Resources,
Better off/Ownership test, Value Chain, SIT innovation, two-sided platforms, social strategies,
etc.)
2. What are the key advantages and disadvantages of the business model? What needs to happen
for this firm to succeed financially?
3. Are they successful financially? If not, why not. If yes, please explain how. (Things you can
do, analyze the trends in sales, number of stores/products/accounts sold, variable costs are
low/high, fixed costs are low/high, hard to break even… etc…). Unprofitable firms will be more
fun to analyze.
3. You will need to tell us how AI is going to transform this business. Do this in a very structured
way. How is AI going to help the company sell more (top line), increase willingness to pay
(marketing), or how it is going to help them to reduce costs or customer acquisition costs and
thus improve the bottom line (net profits). That is, explain how AI will change the value stick.
4. What are the risks of the business model? Will AI solve the issues you outline? What can go
wrong?
All of this in five minutes. What? Yes, five minutes… You are probably thinking “impossible.”
No, it’s doable and everyone in class will appreciate it. It will help you guys get rid of all the fluff
and focus on what matters. It’s the best critical thinking exercise.
Two rounds of presentations. First, present the rough idea, listen to questions and improve it
for the second round of presentations on the last day of class.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Finally, don’t just come and present the optimistic side of the product (don’t just drink the kool\naid). Show all angles, tell us about risks, possible problems, etc. Display your analyst skills.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Please be original. At this point you know more strategy than 95% of the world, so your analysis
is better than almost anything out there. Feel free to use generative AI to help you, but make
sure you are using it to help you do the analysis YOU want, instead of doing whatever it wants (I
5

can tell when someone is doing the analysis using our frameworks and when ChatGPT is doing
the analysis).

Student Athletes
If you are a student athlete, please send me an email and let me know if there are any
ongoing tournaments/games/events that may interfere with your class engagement. I will try to
accommodate as much as I can. If there is any event that may interfere with team presentations,
you need to tell me early in the semester to rearrange the schedule. Last minute events will not
be accommodated.

spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Academic Integrity
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis
University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity.
Students may be required to submit work to TurnItIn.com software to verify originality (If
turnitin is showing a red flag it means you have plagiarized more than 10-20% of the essay,
please fix it before resubmitting). Remember to use quotation marks when citing other people’s
work and to avoid red flags on turnitin. Violations of academic integrity give students the
lowest possible grade (remember people are ranked for all assignments) and can also be
forwarded to the Director of Academic Integrity. Sanctions for academic dishonesty can include
failing grades and/or suspension from the university. Citation and research assistance can be
found at LTS - Library guides.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Office hours\nOffice hours are scheduled on demand and according to student and faculty availability. Please\nemail me to sign up for a 20-30 minute time slot.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Teaching assistant
The teaching assistant is someone who was a very good student in the previous iteration of the
course. It doesn’t mean that he/she/they know everything. It is also not the TA job to teach you
things you should know from other courses, such as accounting and the basics of financial
analysis. The TA can clarify key concepts and frameworks and share tips in Excel and Tableau.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Please DO NOT bother them with questions about the Excel and Tableau assignment the day
before the deadline. Do not complain about grades or the TA’s work directly to them, it’s not
professional. With any issues write to me directly and make sure it is a valid complaint,
otherwise it will reflect poorly on you.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: LEARNING BY THE CASE METHOD\nBecause this course is based on the case method of learning, class participation by all\nstudents is critical.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: • Presence: You are expected to prepare for and attend all class sessions. It is your\nresponsibility to catch up on material for any class that you miss.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
• Preparation: You are expected to do the class assignments and be ready to start class or
answer assigned questions if called on. In addition, it means that you have analyzed the case
and exhibits, not just read them lightly. Where there is numerical analysis to be done to
understand the financials or economics of the case, you will be expected to do this. It is often
extremely useful to work in groups in preparing the cases for each class; you are encouraged
to do this.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: (THERE ARE COLD CALLS in EVERY SESSION)
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
• Participation: You are expected to share your views and questions in class. Your class
participation grade will depend on the cumulative quality of your contributions in class.
There is no makeup work if you miss class and no special discussion of the case during office
hours. If you have an unexcused absence, you are responsible to catch up.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: We may have to switch to zoom for health reasons, if we have a remote guest, or in case we have\nan issue doing the class in person.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
In case we need to switch to Zoom, here is the etiquette I
expect from everyone:
• Don’t be late. I always play music 10 minutes before class starts. If you come in and the
music is over, you are late!
• Cameras should be on, with mics muted, at all times. Unmute only when you’re called on
to speak. Keep the camera on, it’s disrespectful to keep the camera off. If you have an
issue with this just reach out and explain.
• Raise your hand if you want to participate. Yet the professor will manage the queue, so
raising your hand early may not give you first dibs. Also, do NOT leave your hand up ALL
the time. That usually signals that you’re not trying to speak to connect with the
conversation. I reserve the right to clear the hands up queue every time we change
topics.
• Try to find a quiet place with good light to join class. Try to have the light in front of you,
rather than behind you. If for any circumstance you cannot find a quiet place to join the
class please send me a note to explain the situation and we will find an appropriate
solution.
• If you have a poor internet connection or you need to be on your cell phone for any
reason, please send me a note with enough time. I may allow people to have camera’s off
under those circumstances. Yet, please do not connect to class if you’re doing other
things, moving backgrounds or being at the gym will be distracting to others. If you
cannot commit to attend class during the predetermined times please drop the course.
Thanks.
spans.label: CONDUCT

doc_id: 243ECON 83A 1 Statistics for Economic Analysis Nidhiya Menon
course_name: 243ECON 83A 1 Statistics for Economic Analysis Nidhiya Menon

spans.text: nmenon@brandeis.edu\nFall 2024 OH: Tuesday 11am-1pm in Lemberg 164\nECONOMICS 83A
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
OVERVIEW & LEARNING GOALS
This course provides an introduction to the statistical techniques that are used in analyzing quantitative problems
in economics. Topics covered include mean, variance, probability, sampling, estimation, hypothesis testing, and
basic regression analysis. We will also learn how to use R.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: The recitation for this class meets on Tuesdays 6:30-\n8:20pm in Schneider Heller: G002, usually every two weeks. This class is in person. University guidance states\n“in person classes will no longer be required to have an option for remote participation.”
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
PRE-REQUISITES
Econ 2a or Econ 10a. Calculus requirement: You must have either completed Math 10A with a grade of C- or
higher, obtained a 4 or higher on the AP Math AB test, obtained a 3 or higher on the AP Math BC test, or passed
the calculus placement exam given by the Economics department which is scheduled for Friday, September 6,
2024, 1-3pm in Sachar 101.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
REQUIREMENTS
Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of 9 hours
of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, problem sets, recitations, preparation for quizzes
and exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Instruction begins on the first day of class; students are responsible for learning material and
completing assignments from the beginning of the course. Course requirements include timely completion of
assigned readings, mandatory attendance at lectures, completion of problem sets, R exercises, quizzes, and exams.
The exams are cumulative and closed-book. Six problem sets will be assigned of which your highest five
grades will count towards your overall final grade for this course.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: You may discuss your problem sets with other\nstudents but answers must be written individually.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Since you have a week for each problem set, no late\nproblem sets will be accepted.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: There will be three quizzes and three R exercises that are open-book/notes.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Absence from an exam or quiz is excused only if the student has a serious illness or family emergency that\nhas been previously documented. There are no exceptions to this rule.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
The weighting scheme for course requirements is as shown below:
Exam I 20%
Exam II 20%
Exam III 20%
Three In-class quizzes 15%
R exercises 10%
Five Problem Sets 15%
No commitments can or will be made at any point in terms of “curving” final grades for this class.
Further, all course requirements are graded based on a pre-set grading rubric which is equally applied to
every student in class. Consequently, no requests for additional points on answers will be entertained.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
IMPORTANT DATES
Exams: Exam I – Tuesday, September 24
Exam II – Friday, November 1
Exam III – Friday, November 22
Quizzes: Quiz I – Tuesday, September 17
Quiz II – Friday, October 25
Quiz III – Friday, November 15
R Exercises: Friday December 6, Tuesday December 10 (Brandeis Friday)
Problem Sets: Problem Set 1 – Assigned on September 3, due on September 10
Problem Set 2 – Assigned on September 10, due on September 17
Problem Set 3 – Assigned on September 27, due on October 8
Problem Set 4 – Assigned on October 18, due on October 25
Problem Set 5 – Assigned on November 5, due on November 12
Problem Set 6 – Assigned on November 12, due on November 19
spans.label: SCHEDULE

spans.text: TEXTBOOK\nThe text is Wonnacott and Wonnacott, Introductory Statistics (5th edition). A copy will be placed on reserve.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: LAPTOP, TABLET AND CELL PHONE POLICY\nThe use of cell phones, tablets, and laptops in class is prohibited. If you must take a call, please leave the\nclassroom.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: Please speak to me if you are unable to take notes without the use of a laptop.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text: Recording of lectures\nis prohibited without prior arrangement with me.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
STUDENT EQUIPMENT/COURSE SUPPLY NEEDS AND AVAILABLE SUPPORT
In this course, the minimal set of hardware, software and course supplies needed to be successful are a laptop or
tablet with a browser to access the class Moodle page, and common processing programs such as Word and
Adobe.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: Undergraduate students with financial need should contact Student Financial Services to discuss options available\nto purchase equipment and other technology and supply needs. GSAS students should contact GSAS.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: COMMUNICATION\nThere is a Moodle page for this course. Please use email to contact the instructor.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
SPECIAL ACCOMMODATION
Brandeis seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs accommodations as outlined
in an accommodations letter, please talk to me and present your letter of accommodation as soon as you can. I
want to support you. In order to provide test accommodations, I need the letter more than 48 hours in advance. I
want to provide your accommodations, but cannot do so retroactively. If you have questions about documenting a
disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support (SAS) at 781.736.3470 or
access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text: HEALTH AND SAFETY\nAll students in this class are required to observe the university's policies to support the health and safety of all\nclassroom participants.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: INCLEMENT WEATHER POLICY\nThis class will observe the university’s inclement weather policy.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
ACADEMIC HONESTY
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit
work to software to verify originality. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the
Director of Academic Integrity. Sanctions for academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension
from the university. Citation and research assistance can be found at LTS - Library guides.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Statement on use of generative AI tools from the Center for Teaching and Learning at Brandeis:
“As a college student, it is your responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity.
Representing work generated by artificial intelligence as one’s own work is considered to be academically
dishonest.”
Please ensure that all work for which you want credit is your original work. Using any generative AI tool in this
class (like ChatGPT Gemini, AlphaCode etc.) constitutes plagiarism and academic dishonesty. If you have
any questions about my expectations, please ask.
Academic dishonesty will not be tolerated and will be vigorously prosecuted.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

OUTLINE OF LECTURES AND RECOMMENDED READINGS
Week 1 (August 30)
The Nature of Statistic - W&W, Chapter 1- Random sampling and randomized experiments.
Week 2 (September 3, 6):
Review Session: Tuesday, September 3, Recitation
Descriptive Statistics - W&W, Chapter 2 - Mean, variance, frequency tables and graphs.
Probability - W&W, Chapter 3 - Probability models, conditional probability, independence, and
Bayes theorem.
Week 3 (September 10, 13):
Problem Set 1 Due: Tuesday, September 10, in class
Probability Distributions - W&W, Chapter 4 - Discrete random variables, the Binomial distribution, the Normal
distribution.
Two Random Variables - W&W, Chapter 5 - Functions of two random variables, covariance, linear combination
of two random variables.
3

Week 4 (September 17, 20):
Problem Set 2 Due: Tuesday, September 17
Review Session: Tuesday, September 17, Recitation
Quiz 1: Tuesday, September 17
Sampling - W&W, Chapter 6 - Random sampling, shape of the sampling distribution.
Week 5 (September 24, 27):
Exam 1: Tuesday, September 24
Point Estimation - W&W, Chapter 7 – Populations and samples, efficiency of unbiased estimators, consistent
estimators.
Week 6 (October 1):
Review Session: Tuesday, October 1, Recitation
Confidence Intervals - W&W, Chapter 8 – A single mean, small sample t, difference in two means.
Week 7 (October 8):
Problem Set 3 Due: Tuesday, October 8
Confidence Intervals - W&W, Chapter 8 – A single mean, small sample t, difference in two means.
Week 8 (October 15, 18):
Review Session: Tuesday, October 15, Recitation
Hypothesis Testing - W&W, Chapter 9 – Hypothesis testing using confidence intervals, p-values.
Fitting a Line - W&W, Chapter 11 – Ordinary Least Squares.
Week 9 (October 25):
Quiz 2: Friday, October 25
Problem Set 4 Due: Friday, October 25
Week 10 (October 29, November 1):
Exam 2: Friday, November 1
Review Session: Tuesday, October 29, Recitation
Simple Regression - W&W, Chapter 12 – The regression model, confidence intervals, prediction.
4

Week 11 (November 5, 8):
Review Session: Tuesday, November 5, Recitation
Multiple Regression - W&W, Chapter 13 – The regression model and its OLS fit, confidence intervals and
statistical tests.
Regression Extensions, Correlation - W&W, Chapters 14 & 15 – Dummy variables, correlation and regression.
Week 12 (November 12, 15):
Quiz 3: Friday, November 15
Problem Set 5 Due: Tuesday, November 12
Analysis of Variance - W&W, Chapter 10 – One way ANOVA.
Week 13 (November 19, 22):
Exam 3: Friday, November 22

Introduction to R

spans.text:
Course Description
Composition Seminar (C-SEM) is a writing class that helps students develop college writing
practices and strategies in preparation for the First Year Seminar and other courses across the
Brandeis curriculum. Using a small-group setting and frequent feedback, the course allows
students to increase confidence, proficiency, and multi-modal literacy in a collaborative
environment that promotes mutual support and idea exchange. The course also aims to help
students come to better know Brandeis and to develop strong study habits.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals
Students will develop greater confidence:
● In their ability to perform well on writing assignments
● In using different Englishes in different contexts, including Academic English
● In their ability to write papers that express the ideas the student wishes to express
● In approaching college writing assignments
● In their ability to plan and complete longer writing assignments
● In using sources to support their points
● In understanding when and why to use direct quotes and when to summarize sources
Students will develop a greater understanding of:
● The role of rethinking and revision throughout the writing process
● The role that critical reading plays in the writing process
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: ● The role that audience and/or reader play in writing\n● The importance of writing in learning and education\n● The ways in which writing is important to their education and/or career
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Diversity, Equity and Inclusion
We live in a world and a country riddled with inequity and injustice, and these social conditions
affect us all in different ways, in and out of the classroom. My goal is to create a classroom
environment that accounts for differences and promotes inclusivity, considering categories such
as race, socio-economic status, gender, nationality, religion, immigration status, sexuality and
more. Together we will work to recognize our positionalities and differences and to ensure the
classroom is a space in which everyone can be themselves and express themselves. Doing so, we
can interrogate, understand and challenge structures of power, which are vital skills in
maintaining a democratic society.
If something happens in class, or elsewhere, that makes you feel excluded or uncomfortable,
please come talk to me.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Required Materials
All required texts/media will be available online on our Moodle course page.
You should come to class having read the texts assigned for the day. You should also have access
to the texts during class, either in a hard copy or an electronic one.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Writing Assignments
Campus Experience Essay. Students will make an argument about a space on campus and how
it aligns with (or doesn’t align with) the university’s mission statement and values.
Close Reading Media Essay. Students will employ the skills of close reading to analyze an
episode of television (either Black Mirror or Atlanta) and make a cohesive and evidence-based
argument about it.
Group Research Project. In groups, students will research a topic of their choice and present it
to a specific audience. In addition to researching the topic, this project allows students to practice
essential research skills and consider the importance of sources, audience and purpose.
*Each essay will have a detailed prompt, which we will discuss together*
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
**All assignments will be due on Moodle, unless otherwise specified**
The Writing Process
Writing is a skill that requires practice, repetition and revision; no one has ever written a
successful academic essay in one sitting, overnight. We will practice these skills by breaking up
the essays into stages:
● Pre-draft assignments. Preliminary brainstorming exercises to help you develop your
ideas and hone your writing skills. These assignments will form the foundation for your
drafts, in a process of reworking, modifying and developing.
● Drafts. A draft is a first attempt that allows you to test your ideas out and receive
feedback. The focus in a draft should be on your ideas and analysis, rather than polishing
your writing or any technical elements.
● Peer review. In groups, students will provide each other with feedback on their drafts.
Providing feedback is itself a valuable skill which we will practice and develop.
● Individual conferences. After writing your initial draft for each essay, you will meet
with me to discuss it and your revision process. These conferences are required.
● Revision. Considering feedback from your peers and from me, you will revise your essay
into a final version.
● Reflection. After revising, you will write a short reflection about your writing process.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Grading
Campus Experience Essay (3-4 pages) 15%.
Close Reading Media Essay (4-5 pages) 20%.
Group Research Project (equivalent to 7-8 pages) 30%.
Preparatory Assignments 10%.
Reflections 5%.
Regular attendance 10%.
Active Engagement 10%.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Attendance
You should plan to attend class regularly. You are allowed to miss up to 3 classes during the
semester without it impacting your grade. Any additional absences will begin to impact your
grade. If there are extenuating circumstances (prolonged illness; personal or family challenge),
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
please be in touch with me, with your academic adviser and any other relevant resources on
campus (SAS; Health Center; BCC). If you have an obligation that will require you to miss
classes (e.g., religious observances, athletics obligations), please talk with me at the beginning of
the semester. If you start missing class regularly, I will email you to check in, and I may also
contact your academic advisor so they are updated.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: 3 late arrivals will equal an absence.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: Engagement\nThis course is a seminar rather than a lecture, and so it relies on student engagement and in-class\nactivities.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
You are expected to be an active participant by preparing for class in advance, taking
notes during class, asking questions, and participating in class discussions, small group activities,
and in-class writing activities. We all have varying levels of experience and comfort with
classroom participation, and that’s okay. Finding ways of engaging in class that suit you is an
important skill for college and beyond, which we will work on together. If you have any
concerns about this, please come talk to me!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Communication
Make sure to use your Brandeis email account for all university communications, and get into the
habit of checking that inbox frequently. I will communicate with you as a class through Moodle’s
“Announcements” feature, which will also send you an email directly. You’re always welcome to
email me (yaelkenan@brandeis.edu), and I will respond as soon as I can on weekdays.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Office Hours
I hold weekly office hours on Mondays (1:00-2:00pm), Tuesdays (3:00-4:00) and Thursdays
(1:00-2:00pm), and by appointment. These times are dedicated to students and I encourage you
to come talk to me! We can discuss your assignments, class materials or anything else. If none of
my regular office hours work for your schedule, email me and we will find another time!
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Resources
Academic Advising. Your assigned academic advisor is an important resource for you here at
Brandeis. I strongly encourage you to reach out to your academic advisor regarding any question
or challenge - they can discuss matters like time management with you, point you towards
on-campus resources and much more. If you’re not sure who your advisor is, check on Workday,
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: and make sure you have their contact information. You can also contact other advisors if your\nassigned advisor is unavailable at any point. See more information here.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
HWL 58: Foundations for Academic Success is a discussion-based course for first-year
students that is designed to foster community and help you hone the skills needed to be
successful in college and beyond. You will learn about effective college-level study habits and
note-taking skills, time management strategies, communication and self-advocacy, and how to
combat procrastination. More details here.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
English Language Programs: ELP provides all students with additional support in written and
oral communication skills, whether English is your first language or not. As your CSEM
instructor, I will give you feedback on your writing, focusing on argumentation, evidence,
research and structure. I will not be able to instruct you on issues of grammar, vocabulary or
sentence level issues - but ELP can help with that! As part of CSEM, our class will have a
course-specific ELP consultant who will be familiar with our assignments and syllabus - more
details soon! Sign up for tutoring here.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Writing Center. I strongly encourage you to make use of the Brandeis University Writing
Center, which offers free writing support to all Brandeis community members in the form of
consultations (in-person and over Zoom), e-tutoring document-drop appointments as well as
workshops. The Writing Center’s team of expert consultants is on hand to help you with your
writing project at any stage, from brainstorming, to outlining, to revision.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Physical and Mental Health. If you’re feeling unwell - stay home and rest, for your own sake
and everyone else’s. The Student Health Center is available whenever you need it. There is
likewise information specific to international students and health insurance in the U.S., here.
College can be stressful. Your academic success is important, but your well-being should always
come first. Please take care of yourself and those around you throughout the semester. If you or
someone you know is feeling overwhelmed, stressed or anxious, know that there are resources
available on campus to help you, such as the Brandeis Counseling Center. You can find more
information on a variety of campus resources on Moodle (“Free Resources”). If you’re not sure
where to start, feel free to come talk to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Disability and Access Accommodations. Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that
is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I want to support you in your learning. If you
think you may require disability accommodations, you will need to work with Student
Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at
access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. If you already have
an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can so that I
can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Policies
Academic Integrity. You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. The University
policy on academic honesty is distributed annually in section 5 of the Rights and Responsibilities
handbook. Instances of alleged dishonesty will be forwarded to the Department of Student Rights
and Community Standards. Potential sanctions include failure on the essay, failure in the course,
and suspension from the University. If you have questions about my expectations, please ask.
Use of AI Tools. In this class, we will use writing as a way to sharpen both our critical thinking
and analysis skills as well as to develop skills that allow us to clearly communicate our ideas.
While it is appropriate to use artificial intelligence (AI) for tasks like checking spelling or
grammar and for brainstorming ideas, you may not use AI to generate full sentences, paragraphs,
or papers. For example, you may not use AI to generate pre-drafts and drafts/revisions,
reflections, in-class exercises, peer letters, or communications with your instructor. This is
because claiming the work of others as your own, whether created by another human or by AI, is
regarded as plagiarism and therefore will be subject to the penalties outlined above in the
“Academic Integrity” section of the syllabus. If you have any questions about appropriate or
inappropriate use of AI, please don’t hesitate to ask.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Classroom Technology Policy. You are welcome to bring a laptop or other device to class to\naccess our materials and take notes. However, you may not use your phone for this purpose.\nPlease refrain from using electronic devices for non-academic purposes during class time.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: If your\nuse of technology poses a persistent problem in class, I reserve the right to mark you absent for\neach day you misuse an electronic device.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: If you think you may be distracted by using a laptop,\nplease bring a physical notebook to class.
spans.label: CONDUCT

course_name: 251ENG 20B 1 Literary Games Dorothy Kim

spans.text:
dorothykim@brandeis.edu
T/R 3:55-5:15 and Lab F
9:05-9:55, Spring 2025
Shiffman 120 English 20B: Literary Games
Office: 135 Rabb
Office Hours: R 2-3pm and
by appointment
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Literary Games
This class will address a long durée history of the games through the lens of
transmedia. Kishonna L. Gray explains: “I use the term transmedia to engage
what Göran Bollin calls ‘textual production that travels over technologies.’ The
transmedia text involves intricate multiplatform narrative webs that, according to
Henry Jenkins, capitalize on the affordances of digital media convergence. The
transmediated text requires cultural synergy of a multitude of mediated formats”
(Intersectional Tech, 2). Though Jenkins is specifically considering contemporary
digital media, I would like the class to expand the definition of “digital media” to
examine media created and played by one’s hands. This then is the start pointing
to examine how transmedia theory may help unpack issues in what I call “literary
games” from the medieval chess board, dice game, to digital multi-player video
games now. Within a discussion of transmedia we will address the various
theories about narrative and play that have animated discussions about games
from the Middle Ages to contemporary media. This class will also center race,
gender, sexuality, disability, class in thinking through the issues of transmedia and
the gaming cultures that have most recently been in the political mainstream news
in relation to far-right politics.
This class will ask you to read and play some of the following: Alfonso X’s Libro
de Los Juegos (Book of Games); The Game and Playe of the Chesse; James I,
Kingis Quair; The Compleat Gamester; a slate of Children’s board games from
the 19th centuries; 20th-century board games (Monopoly, Life, Settlers of Catan,
etc.); video games (both MMRPG and indie games). The class requires that you
play in groups regularly; give regular oral presentations to the class; write,
analyze, and create critical digital projects in relation to the games you are
playing.
This class requires a lab unit. It means you will be required to play, create
digital projects, and materials in this lab.
spans.label: DESCRIP
DESCRIP

spans.text: I. Response Portfolio: 20%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Throughout the semester you will be required to write 1-page piece that addresses the
reading and game play in the class. These will be used to generate class discussion. There should
be 10 for the semester. You can choose when to write them as what most interests you and fits
your schedule. They will have a portfolio grade for the semester. You should use these pieces as
a way to work on early thinking, analysis, or questions you may have as you do the reading and
as you work through different critical playing and making projects. They can help you with your
larger group projects. Some of these protocols will be required (for example, your group charter
which you will turn in as a group, or your social justice game prototype on Twine). This will be
indicated on the syllabus.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
I strongly suggest you do one a week, though it’s up to you when.
However, there will be some that are due with specific dates (i.e. the ones that are required, like
your group charter or your Twine prototype). Otherwise, we just need a portfolio of 8 other items
by the end of the semester (May 12).
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: II. 4 Group Projects: 60%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
There are 4 units in this class: 1. early literary games (dice, chess, etc.); 2. card games (17th-19th
century); 3. board games (19th-20th); 4. digital games (MMRPG and indie). You will be broken
up into groups of 4 or 6 to play games throughout the semester. You will be asked to create small
group projects and group oral presentations 4 times throughout the semester. For example, in
card games, you will choose which card game (whist, loo, etc.) to do a small presentation in
which you analyze your game using the critical vocabulary and approaches we will discuss in
class in relation to race, class, gender, disability, sexuality and in regards to theories of media,
performance, play, and narrative. You will present in-class, 10-15 minute presentations per group
for each project. You will turn in your project write-up along with your project slides as your
group project. You will upload a chapter for each group project on Scalar.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Each project and\npresentation will count for 15% (10% project/ 5% presentation) of your final grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Your\npresentations will be scheduled with the instructor for a specific class period.
spans.label: SCHEDULE

spans.text: III. Lab Work and Participation: 20%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: This class cannot work without dedicated participation. Since this also includes a lab portion that\nwill allow you to play as a group and also work on your game projects, it is absolutely necessary\nfor students to participate regularly and in cooperation with their group.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Attendance. We are in the middle of pandemics, with the attendant medical, familial,
personal, and other short-term and long-term issues related to this multi-year global trauma. I
am going to take attendance but you have 5 free absences no question asked. I asked that you
do your best to participate either through your thoughts in responses or in participating in
class. But please email me if you expect large periods away from class so that I can help you
catch up.
spans.label: ATTEND
