spans.text:
Policy on Childcare [My thanks to New Wave Feminists for this language]:
The policy described here is a reflection of my own beliefs and commitments to an intersectional
feminist classroom.
1) Children are welcome in this class as often as is necessary (breastfeeding, bottlefeeding,
etc.);
2) For older children and babies, I understand that unforeseen disruptions in childcare often
put parents in the position of having to miss class to stay home with a child. While this is
not meant to be a long-term childcare solution, bringing a child to class to cover gaps in
case is perfectly acceptable. Likewise, considering the lack of infrastructure of family
support for undergrads and graduate students at the Brandeis campus (I believe there is
no family student housing or subsidized/coop-based etc. childcare for students), these
issues should not become burdens for the student in pursuing their degree and education.
I feel like then this class is a village and as a village and a community of care, we will be
a space that supports students with families;
3) I ask that all students work with me to create a welcoming environment that is respectful
of all forms of inclusivity, including in parenting status;
4) In all cases where babies and children come to class, I ask that you sit close to the door so
that if your little one needs special attention, you may step outside quickly in order to
address any issues, feel free to do so without asking me for permission (the class can
keep you posted about what you may have missed);
5) Finally, I understand that often the largest barrier to completing your coursework once
you become a parent is the tiredness many parents feel in the evening once children have
finally gone to sleep. While I maintain the same high expectations for all students in my
classes regardless of parenting status, I am happy to problem-solve with you in a way that
makes you feel supported as you strive for school-parenting balance.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
TITLE IX and Mandatory Reporting (My Thanks to Nicole Bedera whose template I am
using for this statement)
Mandatory Reporting. All faculty and staff are now mandatory reporters. This means that if
we learn about something that might be sex discrimination (e.g., sexual harassment, sexual
assault, intimate partner violence, stalking, pregnancy discrimination, LGBTQ discrimination),
then we must report what we know to the Title IX Office. Usually, reports will only leave a
record of concern, but sometimes, a report can be escalated into an investigation without a
victim’s consent. Mandatory reporters are expected to share information learned in all settings,
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: including during class activities, assignments, office hours, and outside of class. Please be\nthoughtful about what you disclose to me.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
How to Seek Help. Sex discrimination can impact your education. I recognize that you may
need to seek help from me, even if you do not want to report. If you need assistance in class or
help navigating available resources, you can always ask me. You do not need to tell me why
you are asking for help for a friend, another student, or yourself. I will respect your privacy and
I will help you.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Academic Integrity. Plagiarism is the use of someone else’s ideas or words in your
writing without acknowledging the source. It is a serious offense and may result in failing
the class and suspension from the university. When in doubt, see me or consult the
student resources listed by Brandeis Library & Technology Services
(https://lts.brandeis.edu/courses/instruction/academic-integrity/index.html), including this
handout on “How to avoid plagiarism”
(http://writing.wisc.edu/Handbook/QPA_plagiarism.html).

spans.text:
This class will be free of hate speech regarding sexual orientation, gender expression, race,
disability, religion, and socio-economic status or background. Inflammatory remarks will not go
unchecked and will not be tolerated. Each member of this class is responsible for fostering an
environment in which people and their ideas are respected. For the same reasons, students will
strive to make remarks that are informed by our material and the history that surrounds it.
The N-word won’t be used in this class by a person of any race, even if it consistently appears in
our texts. The same goes for the “F” word, regardless of a person’s (perceived) sexual orientation
or gender expression. And, this is simply not a space in which we call people “trash.”
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
The majority of our thinking about the literature will be done outside of class. 3 hours a week is
not enough time to appreciate the richness of this material. Remaining enrolled in this course
means that you are ready to devote the time, effort, and energy to reading and thinking about the
material that it deserves.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Content Warnings: In this class, some of the readings will address topics of sexual assault,
racism, violent genocide, homophobia and transphobia, toxic masculine cultures, ableism, etc.
Feel free to contact me if you need to be absent during particularly difficult discussions. This
will not be counted against you in your participation grade.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Privacy
This class requires the use of tools that may disclose your coursework and identity to parties
outside the class. To protect your privacy, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias rather than
your name in submitting such work. You must share the pseudonym/alias with me and any
teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may submit such work
directly to me.

Success in this six-credit course (course plus lab) is based on the
expectation that students will spend a minimum of 12 hours of study time
per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion sections,
preparation for presentations etc.).

doc_id: 242AMST MUS 35A 1 Rock, Country, and Hip Hop History of American Popular Music
course_name: 242AMST MUS 35A 1 Rock, Country, and Hip Hop History of American Popular Music

spans.text: Contact Details\nTaylor Ackley Office location: Slosberg 224\nEmail: taylorackley@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Communication\nPlease email the professor for all class correspondence. I will do my best to respond within 48 hours\nMonday-Friday. Emails received over the weekend will be responded to at the beginning of the next week.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Meeting Times/Locations
Classes
This class is held entirely asynchronously. All materials are posted on LATTE including lectures formatted as
VoiceThreads.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Student Hours
Office hours are available by appointment. Before meeting please email me to let me know when you
would like to meet and how I can help you. This way I can prepare accordingly and avoid multiple students
trying to meet via zoom at the same time.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Course Prerequisite(s):\nMUS 35a, The History of American Popular Music, has no prerequisites,
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: but does involve a good amount of\nreading and writing. While students will be primarily evaluated for their ideas and understanding,\nproficiency in writing will aid the successful completion of this course.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
In this course students will examine the historical context, stylistic development, and cultural significance
of rock and roll and other closely related genres, spanning from the birth of America popular music
through the present. Close attention will be paid to how political and social changes have interacted with
technological innovations through commercial music to challenge, affirm and shape ideas of race, gender,
class and sexuality in the United States. Ultimately the goal of this course is to provide students with the
vocabulary, analytical tools and historical background to understand the music they listen to encounter
as people in the 21st century. While the course is structure as a lecture, class sessions will be participatory
and will provide space for students to reflect on the music they hear and study in order to share their
personal experiences and observations. By the end of the course students will be able to:
• Engage in detail with the core musical characteristics of rock by analyzing its constituent elements
(rhythm, form, pitch structure, instrumental texture, and vocal style).
• Recognize and identify important musical styles and artists by ear.

• Contextualize the role of rock music within U.S. history and society.
• Demonstrate knowledge of relationships among subcultures and subgenres within the rock music
tradition.
• Build skills in effective writing about rock music and in appropriate modes of listening.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of nine\nhours of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion sections, preparation\nfor the exams, etc.)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Textbook
All students will need to acquire a copy of the eBook 6th edition American Popular Music by Larry Starr
and Waterman. There will be assigned reading from this text as well as articles and videos shared via
LATTE. All listening will be provided as a Spotify Playlist. Because of the online nature of this course,
assigned reading and listening make up a substantial portion of the course materials. Lectures are
supplemental to this materials.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: Participation/Attendance\nThis course is asynchronous so there is no required attendance at specific times. Instead students earn\ntheir participation grade by commenting on the VoiceThread lectures.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Assignments
This class will involve regular assignments in two broad categories: Written Assignments and Discussion
Prompts. These assignments will provide the opportunity for students to demonstrate their knowledge
and understanding of class materials while reflecting their personal experiences with music. Most weeks
will have one of these smaller assignments. Written Assignments will be short responses to listening
assignments and out of class activities. Discussion Prompts will be based on course readings will be
submitted on LATTE in Forums.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Larger Projects
In addition to regular assignments there will be two larger projects spread throughout the course. These
will consist of a Listening Guide and a Final Project. Detailed instructions for these will be provided on
LATTE.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all
students, and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability
accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can
contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student
Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful student FAQs and other resources
on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be eligible for
support from SAS.

If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a
copy as soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of
accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate exam accommodations,
ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an
exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Evaluation and Grading
Student performance will be evaluated in five categories shown below. For specifics see Course
Requirements or ask the professor:
Class Element Grade Percentage
Written Assignments 30%
Discussion Prompts 20%
Listening Guide Project 15%
Final Project 15%
Participation 20%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Course Plan
The basic plan for the “semester” is found below. Our course is divided into Units, focusing on a specific
time period or stylist development. Detailed reading instructions along with any Supplemental Articles
will be provided at the beginning of each Unit. Because we are covering a great deal of material this
schedule may change to accommodate the needs of the class as a whole:
Readings from Starr and Waterman’s American Popular Music - abbreviated APM
(Except where otherwise noted)
UNIT 1 6/3-7: APM Ch 1-4 Introduction and The Birth of American Popular Music
Discussion Prompt 1 Labeling Popular Music? – Due 6/7
UNIT 2 6/10-14: APM Ch 5-6 Race Records and Hillbilly Music
Written Assignment 1 What Music Represents You? – Due 6/12
UNIT 3 6/17-21: APM Ch 7 Music after World War 2
Supplemental: Brown “‘Miss Rhythm’ Speaks Out: Ruth Brown, on R&B and Covers”
Discussion Prompt 2 Covers – Due 6/21
UNIT 4 6/24-28: APM Ch 8 Rock and Roll
Listening Guide Project – Due 6/28
UNIT 5 7/1-5: APM Ch 9 The 1960s and the British Invasion
Supplemental: Williams, “Understanding Dylan,”
Discussion Prompt 3 Bob Dylan’s Poetry – Due ???

UNIT 6 7/8-12: APM Ch 10 The Counterculture: Soul and Rock in the Late 1960s
Supplemental: Brian Eno, “The Studio as Compositional Tool”
Discussion Prompt 4 The Studio as Compositional Tool – Due 7/12
UNIT 7 7/15-19: APM Ch 11 Mainstream Music in the 1970s
Written Assignment 2 The Album – Due 7/19
UNIT 8 7/22-26: APM Ch 12 Outsider Music
Final Project Proposal – Due 7/22 (note - this is the beginning of the week!)
UNIT 9 7/29-8/2: APM Ch 13-14 Popular Music in the 1980s
Written Assignment 3 Interview – Due 8/2
UNIT 10 8/5-9: APM Ch 15 Popular Music and the Internet
Supplemental: Hyden, “It’s Time to Stop Using Rock as a Catch-All Term…”
Discussion Prompt 5 What’s in a Name? – Due 8/5 (the beginning of the week)
Final Project – Due 8/9
spans.label: SCHEDULE

spans.text:
Important Policies and Resources
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which may
include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures
related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work via TurnItIn.com or similar
software to verify originality. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they
apply to a specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or
assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to
the Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be
found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Course Materials/Books/Apps/Equipment
If you are having difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your Student
Financial Services or Academic Services advisor to discuss possible funding options, including vouchers for
purchases made at the Brandeis Bookstore.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: LATTE\nLATTE is the Brandeis learning management system. Login using your UNET ID and password. For LATTE\nhelp, contact Library@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Library
The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis students, faculty
and staff. Librarians and Specialists from Research & Instructional Services, Public Services, Archives & Special

Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios, MakerLab, AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab are
available to help you through consultations and workshops.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Privacy
To protect your privacy in any case where this course involves online student work outside of Brandeis
password-protected spaces, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias. You must share the pseudonym/
alias with me and any teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may
submit such work directly to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Student Support
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. If a student, faculty, or
staff member wants to learn more about support resources, the Support at Brandeis webpage offers a
comprehensive list that includes these staff colleagues you can consult, along with other support
resources:
• The Care Team
• Academic Services (undergraduate)
• Graduate Student Affairs
• Directors of Graduate Studies in each department, School of Arts & Sciences
• Program Administrators for the Heller School and International Business School
• University Ombuds
• Office of Equal Opportunity.

doc_id: 261FA 191B 1 Studies in Renaissance and Baroque Art Raphael and his Legacy Jonathan Unglaub
school: School of Arts, Humanities, and Culture
course_name: 261FA 191B 1 Studies in Renaissance and Baroque Art Raphael and his Legacy Jonathan Unglaub

spans.text:
FA 191b Studies in Renaissance and Baroque Art Topic: Raphael and his Legacy
Th 2:20–5:10 PM Mandel Center for Humanities G10
Professor Jonathan Unglaub x6-2665 unglaub@brandeis.edu
Office Hours: T 1:00-3:00 pm, and by appointment Mandel Center 211
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
OVERVIEW:
No painter in the History of Art can rival the esteem and influence of Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio 1483-
1520). From his own lifetime until well into the 19th-century he defined the canon, though modern tastes
tend to prefer the sublime, tortured grandeur of Michelangelo, or the enigmatic vitality of Leonardo da
Vinci. Raphael forged his style from theirs to create a synthesis of unparalleled grace and power – a
classicism rivaling the ancients that formed a template for three centuries of artistic practice. This course
examines the whole range of Raphael’s output – frescoes, altarpieces, devotional icons, portraits,
drawings, engravings, tapestries, architectural designs – and considers how their embodiment of figural
elegance, harmonious design, and narrative clarity provided such enduring paradigms for the history of
art. We also probe Raphael’s own world, especially how his art served the cultural politics of the
Papacies of Julius II and Leo X during a period of social and religious upheaval. Student research
projects will either focus on certain aspects and themes of Raphael’s oeuvre or evaluate his legacy in later
epochs of art history.
Our weekly meetings will proceed in a roughly chronological trajectory through the events of Raphael’s
life and times, focusing on his major works. In the second part of the semester, students will begin to
present their own research, first on Raphael topics, and, at the end of the semester, on Raphael’s larger
legacy in the history of art.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
LEARNING GOALS
• To gain comprehensive knowledge of the paintings of Raphael, one of the most influential bodies
of work in the history of art, their development, and meaning.
• To understand development of Raphael’s mastery of various media: painting, drawing, engraving,
tapestry design, and architecture.
• Visual rhetoric: to understand how works of art were conceived and designed to tell stories and
convey messages, through expression, dramatic action, allegory, and/or symbolism.
• To understand the historical progression of painting during the period through the lens of
Raphael’s works, and the conventions and innovations that mark different genres such as
devotional imagery, historical narrative, mythological scenes, and portraiture.
• Appreciate Raphael’s status in the 16th-century critical and historiographic discourses, and how
they characterize his relation with Classical antiquity and his artistic peers (Perugino, Leonardo
da Vinci, Michelangelo, Bramante).
• Understand the social, religious, and political contexts that gave rise to major works of art in
central Italy and especially Papal Rome during Raphael’s lifespan, and how these impacted the
artist's patrons.
• Be able to write compellingly about works of art, analyzing their formal and rhetorical properties.
• Be able to draw insightfully on the scholarship of the period in a long-term research project,
encompassing outline, draft, and final paper.
• Make cogent and compelling oral presentations on aspects of Raphael’s work and legacy.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
CREDIT HOURS
Four credits: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a
minimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation on average (less when there are only
readings to prepare, more when assignments are due for class).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: COURSE REQUIREMENTS:\nThis course fulfills the WI and OC requirements in the Fine Arts major, as well as the seminar\nrequirement in Art History.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Its purpose is an in-depth examination of the primary source material and
modern scholarship (though only in English) that makes Raphael one of the most studied artists in his
time and our own. To this end, the course, and your contributions to it, have three components: Readings
/Forum postings, Written Assignments (Reading analysis and Research Paper), and Oral Presentations.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
1. Readings and Forum Posts:
Each class meeting’s readings will be anchored in excepts from the following required text (available in
bookstore, and on reserve in Goldfarb):
Paul Joannides, Raphael (London and New York: Thames and Hudon, 2022).
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Two very fine earlier monographs are on reserve in Goldfarb Library. They are useful to peruse for class
and research topics, and relevant pages are cited for each topic. They are recommended readings to
supplement Joannides but not required, except where listed as a weekly reading (full citation, instead of
<< Author, pp. >>)
Talvacchia, Bette, Raphael (London: Phaidon, 2007).
Jones, Roger and Penny, Nicholas, Raphael (New Haven: Yale University Press, 1983).
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
All other readings in the course schedule are available on MOODLE, posted in the module correlating to
the pertinent weekly course meeting. Wherever possible, these will be high-quality PDFs of articles from
journals available through on-line databases, to which Brandeis subscribes (JSTOR, Academic First
Search, etc.). Otherwise, the readings are scanned from books and journals.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: These should be read\ncarefully to respond to forum questions and prepare for class discussion.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Moodle:
In addition to the readings, each weekly module on the MOODLE will contain a link to the forum for the
readings questions. The PPT will also be posted prior to class time. At the end of the weekly module,
there are separate modules for assignments, including the research paper.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Forum postings: To ensure a thoughtful grasp and reflection on the readings, and to prepare for\ndiscussion, questions on the readings will be posted on MOODLE over the weekend preceding each\nThursday meeting.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: You must respond to these by 9am on Thursday via the MOODLE forum, about 400-\n500 words or so in total.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: The postings will not be graded, but will be accorded 1.5 points per posting, or 1\nif cursory or incomplete. It is my hope that everyone will prepare the readings and receive full points\neach week. You must post 10 of the 12 weeks.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
To prepare for each week, I recommend reading the selections from the from Joannides first (consult
Talvacchia and Jones and Penny as well if you like) for a broad picture of the topics to be considered and
then proceed to the MOODLE readings, in the order they are listed (and questions posed on forum). The
weekly forum readings questions will mostly address the focused MOODLE readings.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
2. Written Assignments
School of Athens Presentation and Reading Analysis
For the class on February 12th focusing on a single work – the School of Athens -- students will pair up to
present a reading to the class and moderate discussion. Students should at least have perused the other
readings and answered Moodle forum questions (4 on texts they are NOT presenting), even though they
will obviously focus on the presentation. Each student should write a summary analyzing their own
reading in relation to at least one of the others (3-4 pp.).
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: This should be submitted on by February 27th. A\nmore detailed prompt will be posted a week or so before.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Research Paper:
The second component is a 15-18 page research paper, which involves several stages:
1. A prospectus (two pages), outline, and preliminary bibliography (of at least 15 items) are due at
midterm (March 9th). Each student needs to meet individually in mid-March with me to discuss
this initial submission and plan for the draft. If timing works out, this meeting can also address
the oral presentation (see below).
2. A rough draft, as close to full length as possible, a is due electronically on Friday, April 17th (or
over weekend). I will return by e-mail the rough draft with comments by Friday, April 24th
(Seniors), Wednesday April 29th (others).
3. The final paper (printed, fully annotated, with captioned illustrations appended to the text) can be
submitted at your convenience during the final exam period, but no later than Wednesday, May
6th (Seniors), Tuesday, May 12th (others).
For each stage of the paper assignment, Word doc is preferred, since track changes are the simplest way
for feedback, but pdf also acceptable.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Possible topics for the research paper are listed at the end of the syllabus. These include topics on aspects\nof Raphael’s oeuvre not thoroughly covered in class, various architecture topics for Architectural Studies\nstudents, and topics on Raphael’s legacy in art history.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Reserves and Research material:
To facilitate your research, a selection of the Library’s Raphael books (including Joannides, Talvacchia,
and Jones and Penny monographs) has been placed on reserve at Goldfarb. I realize it is an
inconvenience not to be able to check out books for your research, but with multiple students requiring
the same resources, there is no other option. Other books are listed under potential research topics. These
titles are in the library stacks (consult and then reshelve accurately). Once research topics are assigned,
we will arrange for titles to be checked out individually, or shared.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
3. Oral Presentations:
As noted above, students will make a 5-7 minute illustrated oral presentation introducing a reading on the
School of Athens and posing questions on February 12th. A pair of students will present each reading.
In tandem with the research for the paper, each student will make a 15-minute illustrated presentation to
the class on the chosen topic. The Raphael topics will be presented in the second half of the semester,
when the topic corresponds to class material; legacy topics at the end of the term. Everyone should meet
with me individually at some point during the prior week (office hours or appointment), to discuss the
scope of the presentation and its visuals. These presentations need to be polished, practiced and
coherently organized – not simply a recitation of bullet points or your paper text.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Grade calculation:
15% 10 Moodle Forum Postings (0-1.5 points given each week).
5 % School of Athens Presentation (ungraded), on February 12th
10% School of Athens readings analysis, due February 27th
10% Research Presentation (various dates)
10% Midterm Prospectus / Outline / Bibliography of Research Paper, due March 9th
15% Draft of Research Paper, due April 17th
30% Final Research Paper (final exam period, by May 6th Seniors, May 12th others)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Since this class meets only once a week, attendance is mandatory. Three points will be deducted from\nyour average for each unexcused absence. Absolute maximum of two excused absences.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
MUSEUM VISIT:
Raphael was chosen as the topic for this iteration of FA 191b to coincide with the landmark retrospective
exhibition “Raphael Sublime Poetry” at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, which opens in
late March. We will try to arrange a field trip at some point in April, which will be at least partly funded.
While this cannot be made mandatory, I strongly encourage all to try their best to participate. We will
discuss possible dates and logistics in class.
spans.label: ADMIN

April 28 and later: Raphael’s Pictorial Legacy (possible topics)
* Giulio Romano: late Raphael collaborations and Roman works (Madonnas, Saint Stephen, Madonna of
Monteluce)
*Raphael’s Stylistic Heirs and Mannerism: Giulio Romano (can focus on Architecture) or Parmigianino
* Professional and Coloristic Rivalry of Raphael and Sebastiano del Piombo (also possible focus on
portraiture).
* Raphael in Late Renaissance Painting and Art theory in Italy (Carracci, Domenichino, Bellori).
* Raphael and French Classicism (Poussin, Le Sueur, Le Brun, Dufresnoy, De Piles).
* Raphael and German Classicism (Mengs, Winckelmann, Goethe, Nazarenes).
* Raphael’s Legacy in Nineteenth-Century France: Neo-classicism, Romanticism, Academic (Ingres,
Delacroix, Delaroche, etc).
* Raphael and the British Grand Manner (Reynolds, West, Copley, Kaufmann).

Cambridge University Press, 1999). ND615 .H39 1999

spans.text:
Course Description:
This course is an introduction to the home grown Nigerian industry, the world second largest film industry.
Lagos, Nigeria’s capital is the center of Nollywood which produces over 2000 films a year, reaching viewers
worldwide, mostly throughout the African continent and its diaspora. The stories told by this booming
industry shape viewer’s ideas about African life. Despite the availability of Nigerian movies on platforms
like Netflix Naija, the term Nollywood is unfamiliar to many viewers in the U.S. We will examine how
Nollywood films project local, national, and regional issues and concerns onto global screens. We will
discuss the conditions of Nollywood’s emergence, its specific genres, its audience, and explore the types of
social critiques it engages as Nigerian films grapples with topics such as globalization, migration, and gender
politics. We will also discuss how distribution, funding, and piracy affect the production and circulation of
Nollywood films. Through a final project, you will have the opportunity to conduct research on the audience
of Nollywood films in the U.S. We will learn to use literary and cinematic reading methods to understand
how aesthetics and context are all integral to understanding the Nigerian films.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals
By the end of the course students will be able to:
• Identify and interpret the aesthetic features of Nollywood films.
• cogently analyze a Nollywood film on their own terms and continue to be comfortable doing so after the class
ends.
1

• Understand how Nollywood films dramatize the social conditions and political issues affecting Nigerian of
various socio-economic and ethnic backgrounds.
• Gain an increased understanding of and respect different cultural traditions.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Course Policies and Procedures\nSuccess in this course requires nine hours of work for every three hours of in class time. Students will use that\ntime to view films, read, write posts, papers, and study for the midterm oral presentations.

spans.text: Course Structure:\nEach week class time will consist of a combination of lecture and discussion to analyze films alongside\nscholarly articles. We will also discuss how to curate a podcast type conversation around a Nollywood film.

Course Requirements:
Attendance at all course sessions is required and is 10% of the final course grade. This class is

taken as grounds for failing the course. You are responsible for all the material covered in missed classes.
If you miss more than 2 classes you must meet with me and find a way to prove your continued
engagement with the class (i.e. extra response papers, etc.).

spans.text:
Diversity Statement: This class is an intercultural encounter between filmmakers, scholars, students, and
instructors. In addition to the learning materials in this class, each one of us is a co-text because our
backgrounds and life experiences may affect how we interact with the course material. We are all figuring
out how to learn from one another most effectively. Our classroom requires a mutual effort in helping
each other understand the course material and the differing interpretative positions we may bring for a
more nuanced understanding of the narratives under study. While each one of us seek to advance our own
knowledge, we are also a community. As such, we are committed to respect and value each other’s ideas
and contributions.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Thoughts and Questions 25%:
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Students will be asked to post weekly responses (around 250 words) to the
course webpage on Moodle. Your post should connect the films we watch to the assigned readings for the
week. Posts will be due by 10: 00 pm on Wednesdays, and students will be expected to have read their
classmates’ posts before class. There are two types of posts that you may write. The first type is analytical.
Here, you may brainstorm about important aesthetic features, themes, motifs, problems, questions, etc., or
you may relate the film and readings to other discussions we’ve previously had. You should think of each
analytical post as having a mini-thesis statement, or a hypothesis/argument that you are trying to make. Most
of your posts should be of this kind. However, you may also choose to write a research-oriented post. In this
case, you can provide any type of historical, political, or cultural background that you find might help your
classmates understand the film better, though you still must be in dialogue with the assigned readings and
show that you have read and thought about them. In all cases though, avoid posts that are centered on your
likes or dislikes. You need to focus on analysis or research rather than on what makes you happy, or sad, or
angry. Posts should be grammatically correct and articulate. I want to emphasize that posts are places for
you to think through problems; discuss compelling features of a film and pose questions. I am not looking
for a developed mini-essay and I therefore will not provide individual feedback for each post, unless I feel
that you haven’t met the requirements described above. Posts are meant to provoke discussion and thought.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
As such, generative AI (including but not limited to ChatGPT, Bing, DALL-E, etc.) may not be used to
complete weekly reading responses and other assignments. There are many reasons for this policy: 1) I
am genuinely interested in your ideas; 2) the course is designed to allow us to think through and makes sense
of Nigerian films and the frameworks that guide the analysis of such films. Posing questions that you don’t
4

know the answer to is entirely legitimate.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Each post will be worth 6 points and you will receive all 6 points\nif you write a satisfactory post. If you receive less than 6 points for the post, I will provide you with a reason\nand let you know how to improve.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Midterm Exam Oral Presentation 25%:
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
In groups, you will select a film from outside our movie list. You
will receive team information on February 1st. You should share your selected film with me by February 15th
and you will present the film to the class in a creative and engaging way during the week of February 24th.
You should design your presentation with the knowledge that not all students will have seen the film. During
your 25mns, you will be expected to present background information about the film, analyze the film, show
clips, and pose questions.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Nollywood Access+ Recording of a Podcast like conversation 25%:
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
With the emergence of the “New
Nollywood,” it is noticeable that the films that circulate globally are those whose directors and producers
have established strong and mutually beneficial partnerships with streaming platforms and distributors
beyond Nigeria’s borders. For this project, your job is to write a report about the local and global circulation
Nollywood films, including the availability of Nollywood films in your local library, who, when, and in
which public settings are you likely to see Nollywood films. You will also prepare and record a podcast type
interview with someone outside of the class. This person could be a family member who watches
Nollywood; a cinephile friend who knows of Nollywood but has not had the opportunity to watch any film; a
avid consumer of Nigerian music and film.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Participation 15%:
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This grade will be determined by the quality (not quantity) of your contributions to the
course in general. The participation grade will be a factor of the following four elements: your level of
attentiveness and engagement during class discussions; your demonstrated effort throughout the semester;
your attitude and openness towards others; and your improvement and progress throughout the semester.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Also, please note that part of participating in class means having the assigned reading materials in\nfront of you and ready to reference. This also include noting specific time logs for film clips you want\nto refer the class to during discussion.

spans.text:
Although not mandatory, they are highly recommended because they are like a face-to-face class,
during which students:
• practice the language via different interactive activities
• can ask questions on unclear concepts
• take advantage of the interaction with peers
• solidify grammar structures
• further learn and are exposed to cultural aspects
• build a stronger learning community to avoid feeling isolated: online classes may sometimes
make you feel like you are learning on your own, but this couldn’t be further from the truth.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Modality:
This course is taught asynchronously, which means there is no designated day or time assigned
to the course (although there is one synchronous session with the instructor on Friday, as well as
virtual office hours or discussion groups). Asynchronous instruction generally involves accessing
content, such as recorded video lectures, readings, discussion prompts, assignments, and
assessments during a flexible time frame, with due dates as specified.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
This course will have a

daily quiz, weekly tests, and a final project at the course conclusion. The project will be
asynchronous, and the due date is in the course calendar.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Platforms:\n• Moodle\n• Zoom

spans.text:
Course Description:
This course sequence is designed for students with no previous classroom instruction or
functional ability in Modern Hebrew. Most students completing this course sequence will be able
to initiate, sustain, and close a conversation dealing with familiar topics, and will be able to write
short narratives and read authentic texts based on specific reading strategies. The majority of
students completing this level usually achieve Intermediate levels in all four language skills.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Objectives:
To acquire the fundamentals of grammar, to build vocabulary, to develop reading, oral and
written skills, and to learn about modern Israeli culture.
The course teaches Hebrew in context, by using the proficiency approach.
Topics will include getting acquainted, living environment, food, family, shopping, the days of
the week, etc.
It is anticipated that by completion of Hebrew 10, the students will be able to:
• recognize the Hebrew letters and vowels
• read Hebrew script and print
• engage in basic communicative exchanges
• introduce and provide personal information about oneself and others
• tell and ask simple questions about: places of residence, school and studies, family, daily
schedule, etc.
• make short descriptions of people, places, and actions
• read and write short passages and dialogues
• count up to 1000, tell the time, state one’s age, ask for a price, order food, etc.
• demonstrate knowledge of some aspects of the Hebrew and Israeli culture: modern and
biblical names, Hebrew dates, idiomatic expressions, maps, songs, etc.
• identify and demonstrate control of the following grammatical terms: pronouns; gender and
number agreement; prepositions; the root and verb system, present and past tense,
infinitive, regular and irregular verbs; possessive, etc.
• look up words in a dictionary
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Requirements:
Homework, quizzes and chapter tests are very important and constitute a major portion of the
final grade.
The final exam will include three parts: reading, writing and grammar, and speaking.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
The entry level of our course, according to ACTFL is Novice low, the expected end of
course level is Intermediate Low.
Novice Proficiency Level according to ACTFL proficiency guidelines:
Students begin level 10 having just arrived at the Novice low level. According to the ACTFL,
students at this level cannot yet communicate. However, after two weeks of study, they will be
at the Novice mid-level, and they will be able to convey short messages on highly predictable,
everyday topics that affect them directly. Students do so primarily through the use of isolated
words and phrases that have been encountered, memorized, and recalled. Novice-level students
may be difficult to understand even by the most sympathetic interlocutors accustomed to non-
native speech.
By the end of the course most students will emerge at the proficiency level of Intermediate
Low, depending on motivation, effort, and diligence.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Based on the ACTFL proficiency guidelines students will be able to:
Speaking Successfully handle a number of uncomplicated communicative tasks by
creating with the language in straightforward social situations.
Conversation is mostly of the concrete exchanges and predictable topics
necessary for survival in the target-language culture. These topics relate to
basic preferences.
Listening Understand most of the information from sentence-length speech, one
utterance at a time, in personal and social contexts, though comprehension
is often uneven.
Writing Exhibit limited, practical writing needs. They can create statements and
questions based on familiar material. Most sentences are recombination of
learned vocabulary and structures. These are short and simple style
sentences with basic word order. They are written almost exclusively in
present/past tense. Writing tends to consist of a few simple sentences.
Vocabulary is adequate to express personal needs related to personal
contexts. There may be basic errors in grammar, word choice, punctuation
and spelling,
Reading Understand most of the information from the straightforward connected
comprehension texts dealing with a limited number of personal needs related to personal
contexts, although there may be some misunderstandings. Readers at this
level will be challenged to derive meaning from connected texts of any
length.
Reading Students will be able to read accurately and fluently out loud familiar
Pronunciation passages such as simple description, biographies, poems in an automated
manner. Students will be able to decode unfamiliar texts that are similar to
the learned material texts and most of the time to read it out loud with some
degree of fluency.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
General:
• Past experience has taught us that beginners will very quickly catch up with their
classmates even though some of them may have studied Hebrew for several years.

individual questions you may have. B.U.G.S - Brandeis University Group Study is open for
all levels of Hebrew for any purpose, practice speaking, study for a test, grammar, etc.

Success in this 4 - credit hour language course is based on the expectation that students will spend

spans.text:
Academic Honesty:
You must complete all assignments alone. In your writing, you must follow rules of attribution,
meaning that you must cite all sources consulted in preparing your papers. As stated in
the Student Handbook, “Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the
highest standards of academic honesty. A student shall not receive credit for work that is not the
product of the student’s own effort.” Examples of penalties for a student found responsible for
an infringement of academic honesty are no credit for the work in question, failure in the course,
and the traditional range of conduct sanctions from disciplinary warning through permanent
dismissal from the University. The use of any online translator will be considered as
cheating.

spans.text:
2 Course Decription
2.1 Learning Goals
This course will introduce students to the basic analytic tools to understand
the behavior of individuals and firms, and analyze their interaction in the mar-
ket. We will cover the application of consumer theory, the theory of the firm,
general equilibrium and welfare, game theory, and oligopolistic markets. By
the end of the class, you should be equipped with the necessary skills to ap-
proach real-world economic and public policy questions in a rigorous way.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:

spans.text:
6 Course Plan
1. Introduction
Introductiontothecourse,adiscussionofmarkets,andreviewofconcepts
GLS Chapter 1
2. Math Camp
Review of calculus concepts used in later parts of the class.
3. Supply and Demand
Review of elasticities, cross price elasticities. Using demand and supply
curves. Consumer and producer surplus. Government interventions.
GLS Chapter 2, 3
4. Consumer Demand
Consumer preferences, utility functions and indifference curves, Marginal
Rate of Substitution, budget constraints, consumer choice, and consumer
optimization, revealed preference.
GLS Chapter 4
5. Derive Demand Functions
Derivation of individual demand from utility maximization, income and
substitution effects, market demand; Derivation of individual demand
from expenditure minimization the Slutsky equation
GLS Chapter 5
6. Producer Behavior
Productionfunctions,productionwith1input,2inputs,isoquants,Marginal
Rate of Technical Substitution
GLS Chapter 6
7. Cost Functions
Cost functions, isocost lines, cost minimization problem. Supply-side and
firmdecisions. Differenttypesofproductioncosts,costminimizationprob-
lem, economies of scale.
GLS Chapter 7
8. Perfect Competition and Monopoly
Profit maximization. Monopoly and Monopsony. Markets with only one
firm. Sources of monopoly. The monopolist problem.
GLS Chapter 8, 9
4

9. Price Discrimination
Non-uniform pricing strategies for firms with monopoly power.
GLS Chapter 10
10. Games
Collusion and Game Theory
GLS Chapter 12
11. Oligopoly and Imperfect Competition
Markets with few firms, quantity setting, price setting.
GLS Chapter 11
12. Factor Markets
Supplyanddemandincompetitivefactormarket;imperfectlycompetitive
factor market
GLS Chapter 13
13. Choice Under Uncertainty
Expected values. Preferences towards risk: risk aversion, risk neutrality,
risk-loving behavior.
GLS Chapter 14
14. Markets with Asymmetric Information
Adverseselection, moralhazard, principal-agentproblems, marketsignal-
ing.
GLS Chapter 16
15. General Equilibrium
Multipleintersectingmarkets,grosssubstitutes,grosscompliments. Social
welfare function, production, consumption, and total efficiency. Equity
and efficiency. Edgeworth boxes.
GLS Chapter 15
16. Behavioral Economics (if time permits)
Behavioral biases, incorporating Behavioral Economics into models, ex-
periments
GLS Chapter 18
spans.label: SCHEDULE

Professor Erin Gee Mozart: Piano Sonata No. 5, K. 283 (Munich,

from Phillip Magnuson
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
In this course, we will explore the music of the common practice period, from Bach (1695A –na lysis 6. Beethoven: Minuet
1750) to Brahms (1833 – 1897) and structurally similar music from different time periods. We
Analysis 7. Haydn: Sonatina, Hob. XVI:1
will analyze how this music is made, construct our own musical fragments that reflect this
Analysis 8. Mozart: Waltz. K.567
structure, and develop the skills to deconstruct music from this musical language. We will
distinguish the concepts at the basis of this fascinating music, and generate 4-part harAmnoalnyysi sa 9n. dB eethoven: Sonata, op.14, no.2
other textures that integrate these principles. I’ll also use more recent music to give examples of
Analysis 10. Mozart: Sonata, K.283
the same principles in different musical genres. During the semester, you’ll become composers
Analysis 11. Mozart: Sonata, K.330
1 Analysis 12. Haydn: Sonata, Hob. XVI:34
Final Project
Copyright © 2008-2009 by Phillip Magnuson.
1 of 2 8/15/14 12:07 PM
1 of 1 8/15/14 12:41 PM

yourselves, and will create your own music based on the well-honed principles from this early
time period.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Objectives – You Will:
• Develop knowledge about the hierarchy of harmonies in Tonal music.
• Synthesize this knowledge while writing Bach-style counterpoint.
• Acquire the skills to write music in the style of Mozart, Haydn and Beethoven.
• Enhance your interpretation of the music you perform, by understanding underlying
structures.
• Cultivate your interest in the relationship between theory and performance.
• Embark on an exciting journey away from home (the home key) and back again.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
To Be Successful in this Course:
There will generally be a homework assignment due almost every day of class. Keep your
completed homework throughout the semester – you will need it to study for the final.
• Plan Ahead
• Ask questions if you need further insight
• Read and re-read what is assigned
• Start studying early
• Remember that daily work will bring greater rewards than a concentrated marathon of
studying
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Required Text and Materials:
• Steven G. Laitz, The Complete Musician: An Integrated Approach to Tonal Theory,
Analysis, and Listening, 5th, 4th or 3rd Edition. Even the 2nd edition is ok.
• Tenuto app – You can purchase this on the app store for $4.99.
• Homework Packet and Supplement Reading (more info on Moodle next week)
• Large 3-ring binder. You’ll probably also like a three-hole punch and stapler.
• Music Manuscript Paper (10 or 12 Staves, on 8 ½ x 11 paper or larger) Visit the course
website on Moodle for manuscript paper examples. Bring manuscript paper and pencil
to class every day.
• Pencils (no pens). All assignments must be completed in pencil.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: • The use of laptops, cell phones and ipads is not allowed during class, except when\nworking on music notation.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: Grading:\nHomework: 30%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Midterms I, II: 20%\nFinal Exam: 15% Listening Exam is 1% of the Final Exam grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Homework must be done individually. Lowest grade dropped.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Quizzes: 15%
Composition Project: 10%
Attendance: 5%
Game: 5%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Grades:
Your final course grade will be determined by the total number of points you acquire during the
semester from quizzes, exams, assignments, compositions and group projects.
We will be using the +/- grading system. Generally, and A- will be 90-92%, A = 93-96% and
A+ = 97-100% (and so on).
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Quizzes and Exams: Short, timed quizzes will be given throughout the semester to test\ncomprehension and speed. There will also be two midterm exams and a final exam, including a\nlistening exam.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
String Quartet Composition Project: This exciting project will allow you to create your own
piece and synthesize the concepts you have learned in the class.
The Lydian String Quartet will perform your piece at the end of the semester.
You will base your composition on a piece that you have chosen from the common practice era
(Bach – Brahms) or any other style of music. This piece will be your “model”. Imagine that you
are creating a set or a costume for a specific period-drama from a specific time period – in
order to be historically accurate there are certain rules you would need to follow: e.g. no virtual
reality headset, no fitbit, no smart phone. A similar principle applies to this composition project
in any style. I encourage you to muster up every ounce of creativity that you have…. while still
remaining within the confines of the chosen model time period or chosen model pieces of
music and to follow the musical “grammar” that we’ve learned in class. It’s a challenge – and it
will work that “creativity muscle”!
The following steps will help to enhance your creativity and keep you on track throughout the
semester. By making timely small steps (i.e. not putting it off to the end of the semester) you
will be able to create something amazing in a timely fashion. Here are some things that we will
be talking about regarding your working method for the assignment. We’ll talk about this in
detail as the semester progresses:
1. Find a piece by Mozart, Beethoven, Haydn or another composer of your choice from
the common practice period to use as a model. I will make a list of pieces that you could
possibly use as starting points for your search, although you may choose any piece that
you wish. Music from the common practice time period can be found at: imslp.org
2. Do a Roman numeral analysis of the piece as much as you can. Ask me or your TA for
help if you get stuck. This will provide the harmonic basis for your own piece. Of
course, feel free to make changes as you like, but remember to stick to the practices of
the time, or the practices of your model piece.
3. Identify the main motive. What does it consist of rhythmically? Melodically? What
intervals are most prominent? What durations are used most frequently? You can build
your motive out of the same building blocks that are used to create the motive in your
model piece.
4. Decide on a simple form. A B A’ is a good one to start with. We’ll talk about this in
class.
3

5. Decide on what key you will use. If you choose a major key, the B section should be in
the relative minor or the key of the dominant (V). If you choose a minor key, then the B
section should be in the key of the relative major.
6. Create your own motive in the key of your choice.
7. Develop the motive melodically. Make sure that it fits with the harmonies that you have
chosen from your model piece.
8. Decide on phrase length. How long is your antecedent phrase? How long is your
consequent phrase?
9. What is the harmonic rhythm of the piece, i.e. how quickly do the harmonies change?
10. Create a contrasting motive in the new key for the B section. Develop it and make it fit
with the harmonies that you have chosen. Again, identify your phrases.
11. Look at the textures that are used in the model composition. Does Mozart use Alberti
bass? (yes indeed.) Or is a chorale texture used? Is the melody or important “voice” (it
could also be an instrument) in the top voice or in the bass? What kind of baseline is
used? What are the inner voices doing? Is the melody exchanged between the
instruments (usually it is…). Texture is an area where you can be very creative and
explore the ways that the instruments can combine and pass around the melody line.
Texture encompasses instrumentation, rhythm, articulation, timbre, pitch, harmony,
repetition, register, etc. We’ll be discussing all of these in detail. Think of texture as all of
the components that make up the “fabric” of the sound.
12. Put it all together, (should be about 2 minutes long) practice and sing through the piece
at the piano to check that you love what you’ve written and…
13. Voilá! You have practiced the skill of composing!
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Concert attendance: There are 3 concerts that you will get to attend for homework points this
semester. Extra credit will be given for writing a thoughtful 300-400-word journal entry on the
concert attended (due 1 week after the concert date). There will be further guidance on this
posted on Moodle. I’ll give you more info about this later in the semester.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Due Dates:
1. Due at the beginning of class on the specific day, these should be uploaded to Moodle
before the beginning of class.
2. Use the app Turboscan or similar to create a clear PDF of your assignment. You may
also use a scanner to create a PDF to upload to MOODLE. Please do not upload
photos of your assignment because the markings are usually difficult to read.
3. Any assignment turned in later than 10 min. after the beginning of class will be
considered 1 day late. Assignments that are 1 day late receive 5% off their given grade.
Assignments that are 2 days late will receive 10% off. Assignments over two days late
will be accepted after talking with me – they will receive a continual 2% off each. These
are actual days and not class days. It’s most important to simply do the work even if it’s
late. I’m here to help you!
4. Students with an excused absence may turn in late homework for full credit . If there is a
University conflict, please arrange with me regarding the new due date.

are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis University Rights
and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Allegations of

3. Expectations for student contributions to coursework
Each course credit carries an expectation that in order to be successful a student needs to engage in
approximately 45 hours of combined in-class and out-of-class work and preparation, where “out-of-
class work” comprises readings, papers, discussion sections, problem sets, etc. Based on this rule,

least 9 hours of study time per week in preparation for class.

spans.text:
It is the policy of the university that instructors strive to support students’ observance of their
traditions by allowing absence from classes for such purposes; by endeavoring to ensure that
examinations, written reports, oral reports or other mandatory class assignments are not scheduled for

Only the dates of the holidays themselves are considered excused absences.

spans.text:
World Languages and Cultures
A cornerstone of global citizenship, proficiency in a world language is essential for effective
communication, cooperation and collaboration among people of different cultures.
The process of learning another language inspires open-mindedness and adaptability,
awakens curiosity and creativity, fosters innovative paths of inquiry and discovery,
and builds a deeper understanding of oneself and others.

spans.text:
Accommodations

Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students,
and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability
accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can
contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student
Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful student FAQs and other resources on the
SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be eligible for support from
SAS. If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as
soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In
order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter
at least 48 hours before an exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Course Description
Course Prerequisite(s):
A grade of C- or higher in JAPN 10A or the equivalent.
If you have not completed JAPN10A at Brandeis and you have studied Japanese somewhere,
please consult your instructors as soon as possible and ask if JAPN20B is the appropriate level for
you.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
The course aims to have students further develop their levels of proficiency that was acquired in
JAPN10A: speaking, listening, reading, and writing as well as understanding various aspects of
Japanese culture. Upon completion of this course, students will be able to understand Japanese
daily life and society to deepen their insight into Japanese culture in a culturally and linguistically
appropriate way. Students will learn more vocabulary, expressions, additional 72 Kanji (total of
115) and their compounds, and grammatical structures to expand their communicative skills
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Objectives:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:
1)Perform (speaking and writing) basic communicative tasks (Interpersonal communication)
such as :
a. favorite things/activities,
b. family,
c. vacations,
d. health,
e. past experiences,
f. comparison,
g. expressing desire, thoughts and opinions.
2)understand spoken and written Japanese (Interpretative communication) on familiar topics in
everyday life, the difference between informal speech and formal speech.
3)Understand various aspects of Japanese culture, customs, and values (Intercultural
communication).
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Four Credit Hours:
Success in this 4 credit hour course is based on the expectation that students will spend a
minimum of 12 hours of study time per week in classroom and preparation for class (homework
assignments, readings, papers, discussion sections, preparation for exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Attendance
Daily classroom attendance is required: Daily attendance and participation is the key to
developing your foreign language proficiency and competence.
• Please come to the class on time. Coming late, into a class in progress, will not only affect
you, but also distract the whole class.
• If you come late for the class, points will be taken from your daily attendance. A student
who comes in 15 minutes late will be considered absent.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
• Grading policy for Attendance and daily participation
o 0 points: absent or come late more than 15 minutes.
o 1 point: attended, but did not actively participate or showed lack of preparation, or
came late more than 10 minutes.
o 2 points: attended and actively participated and showed preparation
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: • More than 13 absences will result in automatic failure of the course.\n• Please contact the instructor in advance about any unavoidable absence or provide\nevidence (e.g. a note from an advisor, a doctor, or an appropriate office).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
• Attending a section in which you are not enrolled is not permitted. Only those who
submitted “Time Conflict Form” can attend another section. In cases of “excused” absence,
you may attend another section. If the absence is NOT "excused," you will not get an
attendance/participation credit by attending another section. Still, it may be better to attend
another section than not attending at all, so that you do not miss the lesson content.
• Unexcused absences will result in a zero point for your daily participation. You are
responsible for checking the material covered during your missed class.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Examples of Excused Absences:
Participation in athletics / Participation in required student activities / Illness and personal
crisis / Religious observances / Jury duty, etc. Please consult your instructor if you have
questions.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Assignments\nAssignments must be turned in before the class on each due date.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
• Points (10% every 24 hours) will be taken off for lateness and poor work.
• No credit is given when an assignment is submitted more than 3 days late (more than 72
hours late).
• Assignments turned in late due to excused absences must be accompanied by a doctor’s
note and/or other evidence.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: • It is all right to discuss homework with your classmates. However, it is your responsibility\nto write your own individual answers. Copying or letting someone else copy an assignment\nis “cheating.”

spans.text: Exams and Quizzes\nThere are two quizzes (vocabulary and Kanji) and one exam per one chapter.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
You are allowed to make up quizzes and exams you missed within ONE week from the scheduled
date/time. Each make-up quiz/exam is worth 90% of the actual score.
Exceptions may be granted in emergencies with official proof provided to your lecturer. In order
to take a make-up quiz and exam, please set up a time with your lecturer as soon as possible.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
NOTE: Any quiz is considered a “make-up” unless you take the quiz in a class that you register
for with the exception of the fact that your lecturer approves when you submit a letter
that describes the reason for the make-up. In this case, you will be given the actual
score that you get.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Participation

You are required to actively participate in oral practice, questions and answers, role-plays, oral
presentations and other classroom activities. You will be graded on your participation in the class,
not simply on your attendance.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Some of the classes will be conducted with Flipped classroom approach. In this teaching/ learning
approach, students are expected to learn a grammatical concept with video materials outside of the
classroom before class. In the classroom, more time will be spent for communicative activities
such as interactions, discussions and presentations. Thus, it is important to complete homework
before each class time because homework is mainly preparation for the next class
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: • Class participation will be evaluated based on: coming to the class on time, active\nparticipation, and preparedness.\n• Unexcused absences will result in a zero daily participation point.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: A distracting behavior such as eating and the unnecessary use of electronic devices (e.g., texting)\nis not allowed.
spans.label: CONDUCT

Important Policies and Resource
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of
academic integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the
student's own effort. Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to
serious penalties, which may include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension
from the University or other sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be
required to submit work via TurnItIn.com or similar software to verify originality. A student who
is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they apply to a specific course or

assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or assignment before
submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the
Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be
found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.

spans.text:
Laptop computer and cell phone use
Laptop and cell phone use during a language class is a distraction. I rely on your maturity to keep your
devices turned off and put away for the duration of class. Using devices during class time or leaving class to
use them in the halls will result in a lowered participation grade. Please refer to the Participation Grading
Rubric on Moodle for details.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: During class, you will be asked to communicate in French to the best of your abilities. Do not be\ndiscouraged by errors, challenges, or corrections – this is an important part of the process. Indeed, if you\nare not making mistakes and are already speaking in perfect French, this is not the right class for you!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: If you\nfind that you have questions about placement, please do not hesitate to speak to me about options.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
French 32 is designed to help students improve their command of French, specifically to develop their
speaking skills and to enrich their knowledge of Francophone society, boosting them to the ACTFL
intermediate level. The class will work on all skill areas (speaking, listening, writing, reading, culture) while
addressing themes pertinent to the modern Francophone world that generate discussion or controversy,
such as the role of artificial intelligence in our lives, gender identity, racial identity, and a variety of cultural
and historical topics. Using a variety of authentic French language media, we will work on your ability to
discuss personal and social topics, support ideas and opinions with facts and personal experience, read
short cultural and literary texts, participate in class discussions, and express your opinions in writing.
Assessment is based on daily homework assignments, a series of oral and written exams/ projects, a record
of attendance and participation, and a series of final assignments.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Please note that in this class, called “Controverses,” we will learn vocabulary and grammar structures that
allow you to engage in healthy debate with francophone peers. As does Brandeis, I consider the diversity
that students bring to the course an invaluable resource. I will present materials and activities that are
respectful of diversity: gender, sexuality, physical ability, age, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, race, and
culture. Each day, we view images and read texts that may reflect other realities and worldviews, which is the
purpose of the humanities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
This semester you will
- develop analytic tools to examine and better understand francophone culture, particularly issues of
controversy that concern young adults (see above).
-improve your confidence in using spoken French in informal and formal situations.
-expand your vocabulary and grammar skills to communicate effectively in different situations while
reviewing “Novice” vocabulary topics, such as family, home, and food.
-improve your pronunciation by focusing on the most difficult sounds of the language (/r/, nasals, etc.).
-express your ideas about past, present, and future events and your opinions and beliefs in a clear, concise,
and persuasive manner in written and oral forms.
-read, analyze, and discuss short literary works, images, films, advertisements, and articles at an intermediate
low-mid level.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Outcomes:
We will measure your ability to reach the above goals by requiring you to
-incorporate pertinent cultural material into class presentations, writings, projects, and exams.
-present phonetically and grammatically accurate verbal reports and conversations.
-manipulate new and old vocabulary and grammar in different modalities (written and oral).
-compose short reports, posters, reactions, opinions, and analyses.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Social justice, attitudes, and behaviors: Learning French will enable you to gain a deeper understanding of
different regions of the francophone world and support your participation as informed citizens in a global
society.
This course will specifically
• expand your knowledge and awareness of traditions and artistic expressions of different
francophone cultures.
• encourage you to reflect on your culture, and weigh its similarities and differences with those of
other cultures.
• better understand what connects people worldwide – linguistically, culturally, and otherwise –
through study of texts, film, and music.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Credit Hours: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a\nminimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class, not including exam preparation.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Materials: All course materials are found on the course Moodle page.
-Controverses, Cahier d’activités, Didier Bertrand, Janet Solberg. Cengage, 2016, Third edition: All
exercises are available on the Moodle page.
- Answer key to the workbook
- Readings, films, podcasts, Slides
*Use www.wordreference.com or linguee.fr (online bilingual dictionaries) – not Google Translate.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Homework Assignments
“Cahier d’activités” = “Student activities manual”): You must complete and correct the grammar exercises in
the workbook (scanned for you on Moodle) for the date indicated. Corrections should be made in a
different color. Because you have the corrections on Moodle, we will not spend time reviewing all the
exercises in class, but we will discuss those that were difficult for you. If you do not ask questions about the
workbook assignments in class, I will assume that you completely understand the material. All workbook
exercises assigned for the chapter will be submitted on the final day of the chapter as a single document,
either on paper or as a pdf.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Late work policy: All homework will be due on Moodle on fixed dates. Each day (24 hours) that an\nassignment is late or missing will incur a late penalty of 10%. A missed in-person assessment (ex. a chapter\ntest, quiz, presentation, interview) without notice will not be rescheduled at a later date.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Participation and Attendance
Because language is a social and interactive phenomenon, attendance and participation are essential to your
success in this class. Learning to speak another language demands in-class engagement. Participation is
noted on a daily basis.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Your attendance counts for 10% of your final grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
You are allowed four absences
for any reason; no justification or explanation will be required. Three tardy arrivals/ early departures for
class equal one absence. Each absence that exceeds the allowed number will result in a lower grade in two
ways: specifically, each additional absence will result both a lowered attendance grade and 1% subtracted
from the final course grade. Eight or more absences will result in a discussion with your professor and the
Director of Languages to ascertain whether you can continue in the class, and it may result in a failing
course grade. Students in situations that will require them to be absent for an extended period should
contact their Academic Advisor; the Advisor will contact your professor about excused absences. If you
miss the first two classes of the semester with no official accommodation, you may be automatically
dropped from the class.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
*Absences due to sports activities, pertinent accommodations as determined by SAS, or religious
obligations will be considered separately. If you must be absent from class for those reasons, please let me
know in advance when possible. In the event that you will miss an in-class assignment or test, please let me
know at once 1 week in advance.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Distribution of Grades
• Attendance 10%
• Participation 10%
• Homework 10%
• 3 written chapter tests 20%
• 2 oral chapter tests (1 in person, 1 film project) 15%
• 1 Extracurricular Activity + follow-up 10%
• Final oral exam 10 %
• Final written exam 15 %
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
New this semester! Proficiency in Language Transcript Notation after
completing two courses beyond the language requirement (FREN 32)
• Successful completion of FREN 105 (or your course, if appropriate) entitles you to receive

the Proficiency in Language Transcript Notation, which is a note on your transcript that
formally documents your achievement in language learning at Brandeis.
• The Notation explicitly recognizes your success at demonstrating an Intermediate-High level
of language proficiency and cultural understanding in French beyond the Brandeis Core
requirement, and it officially commends you on your efforts to expand and maintain your
multilingualism.
• If you are interested in the Transcript Notation, please contact Prof. Harder
(harder@brandeis.edu), the Director of the language program in French. She will register
you for the notation.
spans.label: ADMIN

Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to

Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. If a student, faculty, or
staff member wants to learn more about support resources, the Support at Brandeis webpage offers a

course_name: 253FREN 126B 1 La place de la nature dans le monde culturel francophone Hollie Harder

spans.text: Room TBA\nMonday, Wednesday, Thursday 11:15 a.m. -12:05 p.m.\n(in person)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
In this course, you will deepen your knowledge and understanding of the role of nature in the
Francophone world by examining some of the cultural, political, literary, and artistic
principles that contributed to creating its unique place in the world.
For example, we will discover how, during the Renaissance, the purpose of nature seemed
limited to satisfying the needs, desires and pleasures of human beings. We will try to make
sense of what drove French culture to popularize the notion of le jardin français with its
conical trees and geometrical shrubbery, we will explore what made people around the
Francophone world withdraw into nature as though it were a protective haven, and
subsequently abuse it, especially in the former French colonies in the Caribbean and in
Western Africa, for the purpose of productivity and profit. You will examine your own
0

attitudes toward nature and how your "culture of nature" compares to that of the
Francophone world.
spans.label: DESCRIP
