doc_id: 261BUS 297C 1 Leadership Internships in Social Impact Organizations Ian Roy
course_name: 261BUS 297C 1 Leadership Internships in Social Impact Organizations Ian Roy

spans.text:
Contact Details
Professor: Ian Roy
Email: ianroy@brandeis.edu
Office in the Library, Farber 2
Meeting Times
Classes
Wednesday 5:40-7:00PM - Lemnberg 54 Classroom
Office Hours
Office hours are by appointment. Use email or the booking link below
https://bit.ly/meetwithianroy
We can always meet on zoom, in my office, in the Lemberg Building, or in one of the BDI Labs
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description and Objectives
Course Description
“Real-world challenges. Real community partners. Leadership through action.”
Leadership in Social Impact Innovation is a two-semester course offering a total of 4 academic
credits, 2 each semester. Both semesters are required, not optional. The fall class is a traditional
academic offering with a weekly class meeting time in addition to the board fellow assignment.
The spring term is centered on experiential work, both the amplification of board fellow role
and participation in Deis Hacks, our 48 hour hackathon for social good.
This experiential learning graduate level class provides an opportunity for students to
individually assume high level strategic, tactical and/or leadership assignments in social impact
organizations. Students may become a non-voting board fellow as part of their assignment
and/or may be providing board level strategic and tactical consulting, depending on the nature
of the host organization and the scope of each student’s particular assignment. All
opportunities will provide a forum for students to develop and amplify their own unique brand
of high stakes contributions to real world social impact organizations. A limited number of
undergraduate business majors will be accepted into the class.
As part of their work, students will be exposed to high level organizational as well as business
leadership from the community. Most partnership organizations will be not for profits, but some
may be social for profit enterprises and there may also be foundations and other social funding
assignments. Students will emerge from this experience with a unique multifaceted lens into
the social innovation sector and their own personal abilities to impact entities, whether it be
commercial or not for profit oriented, at a high stakes level.

Each student will be paired with a social impact organization with a somewhat defined
responsibility. Through class discussions as well as individual mentorship with the instructor and
organization leadership, his or her assignment will be developed and advanced. The objective
is to not function as an intern but rather, tackle a challenge with potential strategic impact and
tactical feasibility at a senior level. Students will provide weekly communication to their host
organization as well as the professor as to their activities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Participation in this course is by application only, to apply go to
https://students.global.brandeis.edu/register/Field_Project_Fall_2025
A phone interview may be a part of the selection process. Class is limited to 18 participants,
either at the undergraduate or graduate level.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
The primary student outcome is to gain high-level leadership, strategy and tactical experience
in real world organizations. As part of this class, students will be tackling roles rooted in one or
more of the academic tracks of marketing, real estate, entrepreneurship, finance and data
analytics. All assignments will be with organizations striving for social impact. The course counts
as elective credits for the Brandeis School of Business and Economics MBA and undergraduate
business degree and this course will fulfill the general work practicum requirement for the MA
students. In addition, with the approval of the relevant program director, this course may also
fulfill the work practicum and capstone requirement of the Real Estate, Data Analytics and
Marketing concentrations of the MA.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Credit Hours/Workload Statement
Success in this two-term, four- credit course is based on the expectation that students will
spend in addition to the weekly class time of 1 hour and 20 minutes in the fall term, another
4-5 hours weekly developing their work and in meetings with their host organizations.
In the spring semester, the weekly work around the board fellow assignment expands and there
is a heavy concentration of strategic and leadership involvement in preparation for and during
Deis Hacks. There will not be weekly class meeting during this term but there will however be
several boot camp type sessions and one on one mentoring times with Professor Miller during
the semester.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Goals and Outcomes
▪ Opportunity to utilize classroom learnings in a real-world experience that focuses on
individual professional and personal growth
▪ Chance to be individually responsible for a high stakes role assignment with strategic
impact
▪ Class will function as a cohort and will include cross training and collaboration among
students
▪ Responsibilities will include a presentation(s) to the organization’s board and
management
▪ Exposure for students to high level decision-making for an organization
1

▪ Opportunity to advance personal leadership skills as well as tactics and strategy around
real-world challenges, opportunities and problems.
▪ Develop comfort level with functioning around the high-level leadership in
organizations that will likely include the board room.
▪ Enhance students’ resumes and contacts with experience working with real
organizations
▪ This responsibility can be noted on your resume with the organization’s name but be
careful to state your role and not suggest or state you were an employee of the
sponsoring organization. It provides very interesting topics to discuss at a job interview
and distinguishes you from the competition.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisites\nMaturity, self-motivation, desire to succeed and strong communication abilities in English.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Plan
This class differs in many ways from the conventional course, which will create a number of
challenges during the semesters. In particular, unlike the traditional class with its well-defined
student-instructor role, you must deal with multiple people relationships. First, my role is more
of a manager and mentor than a traditional lecturer and, depending on how well the
assignment is progressing, I will have anywhere from moderate to heavy involvement in your
assignment. You must also deal with your cohort class members who may have different ideas
and work styles. You will also have the sponsor host organization, with multiple people playing
different roles relating to yours. Your success in this course will depend on your ability to rise to
the challenge presented, operating independently, but with mentorship and high-level
guidance from the instructor.
In taking this class you will experience a number of real-world frustrations, including: Lack of
complete role clarity
▪ Delays in communications and deadlines
▪ Changing assignment scope: bigger, narrower, or different
▪ Organizations can be unsure of what they are seeking from your role
▪ Your assignment may not always seem important to your host
▪ Constituents not responding to calls and emails and requests
▪ Organization sponsors or leaders leave their organizations
I want to assure you that this is the reality of both the business and social sector worlds.
Leaders are very busy and, in some cases, various individuals in the client organization might
be in survival mode to keep their jobs. It is essential that you apply personal motivation and
individual leadership to your work – develop your path and demonstrate your impact.
This course will expose you to these real-world leadership/management challenges and
frustrations, and more importantly, teach you how to rise up to meet them. For example, you
will learn how persistence and diplomacy is central to functioning at the right level.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Academic Integrity
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be
required to submit work to TurnItinl.com software to verify originality. Allegations of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the director of academic integrity. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. Citation
and research assistance can be found on the university library website.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Communications
All communication outside of class will take place via email and LATTE. If you have another
student working with your non-profit board, please discuss with one another which platform
you are most comfortable using to communicate.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Assignments
All weekly class assignments will be posted on LATTE at the beginning of each week.
The on-going project assignments with your host organization might involve a variety of
high-level organizational challenges and opportunities, such as:
▪ Developing a social media strategy and plan to advance organizational impact and
foster community engagement
▪ Construct and early adoption of analytics for measuring an organization’s impact
▪ Anchoring the strategic plan for board leadership to achieve its objectives
▪ Assuming a role in shaping the institutional advancement of an organization to attract
increased resources
▪ Design and institute financial modeling for organizations with an asset development
orientation
▪ Constructing a strategic partnership between organizations to expand impact
▪ Real estate support to include acquisitions/dispositions, tax credits and advancement of
affordable housing programming
The assignments/projects will expose students to a wide array of organizational leadership
challenges that are found in all types of entities. Sponsoring organizations will range in size
from small and local to larger and multi-regional. They may be social enterprises organized as
commercial entities or social sector (not for profit) organizations. The scope of student activities
will include meetings at the organization and may include a non-voting board fellow role
depending on the host organization. Students will be individually accountable however, there
may be another student tackling a different role for the same sponsor organization.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: During the course of the year the fellows will assume a leadership role in a social impact\nhackathon in conjunction with the Brandeis MakerLab. DeisHacks will be held January 30-31st,\n2025 and participation is a requirement of the course.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
A high degree of maturity and self-motivation are important prerequisites for acceptance into
the program. Additionally, assignments will require extensive communication within an
organization and its community. Accordingly, English language skills and a strong comfort level
with social interactions at various levels are critical.
Participation
Attendance and active participation in class is essential. Interaction with the class cohort is an
important aspect of a successful experience and students will be expected to demonstrate
collaborative approach, often cross training one another in this course. Individual meeting
times with organizations will be situationally tailored to the host’s meeting schedule and are
critical to a successful engagement. Participation in the social impact two-week hackathon is
also a requirement of the class. The class time will include of a combination of class meetings
as well as individual meetings with the instructor. During both terms, there will be a series of
guest presentations from social entrepreneurs, not for profit executives as well as business
leaders active in board leadership. Students must attend class during the scheduled class time
with video cameras on (if attending virtually) , unless pre-approved by the professor (different
time-zone, etc).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Portfolio\nPlease submit all weekly reflections in their respective folders on Moodle. All other material\nshould be submitted via Moodle unless stated otherwise.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Evaluation
The individual student grades will be based on contribution/participation, deliverables, host
feedback, extra effort, and work product.
35%: DELIVERABLES (QUALITY AND TIMELINESS)
▪ Development of project timeline
▪ Overall project for host organization
▪ Self-reflections/evaluation and weekly updates and weekly assignments
30%: FEEDBACK FROM HOST ORGANIZATION
▪ Timeliness
▪ Detail
▪ Initiative
▪ Follow through
▪ Creativity
30%: CLASS, BOOTCAMPS and HACKATHON ATTENDANCE AND PARTICIPATION
▪ Leadership in class
▪ Sharing ideas with class colleagues
▪ Initiative and engagement
5 %: EFFORT ABOVE AND BEYOND WHAT IS ANTICIPATED
▪ The individual student grade will consider such factors as: did the student demonstrate
motivation, commitment and collaboration, arrive on time to every class; did the
student demonstrate resourcefulness and creativity in generating ideas and strategies to
pursue the ideas; did the student meet deadlines; did the student demonstrate critical
4

thinking; did the student demonstrate mastery of the subject matter by the end of the
project; did the student effectively participate in class; was; did the student make
effective presentations.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Expectations
The following is an elaboration of what the instructor expects from student teams and how
grades will be determined (note that the first two items are of paramount importance to client
satisfaction and avoiding getting a low grade). Remember, in this course you are each an
ambassador of the Brandeis School of Business and Economics, the client organization you are
representing and yourselves – so always leave favorable impressions.
▪ Error-free work
o facts, figures, and spreadsheets must be error-free. The instructor and - - in
particular - - the host organization must not become the quality control
department. Consistent errors in work will lead to a loss of confidence and a low
grade in the course.
▪ Professional conduct
o You must maintain a high level of professionalism in undertaking your work
including: maintaining client confidentiality; showing up for meetings and
conference calls on time; dressing professionally and appropriately for meetings;
and fulfilling commitments (e.g., deadlines and deliverables).
▪ Self-motivation
o Students should be eager to pursue their role and project work as a matter of
self-interest in helping the organization as well as expanding their own personal
leadership growth. Accordingly, students should be self-motivated to work
throughout both terms pursuing deeper impact.
▪ Independence
o Your ability to work self-sufficiently is critical. To the extent that the scope of
work requires instructor input - - as most do - - then instructor/student
brainstorming is important, with the student then working independently week
to week.
▪ Team orientation
o A collaborative approach is an essential aspect of this class. Students are
expected to collaborate and share learning with one another through
discussions and presentations.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Essential Resources
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations as outlined in an accommodations letter, please talk with me and present
your letter of accommodation as soon as you can. I want to support you. In order to provide
test accommodations, I need the letter more than 48 hours in advance. I want to provide your
accommodations, but cannot do so retroactively. If you have questions about documenting a
5

disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support at
781-736-3470 or access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Course Materials/Readings
Unlike most classes, this class does not have any exams. Therefore, active participation is
required to thrive in our class setting.
Materials recommended for purchase, including textbooks, case packets and online materials.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
The costs of textbooks and other course materials can present financial challenges for many of
our students. Please consider ways you can reduce costs for your students and promote
student success while complying with copyright law. Submit course reserve requests: Course
reserves help ensure that all students have access to materials. If you have extra copies of any
course texts, we encourage you to place these on reserve at the library for the semester.
Explore open educational resources and affordable materials with a librarian: OER are learning
resources such as articles, books, audio recordings, images, video, software and digital tools
that instructors can reuse, revise and redistribute. Contact Laura Laura Hibbler associate
university librarian for research and instruction, to discuss possibilities for reducing the expense
of course materials for your students through the use of OER and materials available through
library subscriptions. You are also strongly encouraged to add this sentence to your syllabus: "If
you are having difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your
student financial services or academic services adviser to discuss possible funding options
and/or textbook alternatives.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Privacy
This class requires the use of tools that may disclose your coursework and identity to parties
outside the class. To protect your privacy, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias rather
than your name in submitting such work. You must share the pseudonym/alias with me and any
teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may submit such work
directly to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
AI use in class
Because this class is about innovation, I think it is key to leverage AI in meaningful ways. This
will be the future of work, and I think it is essential that you develop the skills to set yourself
apart from your peers by leveraging emerging technologies. However, for writing assignments
where you are asked to write in your own words, such as weekly updates, reflections, and
reports it is essential that you write it yourself and not use an AI to write it. You can use AI as a
sounding board, or as a critic to review and improve your writing - as long as you do the
critical thinking first. For example, It is okay if you send your writing to an AI and ask it
something like: “Can you critique how my writing reads to a non subject matter expert?” or
“What questions should I be considering that I have not covered on this topic?” - that is
appropriate use of AI. As long as it is your voice and not a copy/paste of AI content, it can be a
useful research tool. This is the essential skill we want to encourage and develop - but for this
skill to work, you have to do the critical thinking first.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Required Readings / Optional Readings
All required readings will be posted on Moodle at the beginning of each week. There may also
be required readings and research associated with each host assignment.
Apps or Tools/Equipment
All resources and links can be found on Moodle.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: LATTE\nLATTE is the Brandeis learning management system: https://moodle.brandeis.edu/. Login\nusing your UNET ID and password.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Library
The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis
students, faculty and staff. These include workshops, consultations, collaboration, materials and
instruction on emerging trends in technologies such as machine learning, emerging trends in
research such as data visualization, and emerging trends in scholarship such as open access.
Students will undertake their own research to support their individual work and will spend time
ideating on how to tackle challenges in an entrepreneurial fashion. Librarians at the Circulation
Desk, Research Help Desk, Archives & Special Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios,
MakerLab, AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab are available to help you.
https://www.brandeis.edu/library/about/index.html.
Student Support
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. The
following resources are available to help with the many academic and non-academic factors
that contribute to student success (finances, health, food supply, housing, mental health
counseling, academic advising, physical and social activities, etc.). Please explore the many
links on this Support at Brandeis page
(https://www.brandeis.edu/support/undergraduate-students/browse.html) to find out more
about the resources that Brandeis provides to help you and your classmates to achieve success.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Teaching Continuity
To maintain teaching/learning continuity in the case of campus closures, long-term absences,
or other disruptions, all updates will be sent via Moodle or email. To learn more about our
Teaching Continuity, please feel free to access this link.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
All students in this class are required to observe the university's policies on physical distancing
and mask-wearing to support the health and safety of all classroom participants. Face
coverings must be worn by all students and instructors in classes with in-person meetings.
Students and faculty must also maintain the appropriate 6 feet of physical distance from one
another when entering, exiting, or being in the classroom and continue to sit in seats assigned
by the professor to assist the university in its contract-tracing efforts. All faculty and
students must also clean their work areas before and after each class session, using the
sanitizing wipes provided by the University. (All classrooms will also be professionally cleaned
by Brandeis custodial staff multiple times per day.)
spans.label: CONDUCT

doc_id: 242ENG 149A 1 Screenwriting Workshop Writing the Streaming Series
course_name: 242ENG 149A 1 Screenwriting Workshop Writing the Streaming Series

spans.text:
Contact Details
Professor Marc Weinberg
marcw@brandeis.edu
Office Hours: Please contact me by email to set up an office meeting.
Meeting Locations & Times
Shiffman 125 or Zoom (https://brandeis.zoom.us/j/96989237974)
Mondays, Tuesdays, & Thursdays 11:10 AM-1:40 PM EST
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description
Skills and knowledge
Combining lectures, workshops, and screenings, this course examines the fundamentals of writing for
situation comedy and episodic drama. The course will examine story structure, characterization, scene
writing, and dialogue. Students will move from ideation to writing a detailed outline and the first draft
of an original pilot script.
This class is both workshop and lecture. You will read scripts from existing television series, pitch ideas
for pilots, and write frequently. Assignments will be reviewed in class, meaning you will receive
criticism on your pages, as well as critique classmates' work.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals
This course is designed to provide you with the tools necessary to develop and write your television
pilot. By the end of the class, you will be able to:
1. Display an understanding of effective pitching skills.
2. Create a 3-7 act beat sheet for your entire teleplay.
3. Write a complete teleplay for an original pilot.
4. Develop a teleplay that includes the following key elements:
• The three-act structure
• Character arc and story arc
• Visual storytelling
• Beat outline
• Script format
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Class preparation time\nOn average, you can expect to spend 4-6 hours a week preparing for class. This may be through writing\nscript pages, reading the textbook and additional readings, or watching assigned television programs.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisites\nWhile there is no prerequisite for the class, students are expected to have basic grammatical skills.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own
effort. Infringement of academic honesty by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which
may include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions (see section 20 of R&R). Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all
policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work to
TurnItIn.com software to verify originality.
A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic honesty as they apply to a specific course
or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or assignment before
submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the Department
of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be found at Brandeis
Library Guides - Citing Sources (https://guides.library.brandeis.edu/c.php?g=301723).
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Assignments\nAll written assignments should be converted into PDF format and emailed to the class by the due\ndate.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Participation
• You must keep up with all readings and assigned viewings.
• All assignments, whether they are graded or not, are mandatory and are expected on time. To
prevent lost pages and avoid late submissions, please save your work to the cloud each time
you finish writing. Computer malfunction is NOT an excuse for a late submission.
• All homework must be typed and include your name, the date, and the assignment title.
• All work must be proofread for grammar, spelling errors and typos. Your work will be graded
down for mistakes.
• Students are allowed two unexcused absences. A third unexcused absence will result in no
credit for the class.
• Students joining class more than 10 minutes late are considered tardy; two tardies count as one
unexcused absence.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Attendance and Participation
Class meets three times weekly (Mondays, Tuesdays & Thursdays, 11:10 AM – 1:40 PM EST). The
expectation is that you will participate in all class meetings. If you cannot take part in a meeting
because of extenuating circumstances, please contact me by email. You will still be expected to meet
course requirements (i.e. submitting pages and writing notes of your classmates' pages).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Late Work Policy
Please communicate with me in a reasonable amount of time (preferably 24 hours before an
assignment due) if you'd like to request an extension on a specific assignment. Extensions will be
permitted on a case-by-case basis.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Communication Policy
Email is an appropriate and preferred method for official communication. If I need to contact you
outside of class, I will use your Brandeis email address, and expect that you will read and respond to
this communication in a timely manner. I will attempt to respond to students within 24 hours. If you
have not received a reply from me within 24 hours, please resend the email.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Portfolio
Throughout the semester, you will be expected to complete writing assignments, designed to show
your growing expertise in dramatic writing and screenplay format. In some instances, assignments will
be assigned, completed, and discussed, all in the same class meeting. Other times, you will have
anywhere from 1 to 3 weeks to complete a more challenging writing assignment. These scripts will be
emailed to me and your classmates by a predetermined due date.
spans.label: ASSIGN

as outlined in an accommodations letter, I want to support you. In order to provide test

of requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support (SAS Student Accessibility

textbook alternatives.

emergencyfunds@brandeis.edu

Apps or Tools/Equipment
You are required to use a screenwriting program for this class. The preferred program is Final Draft, which
you can download and use this semester for free at (https://www.finaldraft.com/edu-trial/).
You may also use another free scriptwriting program, Celtx (http://www.celtx.com).
Do not use a standard word processing software, such as Microsoft Word, to write scripts.

spans.text:
Library
The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis students,
faculty and staff. These include workshops, consultations, collaboration, materials and instruction on
emerging trends in technologies such as machine learning, emerging trends in research such as data
visualization, and emerging trends in scholarship such as open access. Librarians at the Circulation
Desk, Research Help Desk, Archives & Special Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios, MakerLab,
AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab are available to help you.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Privacy
This class requires the use of tools that may disclose your coursework and identity to parties outside
the class. Should you choose to use the screenwriting program, Celtx, your classwork will be stored
online. To protect your privacy, you may wish to use a pseudonym/alias rather than your name in
submitting such work. You must share the pseudonym/alias with me and your classmates as needed.
Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may submit such work directly to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Class sessions may be recorded for educational purposes. You may decline to be recorded; if so, please
contact me to identify suitable alternatives for class participation. These recordings will be deleted
within two months after the end of the semester. If you can be personally identified in a recording, no
other use is permitted without your formal permission. You may not record classes on your own
without my express permission, and may not share the URL and/or password to anyone unaffiliated
with this course.

Your behavior in these recordings, and in this class as a whole, must fulfill Brandeis
standards:
Brandeis University is committed to providing its students, faculty and staff with an environment
conducive to learning and working, where all people are treated with respect and dignity. You must
refrain from any behavior toward members of our Brandeis community, including students, faculty,

Student Support
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. The following
resources are available to help with the many academic and non-academic factors that contribute to
student success (finances, health, food supply, housing, mental health counseling, academic advising,
physical and social activities, etc.). Please explore the many links on this Support at Brandeis page

resources that Brandeis provides to help you and your classmates to achieve success.

Evaluation & Course grading
Evaluation
• This class, as well as all creative writing courses at Brandeis, are offered exclusively on a
credit/non-credit basis.
• All students will be evaluated according to the same percentages and criteria.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
• You must keep up with all readings and assigned viewings.
• All assignments, whether they are graded or not, are mandatory and are expected on time. To
prevent lost pages and avoid late submissions, please save your work to the cloud each time
you finish writing. Computer malfunction is NOT an excuse for late submission.
• All homework must be typed and include your name, the date, and the assignment title.
• All work must be proofread for grammar, spelling errors and typos. Your work will be graded
down for mistakes.

• Students are allowed one unexcused absences. A second unexcused absence will result in no
credit for the class.
• Students joining class more than 10 minutes late are considered tardy; two tardies count as
one unexcused absence.

spans.text:
South Asian Ways of Watching: Films & Graphic Narratives
Instructor: Sahid Mondal (English)
Spring 2026/ Tuesday-Thursday 2:20 PM -3:40 PM/ Shiffman: 120
Office: 143 Rabb
Office hours: Monday/Thursday - 4 PM – 5 PM/By Appointment
Appointment Link: calendly.com/sahidmondal/book

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Fulfills the following requirements: [ djw hum ] [ENG Post-1800, ENG Multicultural\nLiterature, World Anglophone, ENG Media Film, SAS Elective]
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Prerequisites: None
Credit Hours: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will
spend a minimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class (reading and
watching texts, discussions, preparation for exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Communications: Any changes to the syllabus or class schedule, including accommodations for
weather or developments related to COVID-19, will be communicated via the class email list and
posted on Moodle. Please plan to check your Brandeis email account and Moodle daily to stay
informed. Please contact me by email or come to office hours (virtually or in person) to discuss
any additional questions and concerns. On weekdays I check email periodically between the
hours of 10 a.m. and 5 p.m.; I make every effort to respond to a message within 24 hours. On
weekends, I check my email once or twice a day, but I may go a day without checking. Please
plan accordingly.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
How do we watch South Asia and how does South Asia watch itself? This course invites you into
a vivid journey through film and graphic storytelling. Each week, we’ll pair a film with a graphic
narrative, crafting unexpected conversations across mediums. We’ll trace national borders and
fractures, negotiations of caste and care, love and sex, displacement and diaspora, ecological
entanglements, and the defiance of protest movements in South Asia to watch how South Asia
imagines future for itself.
What happens when the same story of love or protest is told on a movie screen versus across the
panels of a graphic narrative? How do South Asian artists use images, whether moving or drawn,
to make us feel the weight of Partition, caste, war, or ecological collapse? Can watching a rap
battle in Mumbai or reading about women’s sit-in protest in Delhi change how we imagine the
future? This class will hold lively discussions and comparative analysis along with creative
opportunities to experiment with your own visual storytelling. By the end of the semester, you’ll
have sharpened your visual literacy and gained fresh insights into how visual stories themselves
make South Asian worlds possible.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Intellectual & Learning Goals:
1. Develop critical tools to watch and read films and graphic narratives as languages
forming the visual economy of South Asia.
2. Examine how rhythm, silence, framing, and color create meaning across graphic
narratives and films.
3. Explore how histories, caste, gender, ecology, diaspora, and protest have been visually
imagined and contested.
4. Put film and graphic narrative into conversation to uncover what each makes visible or
leaves unseen.
5. Compare not only medium, but how artists from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka,
and the diaspora “watch” their worlds differently.
6. Work critically and creatively by pairing traditional analysis with opportunities to
storyboard, sketch, record or remix scenes yourself.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Terminology: In this course, we will encounter and use a range of terms related to South Asia,
including—but not limited to—“South Asian,” “Indian,” “Pakistani,” “Bangladeshi,” “Sri
Lankan,” “diaspora,” “Muslim,” “Hindu,” “Dalit,” “Adivasi,” “caste,” “tribe,” and “Indigenous.”
We will also engage with regionally specific, linguistic, and political identifiers as they appear in
films, graphic narratives, and historical contexts. Racial slurs, including the N-word, may appear
in course materials for critical analysis. They will be discussed with care and context and not used
casually or repeated aloud.
Each of these terms has a particular history and set of political, cultural, and affective meanings.
While some terms are sometimes used interchangeably in everyday speech, their implications
shift depending on context, medium, and speaker. In this class, we will use these terms carefully
and critically, attending to how they are mobilized within visual texts and how they have been
shaped by colonial histories, nationalist projects, caste hierarchies, religious majoritarianism, and
global migration.
In particular, the term “Indian” may refer to citizenship within the nation-state of India, to
cultural or diasporic affiliation, or to a historical category produced through colonial rule. We will
be attentive to these distinctions. When necessary, we will pause to clarify how a term is being
used, by whom, and to what effect. This course encourages students to ask what images show
and what language accompanies them, and how naming, labeling, and categorization shape
visibility, belonging, and power. Respectful, thoughtful engagement with terminology is an
essential part of our shared learning environment.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Materials:
The following graphic narrative texts (not films) are available for purchase at the Brandeis
University Bookstore. I have also attached links to some vendors on the Moodle page. The films
will be available on the course page on Moodle under Course Videos. If you are having difficulty
purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your student financial services or
academic services advisor to discuss possible funding options and/or textbook alternatives.
Please connect with me if nothing works.
Films
1. Supermen of Malegaon (2008) – Faiza Ahmad Khan
2. Garm Hava (1974) – M.S. Sathyu
3. Joyland (2022) – Saim Sadiq
4. The Big Sick (2017) – Michael Showalter
5. All That Breathes (2022) – Shaunak Sen
6. Gully Boy (2019) – Zoya Akhtar
Graphic Narratives
1. This Side That Side (2013) – Vishwajyoti Ghosh
2. Bhimayana (2011) – Durgabai Vyam, Subhash Vyam, Srividya Natarajan, S. Anand
3. Vanni: A Family’s Struggle Through the Sri Lankan Conflict (2019) – Benjamin Dix &
Lindsay Pollock
4. All Quiet in Vikaspuri (2015) – Sarnath Banerjee
5. Shaheen Bagh: A Graphic Recollection (2021) – Ita Mehrotra
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
This course is structured around 10 films and graphic narratives, with no secondary reading. The
bulk of the material will unfold through class discussions and collaborative visual analyses. Since
the course develops cumulatively, attendance is essential for your own understanding and for
your peers’ collective experience. Four (4) unexcused absences will result in failure of the course.
If you miss more than three classes, you must meet with the instructor to discuss how to
demonstrate continued engagement (e.g., through extra work).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: 1. Attendance (15 points) - Ongoing
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: 2. Participation (15 points) - Ongoing
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Participation includes attentive and engaged presence, active contribution to discussions,
engagement in class activities, and peer feedback. We will hold small-group discussions to
encourage in-depth dialogue. You are also encouraged to visit office hours to discuss the
material, clarify ideas, or brainstorm project topics. If you are hesitant to speak in class, visiting
office hours is an alternate way to demonstrate engagement.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: 3. Zine Club Collective Sketch Activity (10 points) – Feb 10
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This collaborative creative session will take place during one of our class meetings, in partnership
with Brandeis Zine Club. Together, we will complete a guided visual prompt linked to course
themes. In a circle, each participant begins with a sketch in response to the prompt; drawings are
then passed along for others to add text (captions or dialogue/narrative commentary) to create a
short, collectively authored zine. Through this process, we will practice peer-to-peer creativity
and collective storytelling, and core skills for reading and making visual media.
To receive full credit, you must:
1. Be present during the Zine Club session.
2. Participate fully in the collaborative prompt activity.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 4. In-Class Visual Workshop (10 points) – Mar 5
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
We will participate in an in-class visual workshop for this assignment. We will explore a text (of
your choice) from the course through a short creative reflection.
For this workshop we will try to create one of the following forms:
A. A short comic or graphic sketch (minimum four panels, with text integrated as captions
or speech bubbles).
B. A photo collage or visual assemblage (minimum four elements such as photos, sketches,
or drawings).
C. A single image or drawing with a 150–200-word reflective paragraph connecting it to a
film or graphic narrative from the course.
The produced creative material must:
1. Explicitly refer or connect to a course text.
2. Demonstrate thoughtful engagement with form and theme.
At the end of the workshop, you will upload the produced visual material to Moodle under the
Assignments tab by Mar 7. You can revise the work you create in the class before the
submission. Your classmates may view and respond to your posts, but responses are optional
and not graded—but highly encouraged.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 5. Prospectus Roundtable & Submission (5+10 points) – Mar 26
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
On March 26, each student will come with a prepared 500-word prospectus for the final
project. This short piece should outline your final project idea [SEE THE NEXT
ASSIGNMENT]—how you plan to analyze, reimagine, or juxtapose two visual forms (e.g., a

film scene and a graphic panel). You will be divided into groups to share your ideas with your
peers. You can incorporate any feedback you receive from your peers before you submit the
prospectus on March 27 on Moodle under Assignments tab. You will receive feedback
from the instructor by March 30.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 6. Final Project Presentation/Exhibition (Draft) & Submission (10+20 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Your final project extends your creative and analytical work into a sustained exploration of visual
form, affect, and media. You will choose one format from the list below and develop it in
conversation with the instructor. Each project must engage at least one film and one graphic
narrative – (one of those (either the film or the graphic text) must be from the course), and it
should build on insights developed through your visual workshop and activities and the
prospectus.
Possible formats include:
1. A Graphic Narrative – Extend your workshopped visual material into a longer or more
complex sequence – at least 4 pages in A5 size (Dividing an A4 paper in half results in
two smaller sheets of the A5 size). The length, scale, and medium (digital or hand-drawn)
can be discussed during office hours.
2. A Short Video Film – Create a 3–5-minute short that reimagines or responds to a
course text and interacts with a text out of the syllabus. This may remix existing footage,
including original material, or combine both.
3. A Collage of Graphic Narratives and Film Stills – Combine drawn panels, film stills,
and/or text to trace a comparative visual argument – one of the texts must be from out
of the syllabus. We will discuss the balance of components during office hours.
4. A Photo Series – Develop a photographic sequence (6–10 images) that stages, mirrors,
or challenges a scene, motif, or affect from the course. The curatorial statement must be
2-page long.
5. An Affective Audio Analysis – Produce a 5-7-minute sound essay that interprets a
scene or visual work through its emotional and sensory registers. May include voice-over,
ambient sound, or archival audio.
6. A Video Presentation – A 5–7-minute multimedia presentation that brings together
clips, stills, and commentary to perform your comparative analysis of a film and a graphic
narrative.
All final projects must:
• Incorporate a minimum of one film and one graphic narrative. At least one of these texts
must come from the course syllabus, while the other may be chosen independently or
during office hours.
• Demonstrate awareness of form, composition, rhythm, and affect.
• Be accompanied by a brief Curatorial Statement (500 - 1000 words) explaining your
concept, choices, and critical insights.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
A NOTE ON THE PRESENTATION –
We will present or exhibit the final projects on Apr 21 and Apr 23 (Th) – We will discuss
the possible presentation/exhibition format during your one-on-one meeting, and the
presentation duration will be decided after the first two weeks of the classAssignment

spans.text: Late policy: Late assignments will be docked unless you make prior arrangements with me.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Academic Integrity: You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. This includes
avoiding plagiarism, which is the use of someone else’s ideas or words in your writing without
acknowledging the source. Students may be required to submit work to plagiarism detection
software to verify originality. The use of AI is not permitted in this course for all the

assignments; if I suspect that any part of an assignment was composed using AI, I will ask you to
verify the work as your own; please document all stages of your research and writing process
(including notes, outlines, and drafts). If you cannot demonstrate that it is your own work, you
will not receive credit for the assignment. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Cases of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the director of academic integrity. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. A guide
to Citing Sources can be found on the Brandeis Library website. Please see me if you have
additional questions about proper attribution and citation of sources.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Writing Center: Students who want additional help with their writing and assignments are\nencouraged to contact the University Writing Center, Goldfarb Mezzanine 232 at 781-736-4885\nor writingcenter@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Content Note: Some course materials include depictions or discussions of suicide, sexual
violence, bodily harm, caste and religious violence, war, displacement, environmental harm, and
political protest. These topics are approached analytically and with care. Students are encouraged
to reach out to the instructor if they anticipate needing support or accommodations related to
particular materials.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Electronic devices: Computers and tablet devices are allowed during class for taking and
reviewing notes only. Please refrain from checking email and social media or Internet browsing

during class time (except as part of structured class activities). Cell phone use during class is
prohibited.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Recording Policy: To support open discussion and creative risk-taking, audio and video
recording of class sessions is not allowed without my prior permission. This includes lectures,
screenings, discussions, workshops, and peer presentations. Approved recordings are for
individual study only and may not be shared.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: Health and Safety Standards: All students in this class are required to observe the university’s\npolicies on physical distancing and mask-wearing to support the health and safety of all\nclassroom participants.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
In the event that you are required to quarantine or isolate, or if in-person classes are
suspended due to COVID-19 or to severe weather, you will need a functioning, Internet-enabled
personal computer or tablet that is equipped with Zoom, and a reliable wi-fi connection. For
technical assistance, please contact the Technology Help Desk (help@brandeis.edu; 781-736-
4357). Undergraduates with financial need should contact Student Financial Services
(sfs@brandeis.edu; 781-736-3700) to discuss options available to purchase equipment and other
technology and supply needs. (You may also want to refer to the list of resources for Emergency
Funding in the Office of Student Financial Services.)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Emergency Closures: In case of inclement weather or other emergencies that require campus
to be closed, class will be held on Zoom. Students who are unable to attend these sessions will
be allowed to attend class asynchronously (e.g., watching a recording of the class and submitting
a written response to the issues discussed there) without penalty.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Disabilities: Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of
all students, and I want to support you in your learning. If you have questions about
documenting a disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility
Support (SAS) at 781-736-3537 or access@brandeis.edu. You can find helpful student FAQs and
other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be
eligible for support from SAS. If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please
provide me with a copy as soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of
accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you should
provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an exam. Live auto transcription is
available for all meetings or classes hosted on Zoom; you can turn it on or off to support your
learning. Please check for Zoom updates to take advantage of this new feature. To learn more,
visit the Zoom Live Transcription webpage. For questions, contact help@brandeis.edu.

course_name: 261BIOL 51A 1 Biostatistics Kene Piasta

spans.text:
BIOL51b, Spring 2026
Biostatistics
Contact Detail
Kene N. Piasta Office location: Shapiro Science Center, 0-16E
Email: kpiasta@brandeis.edu Telephone: (781) 736-3159
TAs:
• Ava Towle - avatowle@brandeis.edu
• Adrienne Hwang - adriennehwang@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Communication Policy
• All course communication will take place through Moodle and your official Brandeis email address.
Messages from personal email accounts, social media, or other channels will not be answered.
• Office Hours & Appointments: Bring questions to office hours or schedule an appointment at least
72 hours in advance. Unannounced drop-ins or contact via personal channels (cell phone, home
address, etc.) are inappropriate and will not be tolerated.
• Response Time: Email is the most reliable method of contact. Allow at least 48 hours for a reply,
with longer delays over weekends and holidays.
• Absences: If you expect to miss more than one class or lab, notify the instructor as soon as possible.
• Announcements: All course updates will be posted on Moodle — check it regularly.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Meeting Times/Locations
• Classes
o Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday, 10:10 AM to 11:00 AM, Gzang 124
• Recitation
o Wednesday, 5:40 PM to 7:00 PM, Gzang 124
• Student Hours (Office Hours)
o Dr. Piasta: TBD and by appointment.
o TAs will announce their office hours at the start of the class and they will be posted on
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Prerequisite(s):
• BIOL14a
• BIOL15b
• Mathematics using algebra will be used regularly.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
By the end of this course students should be able to:
• Identify different data types and their usefulness
• Learn common statistical notation
• Interpret common statistical tests and results
• Develop statistical intuition regarding use of statistical tests
• Utilize statistics to solve statistical problems in biology by hand
• Learn the basic use of the R statistics package
• Acquire the ability to use R to solve statistical problems computationally
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Success in this course will provide essential statistics skills that can be utilized in performing your own\nresearch in a laboratory on or off campus and critical thinking skills that allow you to develop your own\nworking models to explain experimental data.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Attendance and Participation
• Lecture Attendance: You must attend the three weekly lectures. These sessions review topics,
introduce new concepts, cover announcements, and summarize the week’s content. You are
responsible for knowing all material covered.
• Recitation Attendance: You must attend the recitation for which you are registered: there are no
make-up recitations nor the assignments that occur during recitations. If you have a legitimate
conflict, email Dr. P before the recitation begins (if possible). A missed lab for any reason will
require you to use your drop of the lowest Recitation Assignment or use the Assessment Policy
specified below.
• Active Engagement: Science is best learned by actively listening, taking notes, and following
explanations in real time. Because much of the material builds on equations, diagrams, and in-class
discussion, recordings will not be provided except for students with official accommodations. This
approach helps you develop the skills needed to follow and apply scientific reasoning—essential for
success in future classes, research, and professional work.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Device and Note-taking Policy
• Research shows that access to cell phones and laptops in class reduces focus and learning—not
only for the user but also for those nearby. For this reason, all cell phones and laptops must be
turned off and stored during lecture.
• Evidence also indicates that handwritten notes improve learning and understanding compared to
typed notes. Students are expected to take notes by hand (pen or pencil). Tablets may be used
only for handwritten notes, with cellular and Wi-Fi disabled. If tablet use becomes distracting to
others, you may be asked to switch to paper.
• Students with an approved accommodation for laptop note-taking must meet with the professor
before the first lecture.
• Computers/Tablets in Recitation: During specific recitations, you will need to bring a computer. Use
of computers/tablets during all other recitations is prohibited unless for a specific accommodation.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
AI Policy
• AI and generative AI can be valuable tools in science, but they cannot replace your own
understanding—you must be able to recognize when these tools are incorrect.
• AI-generated content is not your own work and must always be cited if used.
• If you use AI in any submitted assignment, you must also submit the AI output along with your
work. Detailed instructions will be provided.
• For studying or unsubmitted work, you may use AI, but you are strongly encouraged to verify all
information to avoid learning incorrect material.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Coursework Expectations
• Moodle Announcements: Check the BIOL51 Moodle site regularly for announcements, syllabus
updates, and important information.
• Questions on Grading:
o I understand that you care deeply about your academic performance; however, engaging
with assignment feedback immediately upon release is crucial for your continued learning
in this course.
o For this reason, all regrade requests must be submitted within 7 days of the grade
release. After this 7-day window, all grades are considered final.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Matters to Address with Dr. P Directly
Do not bring these concerns to your TAs:
• Regrade requests for any assignment
• Missing lecture or recitation for any reason
• Requesting extensions on assignments
• Any other matter affecting your ability to complete your work
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Materials/Books/Apps/Equipment
If you are having difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your Student
Financial Services or Academic Services advisor to discuss possible funding options, including vouchers for
purchases made at the Brandeis Bookstore.
Software utilized is all freely available:
● Posit Cloud (web-based RStudio): You will need to sign up for a FREE account.
o https://posit.cloud/plans/free
o DO NOT PAY FOR an ACCOUNT!
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Credit Hours:
Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of nine
hours of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion sections, preparation
for the exams, etc. As this course is a laboratory, coming to lab outside of Registrar scheduled time is
required.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Due Date and Time Policy
• All assignments have fixed, nonnegotiable due dates designed to keep you on track and to allow
TAs time to provide timely feedback.
• Assignment schedule:
o Lecture Attendance: Mondays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays anytime between 10:10 AM
and 11:00 AM in-person during lecture.
o Recitation Assignments: Wednesday, 7:10 PM
o Assessments during Recitation: Wednesdays, 7:00 PM
o Weekly Lecture Reflections: Fridays, 11:59 PM
• Submitting on Gradescope: Submit well before the deadline. If Gradescope marks your work late,
it is late — no grace period. Avoid technical issues by submitting early; if needed, contact LTS
(help@brandeis.edu) before the deadline. Make sure to assign answers to questions correctly
and/or upload your pages in the correct order.
• Office hours: Scheduled to align with assignment deadlines. Extensions granted by Dr. P do not
guarantee extra office hours outside the regular schedule. Students with accommodation-based
extensions should attend regular office hours to stay on track.
• Drops: If you use your drop(s) for an assignment type early in the semester, you will not receive
additional drops for that type.
• Assessments during Recitation: Do not make travel arrangements that conflict with assessment
dates. There are no makeup assessments.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Attendance
• Attendance will be taken by filling out a form handed out at any point during lecture.
o If you are not present when the form is handed out, you cannot earn credit.
• You must hand the form to Dr. P at the end of the lecture.
o Forms will NOT be accepted from other students. This means each individual student
must hand their form to Dr. P. No exceptions will be made.
• Your grade will be calculated out of a total of 36 lectures. This means that you can miss up to 3
lectures before it will affect your grade.
• Notify Dr. P within the first two weeks of the semester about any conflicts due to religious
observance. Only the dates of the religious holidays themselves are excused; travel days are not
excused.
o Full Brandeis policy on religious observance: Registrar’s Religious Policy.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Weekly Lecture Reflections
• Due Fridays by 11:59 PM EST via Gradescope.
o Are available after the second lecture of the week occurs.
• Grading: each question is graded for effort and accuracy.
o No credit will be earned for any question that is not answered in full.
o Full credit will not be earned for any question that is lacking effort.
• Late reflections are not accepted.
• Lowest reflection grade dropped.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Recitation Assignments
• Due on Wednesdays at the end of Recitation, 7:00 PM, as specified on the Course Calendar below.
• You must upload assignments to Gradescope.
o You may need to assign your work to the correct question using Gradescope
o You may need to upload your assignment in the correct order using Gradescope.
o I highly recommend using the official Gradescope Moblie App.
§ https://guides.gradescope.com/hc/en-us/articles/22016028459789-Using-the-
Gradescope-Mobile-App-for-Students
• You may need to bring a computer/tablet to complete the assignment and if you do not have a
device to complete the assignment you cannot earn credit.
• On the computer/tablet you will need a Posit Cloud (web-based RStudio) account and ability to
use the website.
o https://posit.cloud/plans/free
• Each question is graded as credit/no credit for completion.
• Late Recitation Assignments are not accepted.
• Lowest Recitation Assignment grade dropped.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Assessments during Recitation
• Assessments are held on Wednesdays during Recitation.
o If you arrive late, you only have the remaining time to complete the assessment.
• Each assessment consists of two distinct attempts with different rules for each:
o Phase 1: The Initial Attempt
§ Materials: You may use one official notecard (provided by Dr. P during lecture).
You may handwrite anything on both sides.
§ Submission: You must turn in the assessment and your notecard to the TAs.
§ Feedback: By Monday evening, you will receive your score for each question (e.g.,
"1/2 on Question 1") and a blank copy of the test. Detailed feedback on specific
Page 4 of 8

BIOL51b, Spring 2026
parts of questions will not be provided; it is your responsibility to identify your
errors.
§ You MUST take the initial attempt in order to be eligible to take the second
attempt.
§ If you miss the first attempt for a university sanctioned reason, you must
complete an oral version with Dr. P before the recitation during which the
second attempt will occur.
o Phase 2: The Second Attempt (optional)
§ Materials: No outside materials permitted. (No notecard, no notes).
§ Requirement: You must retake the entire assessment, even questions you
answered correctly the first time.
§ You do not need to attempt the assessment for a second time. If you do
not, your first attempt will be used as the final grade.
§ If you miss the second attempt for a university sanctioned reason, you
must complete it by scheduling a time with Dr. P before the end of that
week.
§ Preparation: Use the time between Monday and Wednesday to study and
determine the correct solutions to all problems.
§ I strongly suggest utilizing office hours!
§ You can and should work together, but understanding why the answer is
correct will make taking the assessment again much easier than trying to
memorize the answers.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
o Grading Formula
§ Your final grade is a weighted average that rewards improvement while
acknowledging the benefit of seeing the questions beforehand.
§ This means your final grade will almost always be lower than your second
attempt grade except for very unique circumstances.
§ 𝐹𝑖𝑛𝑎𝑙 𝐺𝑟𝑎𝑑𝑒 = (𝐴𝑡𝑡𝑒𝑚𝑝𝑡 1 × 0.4)+(𝐴𝑡𝑡𝑒𝑚𝑝𝑡 2×0.6)
Attempt Score Weight Contribution
First Attempt 70% 0.4 28%
Second Attempt 90% 0.6 54%
Final Grade 82%

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I
want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations, you will
need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3537, email them
at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful
student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you
might be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can
so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate
exam accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an
exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which may
include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures
related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work via Compilatio or similar software
to verify originality. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they apply to a
specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or
assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to
the Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be
found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.
All written work and assignments must be completed independently unless specifically told otherwise. It
is acceptable to use the words or ideas of another person, provided the source is properly
acknowledged. You must use proper citations and quotation marks to indicate the source of any phrases,
sentences, paragraphs or ideas found in published volumes, on the internet, or created by another
student. Note that the use of full quotations is prohibited. You must cite using the specific format
provided on Moodle.
All assignments submitted are checked with Compilatio software. Any assignment that is flagged as
having a high similarity or identity to any other work, whether from another Brandeis student or from
another source, that is not cited will be submitted to the Office of Student Rights and Community
Standards.
If any assignment is thought to be produced by any person or any software other than the student which
submitted, an in-person meeting with the faculty meeting will be scheduled. During this meeting an oral
explanation of the assignment, the thought process behind the assignment, and the ideas within the
assignment will be assessed. If a student is unable to orally defend the assignment, the assignment and
student will be reported to the Office of Student Rights and Community Standards.
Any assignment found to be plagiarized will be an undroppable zero.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Classroom Health and Safety
• Register for the Brandeis Emergency Notification System. Students who receive an emergency
notification while attending class should notify their instructor immediately. In the case of a life-
threatening emergency, call 911. As a precaution, review this active shooter information sheet.
• Brandeis provides this shuttle service for traveling across campus or to downtown Waltham,
Cambridge and Boston.
• Brandeis University is committed to providing its students, faculty and staff with an environment
conducive to learning and working, where all people are treated with respect and dignity. Please
refrain from any behavior toward members of our Brandeis community, including students, faculty,
staff, and guests, that intimidates, threatens, harasses, or bullies. Please consult Brandeis University
Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and practices related to respectful environment.

The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis students, faculty
and staff. Librarians and Specialists from Research & Instructional Services, Public Services, Archives & Special
Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios, MakerLab, AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab are
available to help you through consultations and workshops.

Privacy
To protect your privacy in any case where this course involves online student work outside of Brandeis
password-protected spaces, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias. You must share the pseudonym/
alias with me and any teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may
submit such work directly to me.

Class Recordings
Students' presentations will be recorded for grading purposes only and will not be shared. You may
decline to be recorded; if so, please contact me. If you can be personally identified in a recording, no
other use is permitted without your formal permission. You may not record classes on your own without
