doc_id: 253NBIO 145B 1 Circuits and Systems Shantanu Jadhav
course_name: 253NBIO 145B 1 Circuits and Systems Shantanu Jadhav

spans.text:
NBIO 145B: Circuits and Systems Neuroscience
Contact Details
Instructor: Dr. Shantanu Jadhav
Office location: Bassine 302
Telephone: (781) 736-3147
Email: shantanu@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Continuity:
In the event of campus closures (snow days, lockdowns, or other disruptions), we will continue with
remote sessions, or schedule make-up sessions if necessary. Communication about any changes to
class schedule will be via email.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Meeting Times/Locations
Classes
Time: Tuesday/Friday, 9:35 AM - 10:55 AM; Room: Volen Center 106
Labs or sections
None
Student Hours
By appointment
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Course Description\nPrerequisites\n(NBIO 140 or NBIO 240: Principles of Neuroscience) -OR- permission from the instructor.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Overview
This course is an advanced seminar class in which students will explore fundamental questions in
circuit-and systems-level neuroscience. This course will take a systems-level perspective to
explore how the brain supports behavior and cognition. Students will read, discuss, write, and
present on seminal and current findings to understand how the brain represents and perceives the
external environment, adapts and learns, executes control, and uses internal representations to
make decisions and drive behavior. The focus will be on underlying circuits, neural
representations and neurophysiological mechanisms. The course will follow an arc that covers
sensory systems, perception and behavior, neural coding, cognition and motor control. Emphasis
will be on bridging across these levels, and on critical analysis and synthesis. Critical elements of
this course are in-class participation for paper readings, oral presentations, student-led questions
and discussion, and writing assignments.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Attendance is mandatory.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Course Material
• Papers for discussion are listed on the syllabus and are available through the Brandeis
eJournals; students must access reading well ahead of time. Links to some classic papers that
are more difficult to access will be listed on the syllabus and available on Moodle. References

to the primary literature and other sources can be found through PubMed or other database
searches. Changes to reading assignments or timing will be announced in class.
• Optional Text: Principles of Neural Science – Kandel, et al. Note: This textbook is optional for
background reading.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
By the end of this course, students will be able to
• Discuss modern approaches to study systems-level neural functions
• Develop skills necessary to research original literature
• Read and critically analyze and synthesize research papers
• Discuss and argue in a peer review style the content of research papers
• Present and write about papers in a scientific style that summarizes a body of research
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Requirements
This is a discussion class, and lectures will be minimal. Students are expected to refresh their
understanding of key background concepts and material prior to reading papers. The class format
will focus on discussion of key papers from the primary research literature. Reading MUST be
completed prior to class. We will discuss research papers in detail including each figure, the
methods used to generate it, how the data were analyzed and represented, the take-home message
of the figure (and of the study overall at the end), and any weaknesses. There will be two in-class
presentations on assigned topics for each student, and several small writing assignments due
throughout the semester.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Readings and Class Participation
The class format will lean heavily towards discussion of research papers. Each week, a new set of
papers must be reviewed (usually 2 papers per 1 hour class), and students should be prepared to
discuss the assigned papers in class. As a general guideline for the discussion, you need to be
thoughtful, argumentative (but reasonable), ready to discuss multiple points of view, and be able
to critically analyze, synthesize, and draw connections across topics. It is expected that you will
arrive at class fully prepared to discuss the assigned papers; a score will be entered each week
reflecting your involvement in the deliberation. “Extra credit” will be available for those who: 1)
bring relevant outside readings into discussions; or 2) make a substantial intellectual contribution.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Class discussion. For each paper the instructor will assign one student to give an introduction to the
paper (what was known previously, what gap in knowledge the paper attempts to fill), and one
student to discuss the general methods (including species, brain region under study, what the
experimental paradigms and techniques are). After that the instructor will ask for volunteers to
discuss each figure in turn, and/or will call on students. The instructor will endeavor to make sure
that students are given equal opportunity to participate, as this is an important part of the learning
process and participation contributes to the student’s grade. It is highly recommended that students
generate summaries/notes on each paper, including each figure, prior to class to aid in class
discussions. To prepare for discussion I recommend that you first try to get an overall idea of what
the authors were trying to accomplish. The introduction to the paper should set the stage by
describing the important problem they are addressing and what has been done before by other
groups. The next thing to do is to look at the results. Make sure you know the following about
each experimental figure: 1) What was the question the authors were trying to address with that
particular experiment? 2) How was the experiment done? What techniques or analysis methods
were used? For this you will have to refer to the methods section. If you find you do not have

adequate background to understand a figure, review the topic in textbooks, NBIO140/240 notes,
and do the background reading suggested on the syllabus. 3) Also be sure that you know what
exactly is being plotted: what are the axes, what are the units?
Finally, you should develop an opinion about each paper. Did the authors really answer the
question they set out to investigate? Were the approaches used sound? Are there other ways to
address the overarching questions posed? What might the authors do next to delve deeper into the
issues posed? For older landmark papers, what were the technical limitations/knowledge base at
the time and how did that affect their approach and conclusions? Many papers will have a final
model/figure in the discussion that is helpful in making these types of assessments. If not, try
constructing one yourself to aid your understanding.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Attendance and Involvement
This class depends critically on everyone attending and participating, and attendance will be
reflected in your in-class participation grade. If you must miss class for any reason (illness,
religious observance, official Brandeis athletic competition, etc.) please let me know as far in
advance as possible. Students will be allowed a maximum of 3 excused absences without penalty.
Since this is a discussion-based class, active participation is vital. This includes attending class,
completing the assigned readings before attending class, preparing for presentations, asking
questions and participating in class discussions, etc.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Paper Presentations – Oral Communication
Students will give oral presentations using prepared slides (~15-20 minutes) for assigned papers.
Each student will present at least twice during the course. Presentation assignments will be
scheduled at the beginning of the course, and students should expect to present at least once each
in the first and second half of the semester. You should present the paper similar to a research
conference, including the background, central question, brief methods, results, summary and
future directions. The most important figures should be explained, but presenting every figure is
not mandatory unless you think it is necessary. You can use your own computer or the class
computer. Presenters on the same topic are encouraged to discuss their assigned papers and
presentations. Students are expected to submit their presentations, including list of references, to
the instructor after the presentations. Grading for the class presentation will be based on
comprehension of material; clarity, completeness, and accuracy of the presentation; and how well
students can answer questions. Students are responsible for carefully reading all cited sources, and
ensuring that they accurately support the arguments/points being made.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: The class is expected to attend to student presentations (computer screens and phones off), and ask\nquestions at the end during discussion.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Written self-evaluation for presentations
After presentations, you will do a written self-evaluation of the presentation (1-page max limit),
due by noon on the following Tuesday after your presentations. This should provide you with a
chance to reflect on 1) was it an effective presentation; 2) were you able to present an effective
background, explain the results, summarize and interpret, and talk about future directions; and 3)
what you would do differently if you had to do it again. You will receive Instructor feedback and
subsequent presentations will provide an opportunity to implement what you learn from the first
presentation.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Writing Assignments
During some student presentation days, writing assignments will be required. Everyone (except
for the 2 assigned presenters for that class) must read the two assigned papers and turn in a
summary (1 page maximum) addressing the following questions for each paper:
a) take-home message of the paper, b) strength of the paper, c) weakness of the paper, d) questions
you would ask authors if you visited their poster or in a talk.
Format your 1-page-maximum summaries for each assigned paper reasonably. You can submit
either in Word or PDF format. Please add your name to the filename. General instructions: you
can use Normal margins, Arial 11-pt or Times New Roman 12-pt Font (or equivalent), and
preferably single spacing or Multiple at 1.15 lines.
There will also be 2-3 short writing assignments throughout the semester. The purpose of these
assignments is to foster critical and integrative thinking about topics covered by the reading.
Grading will be based on accuracy, synthesis of material, and demonstration of critical thinking.
Submitted text must be original; see below for use of generative AI in your assignments. The
student is responsible for carefully reading all cited sources, and ensuring that they accurately
support the point being made.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Evaluation
• 40% In-class participation and discussion. Command of reading material, preparation,
participation in discussion, and response to questions.
• 40% Presentation of journal articles to the class
• 20% Writing Assignments.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Success in this 4 credit hour course is based on the expectation that students will spend 3 hours per\nweek in class and a minimum of 9 hours of study time per week in preparation for class.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Outcomes:
Students will gain familiarity with many important concepts in circuit/systems neuroscience, and
will learn how to read and critically evaluate papers from the primary scientific literature. Students
will learn how to research a scientific paper that involves summarizing, evaluating, and
synthesizing scientific information from many primary sources.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Important Policies and Resources
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which may
include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures
related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work via TurnItIn.com or similar
software to verify originality. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as
they apply to a specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that
course or assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be
forwarded to the Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research
assistance can be found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Use of generative AI in assignments
AI can be a useful tool when used thoughtfully and carefully. However, generative AI tools such as
ChatGPT in their current instantiations have no accuracy filters, and often generate plausible-sounding
misinformation, including non-existent citations and made-up details. Using these tools as a starting
place to get some ideas flowing is fine as long as these limitations are understood. However, it is not
acceptable to turn in AI-generated or extensively modified text as your own. Importantly, the student is
responsible for vetting all information and citations included in their oral and written work through
standard sources (textbooks, citations from the primary literature), and must cite these sources
appropriately.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Classroom Health and Safety
• Register for the Brandeis Emergency Notification System. Students who receive an emergency
notification while attending class should notify their instructor immediately. In the case of a life-
threatening emergency, call 911. As a precaution, review this active shooter information sheet.
• Brandeis provides this shuttle service for traveling across campus or to downtown Waltham,
Cambridge and Boston.
• If you are unwell/sick, please do not attend class.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I
want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations, you will
need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS) (781-736-3470, access@brandeis.edu). You can
find helpful student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know
whether you might be eligible for support from SAS. If you already have an accommodation letter from
SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of
accommodations for this class, that are consistent with the class structure and learning goals. No
retroactive accommodations will be granted. In order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you
should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text: MOODLE\nMoodle is the Brandeis learning management system. Login using your UNET ID and password. For\nMoodle help, contact Library@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Library
The Brandeis Library collections and staff offer resources and services to support Brandeis students, faculty
and staff. Librarians and Specialists from Research & Instructional Services, Public Services, Archives &
Special Collections, Sound & Image Media Studios, MakerLab, AutomationLab, and Digital Scholarship Lab
are available to help you through consultations and workshops.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Privacy
To protect your privacy in any case where this course involves online student work outside of Brandeis
password-protected spaces, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias. You must share the pseudonym/
alias with me and any teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may
submit such work directly to me.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Student Support
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive. If a student, faculty,
or staff member wants to learn more about support resources, the Support at Brandeis webpage offers a
comprehensive list that includes these staff colleagues you can consult, along with other support
resources:
• The Care Team
• Academic Services (undergraduate)
• Graduate Student Affairs
• Directors of Graduate Studies in each department, School of Arts & Sciences
• Program Administrators for the Heller School and International Business School
• University Ombuds
• Office of Equal Opportunity.

spans.text:
English46b
AmericanGothicandAmericanRomance
M,W2:30PM–3:50PM
GoldingJudaica: 109
JohnBurt
Rabb206(x62158)
burt@brandeis.edu
Officehours: M11W1211andbyappointment
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Gothic fiction, however one may define it, is a minor strain in the literature of Britain, and there
is often found in parodic form, but in the United States gothic fiction is a mainstream genre found
not only in what we slightingly call “genre fiction” but in its most canonical texts, from Poe and
Hawthorne to Toni Morrison and Cormac McCarthy. It is possible to imagine all of the authors
we examine as being in a kind of extended conversation with each other, sharing not only themes
and interests, but also characteristic turns of plot and even characters. In them one sees many of
the features critics have described as distinctive characteristics of American fiction: its interest
in mythic rather than representational characters (and particularly in isoladoes and fanatics), its
fascination with extreme action, especially of a destructive or self-destructive kind, its sense that
the deepest kinds of knowledge are transgressive and revealed only at the boundaries of human
experience, its comparative lack of interest in the problems of love, career, and social life that are
themeatandbreadofBritishfiction,evenitsplainhostilitytoordinaryliving.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Texts
• EdgarAllanPoe,TalesandPoems
• NathanielHawthorne,SelectedStories,TheScarletLetter
• HermanMelville,MobyDick
• HenryJames,TheTurnoftheScrew
• WilliamFaulkner,Sanctuary
• RobertPennWarren,BrothertoDragons
• FlanneryO’Connor,TheViolentBearitAway
• ToniMorrison,Beloved
• CormacMcCarthy,BloodMeridian
Hard copies of these books are available in the bookstore. I have ordered Norton Critical Editions
where possible, both for the quality of the editions, and for all of the supporting material they
provide. But all of the texts through James are in the public domain, and ebooks are probably
availableforfreeontheinternet. Allofthetextsarealsoavailableinpaperback.

and philosophical concepts, such as the romantic concept of imagination, the ethical possi-
bilitiesofinwardnessandautonomy,thetensionbetweeenexplicitandliminalmeanings
• Tounderstandtheplaceofthesefictionsinliteraryhistory,thedevelopmentofthesefictional
traditionsovertime,andthewaysparticularworksturnthetraditionsofgothicandromantic
fictioninnewdirection

gothicandromanticfiction
3

• To see gothic and romantic fictional texts in the historical and political context under which

spans.text:
Requirements
1. Short papers There will be short (two pages or so) writing assignments due every “session
2” for the first six weeks of the term (except the first week). You need only submit five of them,
and you get to pick which week you don’t have to submit. You will pick a passage of about 250
wordsfromthereadingforthatdayorthenextandtypeitout. (Thispassageisn’tincludedinyour
word count. I want you to type the passage out because doing so will bring its particulars more
closelyintoview.) Besuretopickapassagewhichstrikesyouasrichandinterestingandfullofa
significance that might not be already obvious to every reader of that text. In other words, I don’t
want you to pick a passage that will enable you to repeat some point I have already made in the
lecture,orthatyouwereallreadytomakeonsomeotherbasis,butrathersomepassagewhichwill
enableyoutobringanewreflectionintoourconversation,somepassagethatcastssomenewlight
upon the conversation we have already been having, some light that we might not have seen were
itnotforyou. Youwillwriteatwotofourpagecommentary(500to1000words)onthatpassage,
giving what you take its point to be, noting its context, and developing in cogent detail the claim
it leads you to make about the text. You are not merely to give a summary of the passage, but to
point out something important in it that other intelligent, well-informed readers might well have
missed, some unexpected turn of argument, some subtext, some complexity of tone or treatment,
some unanticipated light upon the characters and their situation, some insight into the point of
view of the narrator or the agenda of the novel. Imagine that you are writing for someone who
hassomeknowledgeofthetextbutwhodoesnotknowwhatpreciselyisyourpointofviewabout
it—someone rather like the other members of this class, for instance. I will not give particular
papers letter grades, but I will comment upon them in detail and give them either a check, a check
plus,oracheckminus. Youmayreviseandresubmitthesepapersasoftenasyouwish,butplease
donotmerelyeditincorrectionsoferrors.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
2. OtherpapersOnemediumpapersdealingcloselywithabrieftextorwithanaspectofalonger
one. The first, of 5-6 pp., will be due on Week 8 to your writing group, and on Week 9 to your
instructor. This paper should be an extended exercise in close reading. The second, of 7-9 pp.,
should engage the literary-critical tradition of commentary on the text you discuss. This will be
dueonWeek12toyourwritinggroup,andonthelastdayofclasstome.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Responding to Each Other’s Papers
As you read each other’s papers bear in mind that the point is to help each other to write better
papers. Bear in mind also that as readers you are not all that different from me. We both have
some familiarity with the text, we both know the kinds of things we have talked about in class,
and we both know a thing or two about literary interpretation. We also can recognize that some
“tacks”giveanewtwisttothediscussionswehavealreadybeenhaving,andmaybesuggestsome
discussions we ought to have in the future. It is this sense—of advancing a conversation already
underway—whichyoushouldkeepinmindasyoureadeachother’spapers. Donothesitate,then,
4

to write in the margins—you should, in fact, hold up your end of the conversation there. Note
down where the major claim is made, and what you think it is. Note also where some point is
tellingly made. Pay attention also to those moments where you don’t quite get what the author
is saying. Sometimes these moments happen when the author has left something out that seems
obvious to him or her but not to anyone else. ( If you find such a moment see if you can guess
what’smissing.) Ifyouunderstand,saythatinthemarginstoo–putinthemarginallthe“uhhuh’s”
and “wait a minute’s” and “have you thought of’s” that you would be bursting out with if your
room-mateweretellingyouwhathisorherpaperisabout. Attheendofthepaperyoushouldalso
write a comment. These comments are most useful if they’re not all evaluation. It’s very useful,
for instance, to summarize what you think the paper’s argument is, because sometimes the author
hasn’t made the argument he or she intended to make, and that is something it is important for the
authortoknow. Hereareafewmorequestionstoaskyourselfasyoucomposeyourresponses.
1. What is the author’s major claim? Has the author staked out an argument, or merely an-
nounced a subject matter? Is this a subject matter which will call for the author to read the
text closely, or could someone who has read the text cursorily make the same argument?
Whatmighttheauthorlearnfrommakingthisargument?
2. What does the author need to do in order to substantiate his or her claim? Do these things
get done? Are there counterarguments to be considered, say, or other ways of reading the
passages the author chooses to examine? Has the author chosen the passages he or she cites
merely as evidence of something the author already knows, or do the passages themselves
providerichsourcesofinformation?
3. How does the author’s style strike you? Are there gaps in the presentation? Does the author
talk “up” to you, or “down” to you? Does this author have an idea of what it’s like to read
thispaper?
4. Istheauthor’sconclusionmerelyarestatementoftheoriginalclaim,ordoestheauthorclose
on a note which makes you wonder what turn he or she will take next? Are there questions
you still want to ask at the end of the paper that should have been answered in the paper?
Are there other questions which you might want to answer in another paper, your own, for
example?
Obviously you can’t answer all of these questions in your responses, and some of these questions
probably can’t be answered explicitly anyway (although you should keep them in mind). But do
remember that the point of reading each other’s papers is not to grade them (that’s my job!) but
to see how they are put together and to suggest ways they might better further our continuing
conversation.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Writing Groups
On the day the drafts of your papers are due you should bring enough copies for everyone in your
group to have one. Even these drafts should be typewritten if at all possible. You should arrange
with your writing group for a convenient time to meet and discuss your papers among yourselves,
andyoushouldreadandcommentuponthepapersoftheothermembersofyourgroupsometime
beforeyoumeet. (Youshouldlookovermyhandoutdescribinghowtodothis.)
I will leave the format of your actual meetings up to you, but you will probably find them most
useful if you keep them somewhat informal. You might want to begin by asking the author to tell
5

you briefly his or her sense of what the paper is about, and what he or she has learned or proven
by writing it. (There is much to be learned from those occasions where the author describes a
different—and usually better—paper than the one you have read.) You will also find that time
spent on examining what the author is trying to show will generally prove more useful than time
spent editing and correcting small errors, although time spent on organization and style can be
well spent also. If you do discuss style, be sure not to merely say that the style is vague, or worse,
boring—specify instead some of the things the author must add to make the meaning clear, or
specify some of the things the author need not say and should omit to avoid insulting the reader’s
intelligence.
When the final draft is due, please turn in the revised version—and make sure it is revised rather
than merely corrected—along with all the copies of the first version. I won’t be grading the draft,
obviously, and I won’t be grading the marginal comments you make in each others’ work either
(althoughItaketheseveryseriously),butIwouldliketoseejusthowdifferentthedraftsandfinal
versionsareandwhatyouhavelearnedfromtalkingwitheachother.
spans.label: ASSIGN

doc_id: 261PHYS 18B ALL Introductory Laboratory II Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden
school: School of Science, Engineering, and Technology
course_name: 261PHYS 18B ALL Introductory Laboratory II Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden & Seth Fraden

spans.text:
The goal of this class is to allow you to have a hands-on, lab-based experience in the hopes of
demystifying physics and demonstrating deep connections to other disciplines like biology
and chemistry. By the end of the class, you will learn how to design experiments to address
scientific problems. To do so, you will work with and think critically about data, synthesize
concepts in physics, chemistry, biology and beyond, and communicate science in a variety of
ways. This semester we will continue to develop tools for data analysis while focusing on
new contexts of physics including electricity, magnetism, and optics, and ultimately crafting
your own multi-week research projects.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Contact Information:
Instructor: Prof. Seth Fraden
Office: Abelson 214
Contact: fraden@brandeis.edu
Communication: Course announcements will be made by email and through Moodle.
PHYS-18B-ALL. All the content for the course will be here.
PHYS-18B-(1 - 5). There are five sections, 1 - 5. All assignments will be submitted and
corrections will be received from this site.
For questions regarding course material or concepts, you can email the instructor. I will do
my best to reply within 1 business day. You are encouraged to send a follow-up email if you
do not receive a response within 1 day.
For logistical and lab-related questions, please email your TA (you can cc’ the instructor).
You TA is the best resource for lab specific questions.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
By the end of this course, you should be able to:
 Perform basic biophysical laboratory techniques such as image analysis, scientific
computing, microscopy, etc.
 Create a simple laboratory protocol using web and peer reviewed resources.
 Quantitatively analyze, interpret, and troubleshoot data from scientific experiments.
 Test the validity of various models against collected data.
 Formulate reasonable conclusions when presented scientific data and design rational
hypotheses.
 Think, read and write critically about science.
 Develop and perform an independent research project.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Overview:
Overall Structure:
There will be two primary sessions for the course:
1. Weekly lectures: a review of important concepts in physics and in data analysis that
are relevant to the labs. There are no live lectures. All lectures will be recorded with
links accessible on Moodle.
2. Lab sessions: All lab sessions are in person in 116 Abelson. They are hands-on
opportunity to collect / analyse data, and produce a summary of your work, as a lab
report and / or presentation.
You will receive weekly feedback from your TAs regarding your work.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Preparing for lab:
You must read the lab manual before coming to lab. Be sure to bring a calculator, paper,
and/or computer to every lab. You will need them for your calculations. Make sure you have
Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, or an equivalent tool for recording data in spreadsheets.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
The lab session will begin with a short review of the concepts related to the experiment at the
start of each lab. You will work in groups of 2-3 people to collect and analyse your data.
Assigned groups may be changed throughout the semester.
Your TA is available for questions during your lab session. TAs will also hold limited office
hours outside of section time.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Expectations:
I expect that both students and instructors will adhere to the following:
• All communication should be respectful and professional, whether with students, TAs
or the instructor, and whether in person or via email.
• Feedback should be constructive and should provide specific suggestions for
improvement.
• Everyone should participate and engage in a positive manner, and to the best of their
ability.
• Everyone should exercise reasonable patience, empathy and understanding.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
• Phys18a/b is a 2-credit course, which has the expectation of 90 hours work over the
semester. We have assigned 6 labs and expect you to spend 10 hours / lab. Roughly, 2
hours on the pre-lab, 3 hours for the lab and 5 hours for the post-lab. The capstone
project is spread over 3 weeks. Expect to spend 30 hours total on the capstone project.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Late Assignments:
You are entitled to 7 total late days over the course of the semester. If you intend to use a
late day, please specify this at the top of your assignment. Late assignments will not be
accepted beyond these 7 late days, except in exceptional cases limited to health and family
emergencies. Late days cannot be applied to time-sensitive assignments such as pre-labs,
presentations, peer reviews, etc.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Absences:
Attendance in lab is mandatory, and there will be no make-up labs. You are entitled to 1
excused lab. Reasonable excuses and exceptional cases include health emergencies, family
emergencies, religious holidays or Brandeis-related extra-academic conflicts. Unexcused
absences result in a zero.
If you need to miss a lab, please let your TA and instructor know well in advance and
they will make arrangements.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Grading and Evaluation:
This course is a lab-based introductory, algebra-based physics class. All assignments,
experiments, and assessments are designed with at least one if not more of the following
objectives in mind:
 To teach you several basic physics concepts and how to apply them in an
experimental setting;
 To familiarize you with several common techniques, pieces of equipment, and
analysis tools used in modern biophysics laboratories;
 To teach you how to design your own experiment, test your own hypothesis, and
critically and objectively interpret your results;
 To introduce you to practical skills such as estimation, dimensional analysis, and
scaling;
 To familiarize you with working collaboratively with colleagues and peers;
 To help enhance your scientific communication skills.
Course Grades
All sections will be graded on the same absolute scale. If there is significant variation in
grades between sections due to TA judgement, the instructor will consider curving the grade
between sections, but this is very unlikely.
>= 93.00 A 73.00 - 76.99 C
90.00 - 92.99 A- 70.00 - 72.99 C-
87.00 - 89.99 B+ 67.00 - 69.99 D+
83.00 - 86.99 B 63.00 - 66.99 D
80.00 - 82.99 B- 60.00 - 62.99 D-
77.00 - 79.99 C+ <= 59.99 F
Your grade in this class will be determined by the following.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Lab Reports (30%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Learning how to analyze, interpret, and communicate experimental findings are key
components of this course. Labs 1, 3 and 5 will have lab reports. Your lab report will present
and interpret data, formulate hypotheses, discuss sources of uncertainty and the limitations of
your experiments, draw conclusions, etc.

Each group member will produce an individual lab report, no more than 3-4 pages long (not
including figures and tables). Your lab report should be typed, concise, clear, and insightful.
Lab reports should not take more than 5 hours to complete. You may not cheat or plagiarize.
This includes, but is not limited to, paraphrasing another person’s lab report, replicating
sections of another report and presenting it as your own, or relying on online resources or AI
assistive programs to support your writing. Academic integrity will be strictly enforced.
Each lab report is due at the beginning of your section to your TA at the specified
deadline. Lab reports are graded according to the lab report rubric. You will receive feedback
within a week of your submission from your TA.
Correcting and resubmitting assignments. You may correct and resubmit each Lab Report
once if you choose. For each corrected answer, you must write a few sentences describing the
shortcomings and inaccuracies of your original answer, and how you have addressed those
issues in your corrected answer. Your final grade on Labs 1, 3 and 5 will be computed by
averaging together the original and resubmitted scores.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Research Memos (20%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Labs 2, 4 and 6 will have Research Memos, instead of Lab Reports. The goal of the research
memo is to help you to develop your research project by reflecting on labs in the course. A
research memo is a set of reflections after a lab that helps you to synthesize what you have
learned. In this class, you will need to answer all the questions in the discussion part of your
post-lab and reflect on how the lab relates to your capstone project. There are no resubmits
for Research Memos.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Capstone Research Project (40%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: See project assignment for details.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Participation and Professionalism (10%)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This class relies on collaboration between lab mates. Your best opportunities to learn and
succeed are by seeking help, first, from your classmates, and, then, from your TA and your
instructor. I expect that all students will participate in creating a respectful, encouraging,
professional learning environment, which promotes healthy debate and questions.
Your participation and help maintaining a professional environment will be determined from
your engagement with the following aspects of the course. Remember, there are many ways
to participate that do not require you to speak up in class if you are uncomfortable doing so.
• Demonstrating Preparedness for Class: Completing and submitting pre-lab assignments
(answers should reflect your best attempts at correctly and completely answering the
questions provided); coming to class with relevant questions for your TA; demonstrating
an overall understanding of the lab goals; participating in class-wide discussions.
• Collaboration: Working well and efficiently with your group; dividing and collaborating
on collecting and analysing data; ensuring each group member fully understands the task
before moving on.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
• Lab Etiquette: Investing effort into your experiment; collecting a sufficient amount and
quality of data needed for the assignments; using equipment as directed and intended;
returning equipment to the TA after use; cleaning your workbench after each lab.
• Respect: Maintaining a respectful, professional tone in conversations, student hours, in
the laboratory, and via email; respecting the unique skills and ideas brought by a
teammate and ask/answer questions respectfully; respecting the time of your colleagues,
TA and instructor; respecting your physical space and lab equipment.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Academic Integrity:
Academic integrity is central to the mission of educational excellence at Brandeis University.
Each student is expected to turn in work completed independently. Violations of University
policy on academic integrity, described in Section 4 of Rights and Responsibilities, may
result in failure in the course or on the assignment, and could end in suspension from the
University. If you are in doubt about the instructions for any assignment in this course, you
must ask for clarification. Please see Section 4 of Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities.
A note about ChatGPT and assistive AI use in the classroom: ChatGPT can be a resource for
your learning, but not if it is doing the work for you. In a writing heavy and communication-
based course, I expect you to practice and refine the skill of writing on your own, with your
TA or with use of other resources; that is, use of AI for written text will NOT be allowed.
Nevertheless, as a physicist, I often use crowd-source, consolidated resources like Wikipedia,
Stack Exchange, Quora or ChatGPT to better understand concepts. Be wary of
unverifiable/untrustworthy information, but, if you need an alternative explanation for
material seen in class, feel free to use AI for concept-based learning.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

Your academic success depends on your personal health and well-being. Recognize that

spans.text:
Laptop computer and cell phone use
Laptop and cell phone use during a language class is a distraction. I rely on your maturity to keep your
devices turned off and put away for the duration of class. Using devices during class time or leaving class to
use them in the halls will result in a lowered participation grade. Please refer to the Participation Grading
Rubric on Moodle for details.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: During class, you will be asked to communicate in French to the best of your abilities. Do not be\ndiscouraged by errors, challenges, or corrections – this is an important part of the process. Indeed, if you\nare not making mistakes and are already speaking in perfect French, this is not the right class for you!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: If you\nfind that you have questions about placement, please do not hesitate to speak to me about options.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
French 32 is designed to help students improve their command of French, specifically to develop their
speaking skills and to enrich their knowledge of Francophone society, boosting them to the ACTFL
intermediate level. The class will work on all skill areas (speaking, listening, writing, reading, culture) while
addressing themes pertinent to the modern Francophone world that generate discussion or controversy,
such as the role of artificial intelligence in our lives, gender identity, racial identity, and a variety of cultural
and historical topics. Using a variety of authentic French language media, we will work on your ability to
discuss personal and social topics, support ideas and opinions with facts and personal experience, read
short cultural and literary texts, participate in class discussions, and express your opinions in writing.
Assessment is based on daily homework assignments, a series of oral and written exams/ projects, a record
of attendance and participation, and a series of final assignments.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Please note that in this class, called “Controverses,” we will learn vocabulary and grammar structures that
allow you to engage in healthy debate with francophone peers. As does Brandeis, I consider the diversity
that students bring to the course an invaluable resource. I will present materials and activities that are
respectful of diversity: gender, sexuality, physical ability, age, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, race, and
culture. Each day, we view images and read texts that may reflect other realities and worldviews, which is the
purpose of the humanities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
This semester you will
- develop analytic tools to examine and better understand francophone culture, particularly issues of
controversy that concern young adults (see above).
-improve your confidence in using spoken French in informal and formal situations.
-expand your vocabulary and grammar skills to communicate effectively in different situations while
reviewing “Novice” vocabulary topics, such as family, home, and food.
-improve your pronunciation by focusing on the most difficult sounds of the language (/r/, nasals, etc.).
-express your ideas about past, present, and future events and your opinions and beliefs in a clear, concise,
and persuasive manner in written and oral forms.
-read, analyze, and discuss short literary works, images, films, advertisements, and articles at an intermediate
low-mid level.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Outcomes:
We will measure your ability to reach the above goals by requiring you to
-incorporate pertinent cultural material into class presentations, writings, projects, and exams.
-present phonetically and grammatically accurate verbal reports and conversations.
-manipulate new and old vocabulary and grammar in different modalities (written and oral).
-compose short reports, posters, reactions, opinions, and analyses.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Social justice, attitudes, and behaviors: Learning French will enable you to gain a deeper understanding of
different regions of the francophone world and support your participation as informed citizens in a global
society.
This course will specifically
• expand your knowledge and awareness of traditions and artistic expressions of different
francophone cultures.
• encourage you to reflect on your culture, and weigh its similarities and differences with those of
other cultures.
• better understand what connects people worldwide – linguistically, culturally, and otherwise –
through study of texts, film, and music.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Credit Hours: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a\nminimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class, not including exam preparation.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Materials: All course materials are found on the course Moodle page.
-Controverses, Cahier d’activités, Didier Bertrand, Janet Solberg. Cengage, 2016, Third edition: All
exercises are available on the Moodle page.

*Use www.wordreference.com or linguee.fr (online bilingual dictionaries) – not Google Translate.

the workbook (scanned for you on Moodle) for the date indicated. Corrections should be made in a

either on paper or as a pdf.

Because language is a social and interactive phenomenon, attendance and participation are essential to your
success in this class. Learning to speak another language demands in-class engagement. Participation is
noted on a daily basis.

You are allowed four absences

class equal one absence. Each absence that exceeds the allowed number will result in a lower grade in two

contact their Academic Advisor; the Advisor will contact your professor about excused absences. If you

Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Using phrases, ideas, or even fragments from undocumented Internet sources, native speakers, ChatGPT

other A.I., or other “helpers” is not a reflection of your abilities and will not end up helping you learn the

Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to

doc_id: 253FREN 126B 1 La place de la nature dans le monde culturel francophone Hollie Harder
course_name: 253FREN 126B 1 La place de la nature dans le monde culturel francophone Hollie Harder

spans.text: Room TBA\nMonday, Wednesday, Thursday 11:15 a.m. -12:05 p.m.\n(in person)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
In this course, you will deepen your knowledge and understanding of the role of nature in the
Francophone world by examining some of the cultural, political, literary, and artistic
principles that contributed to creating its unique place in the world.
For example, we will discover how, during the Renaissance, the purpose of nature seemed
limited to satisfying the needs, desires and pleasures of human beings. We will try to make
sense of what drove French culture to popularize the notion of le jardin français with its
conical trees and geometrical shrubbery, we will explore what made people around the
Francophone world withdraw into nature as though it were a protective haven, and
subsequently abuse it, especially in the former French colonies in the Caribbean and in
Western Africa, for the purpose of productivity and profit. You will examine your own
0

attitudes toward nature and how your "culture of nature" compares to that of the
Francophone world.
spans.label: DESCRIP

equivalent. If you have questions about whether this course is the right one for you, please

spans.text:
Learning Goals and Outcomes
Core skills
Specific learning goals and outcomes for each unit are outlined on Moodle. By the end of the
semester, you will be able to
• communicate ePectively with others about concrete and abstract topics that you
have explored or researched (interpersonal communication), exchanging ideas with
others without mistakes that impede understanding and transmission;
• present information and express your thoughts to others in written and spoken French
(presentational communication) without mistakes that impede communication;
and
• quickly understand main ideas and arguments when reading and listening and
explain key supporting information that clarifies larger cultural notions (interpretive
communication)
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Knowledge
Specific learning goals and outcomes for each unit are outlined on Moodle.
By the end of the semester, you will know
• about more complex intellectual, social, political, artistic, and cultural aspects of the
French-speaking world (intercultural investigation), for example
-- current policies and practices that are emblematic of the role that nature plays
in French-speaking cultures
--the evolution of thought and the development of practices that have led to current
notions about nature and the environment

--the culturally unique ways in which attitudes toward nature and the environment
have progressed in the Francophone world during the twenty-first century
• how to interact appropriately in nuanced situations with French speakers
(intercultural interaction).
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Global Engagement and Social Justice through World Languages
and Cultures at Brandeis
FREN 126B contributes to the Brandeis Core curriculum through its role in
the Global Engagement / World Languages and Cultures requirement.
Proficiency in another language is a cornerstone of global citizenship, and
it is essential for ePective communications and collaboration among
people of diPerent cultures. Learning another language also plays a
central role in cultivating a sense of social justice and in deepening and
broadening sensitivity to issues of inclusion and exclusion.
The process of learning another language inspires open-mindedness and adaptability,
awakens curiosity and creativity, fosters innovative paths of inquiry and discovery, and
builds a deeper understanding of yourself and others.
This course will help you to develop and test your ability to think critically. Through the
analysis and evaluation of texts, situations, films, and images, and you will piece together
meaning from your examination of the products, practices, perspectives, and power
structures you find in Francophone cultures. This process of reflection will challenge you to
identify your own social, cultural, and political traditions and assumptions, and it may
prompt you to reevaluate your own ways of thinking.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Teaching and Learning Strategies in FREN 126B
This course is student-centered and depends on learner engagement, interaction, and
participation.
In a traditional classroom, the teacher is often the center of the learning experience and
takes the "active" role, while the students assume a more "passive" or receptive role.
In this course, you will take an active role every day in your learning, interacting with
fellow students and your professor in order to build an intellectual community.
This course is based on a flipped-classroom model.
You will often interact with new information first as homework. We will use class time to
discuss any questions you have about the new material and then put the new (and already-
familiar) components into practice. This hands-on experience in class enables you to focus
on the processing part of learning (synthesizing, analyzing, applying, evaluating, etc.) as you
use language to question and communicate.
spans.label: DESCRIP

(https://www.brandeis.edu/academic-services/advising/people/index.html)
to discuss possible funding options or other solutions.
Please note, too, that Brandeis University has an emergency fund
(https://www.brandeis.edu/student-financial-services/financial-aid/brandeis-emergency-

spans.text:
Academic Integrity and Generative AI
Taking a course in a language other than English requires a
special commitment to academic integrity. Our goal in this
course is for you to improve your skills in speaking, reading,
listening, and writing in French; using AI tools in the form of
ChatGPT and translation tools where you outsource the work
of this course prevents you from mastering French.
EAective learning requires you to work through diAiculties
that you may have in expressing your ideas and in understanding what you hear and
read. EAective learning requires you to use resources to figure out solutions to better
internalize them so you can mentally retrieve them and use them in the future.
All the work you do in this course must be your own. It is not acceptable to ask that an entity
(such as a tutor, a fellow student, a friend, a relative, or an AI model) help you to edit,
correct, translate, or write any part of an assignment.
Representing work generated by artificial intelligence as one's own work is academically
dishonest. Please properly cite any sources that you use.
Assignments that are partially or completely written by an AI model will not be accepted, and
they will be treated as plagiarism.
The goal of this course is for you to enhance your learning and understanding. ChatGPT and
other AI tools are not a replacement for your own critical thinking and original ideas, and
inappropriate use of them will prevent you from making progress in this course.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Brandeis Academic Integrity Code for oral and written tasks in a foreign-language class
Academic work in language classes requires you to use
language creatively to express your thoughts and opinions.
The language you use must be verifiably your own.
I encourage you to:
● use class materials as a source of language models (culturally and linguistically
appropriate ways of expressing ideas);
• use dictionaries and grammar tables (including digital resources) to check forms
(such as verb endings, adjectives, etc.), spelling, and meanings;
● search authentic sources to identify expressions and phrases you may need for your
own writing;
● proofread your own work; read it aloud and hear how it sounds as well as how it looks.
I encourage you to use class materials and dictionaries in preparing the drafts and final
versions of your essays. However, please do not:
• consult native speakers or others about your work
• use Al models or translators (such as Google Translate) while composing or
correcting your work.
Essays that in style, syntactic, and lexical complexity do not correspond to your level of
language proficiency will stand out as being suspect, and you will be asked to provide an
explanation.
PLEASE TAKE NOTE: DOCUMENT THE HELP YOU GET ON ASSIGNMENTS
If you consult outside sources to complete an assignment, please indicate the type and
source of help you received. For example:
• I used the online Larousse French-English dictionary [www.larousse.fr] to find “to
expect,” “lighthouse,” “to challenge,” and “with regard to” for this exercise;
• I used the Wikipédia site on “greetings in French”
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salutation] to help me explain my ideas.
• Please use the Brandeis Library Guide when you are citing AI for this course:
https://guides.library.brandeis.edu/citeai
spans.label: INTEGRITY

University-wide Policies and Resources
Academic Integrity

Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of
academic integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the
student's own ePort. Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student
to serious penalties, which may include failure on the assignment, failure in the course,
suspension from the University or other sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University
Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity.
Students may be required to submit work via TurnItIn.com or similar software to verify
originality. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they apply
to a specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that
course or assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic
dishonesty will be forwarded to the Department of Student Rights and Community

Sources.

doc_id: 261FIN 181A 1 Banks and Financial Stability Carol Osler
course_name: 261FIN 181A 1 Banks and Financial Stability Carol Osler

spans.text: Professor Osler
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Mondays & Wednesdays, 2:30-3:50, Lee Hall
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: cosler@brandeis.edu Office hours: Wednesdays, 10:30-12:00\nPersonal website: here Office: Sachar 205
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
OVERVIEW
Banks have been the beating heart of world commerce for over 500 years. A sound banking
system is essential for robust economic growth. By investing depositor funds wisely, banks
help individuals increase their wealth and reduce their risk. Bank profits reflect the expertise of
bankers in assessing and managing risk. They are good at identifying potential deadbeat
borrowers, for example, and they know how derivatives can protect a bank from interest-rate
swings.
The banking industry is inherently unstable due to the risk of bank runs and fraud. The failure
of a single US bank in 2023, Silicon Valley Bank, triggered a massive contraction in the start-
up sector. The failure of a single dominant bank can stop an entire economy in its tracks, as the
Dominican Republic learned in 2003-2004. Because the banking industry is so economically
important and so unstable, it is more regulated than any other industry. The best way to regulate
banks is hotly disputed, however.
This course introduces you banks and the banking industry. We’ll discuss the structure of a
normal bank, the risks it faces, and how it manages those risks. We’ll also discuss why the
banking industry is potentially unstable, why and how it is regulated by governments, and why
some regulatory strategies are controversial. More broadly, we’ll study the origins of banking
crises and why they occur frequently.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisites: Financial Management (Fin 103) or Financial Economics (ECON 171a) and\nBUS 6a, Financial Accounting (neither finance nor accounting can be taken concurrently with\nthis course). Comfort with basic Excel skills.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
LEARNING GOALS
1. Understanding of a bank’s balance sheet and bank performance is measured.
2. Understanding of the risks facing a bank and how they manage those risks.
• Credit risk  Interest-rate risk
• Liquidity risk  Currency risk
3. Understanding of the inherent instability of fractional-reserve banking systems.
• Retail and wholesale bank runs  Fraud
4. Understanding of how and why banks are regulated and the risks that arise when banks are
not properly regulated. Familiarity with associated controversies.
• Deposit insurance  Bank equity as an option
• Capital adequacy  Gamble for resurrection

• Loan-loss reserves  Bank charters
• Liquidity reserves
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
COURSEWORK
1. Class participation
 Attendance: This is an in-person class; attendance will be taken.
 You can miss at most two of the semester’s 26 classes without penalty. If you are ill, let me
know in advance so I can try to set up a Zoom link.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
2. Assignments: Practice is essential for developing any skill, including the skills involved in bank risk
management. At least once each week you will practice using the concepts just covered in class.
Guidance for assignments is provided via examples or detailed instructions.
 Each assignment is designed for you to practice using specific analytical tools and methods
demonstrated in class. Success thus depends on studying the text or lecture notes while
working on the assignments. No credit will be given when the lecture notes are ignored.
 The single most helpful suggestion for doing well in this course is to study the lecture
notes while doing the assignments. The surest way to do poorly is to assume you can do
these otherwise.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
 Your own work: You may not rely on AI or classmates. You must explain everything in your
own words and do the calculations on your own. If not, the assignment gets zero credit.
 You are welcome to work with others to understand the problems or to master Excel techniques
(but not to write up the answers).
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: 3. Quizzes: There will be a 5-10 minute quiz at the beginning of each class. Most question will cover\nthe most recent class; other questions will cover earlier material.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 4. Team Bank Analysis: You and another student will analyze a bank of your choice.\n Further information on the bank analysis is provided at the end of this syllabus and on Moodle.

spans.text:
ACADEMIC INTEGRITY: You, as a member of the University community, must maintain the highest
standards of academic integrity. You will not submit work that is falsified, that copies anyone else’s text
for any reason unless it is in quotations and cited (plagiarism), that is the result of someone else’s effort.
You may not use AI for any purpose except polishing your prose. You will cite your sources, including
any AI for polishing prose, and provide complete, verifiable references. If you violate this or the
University standards for academic integrity the assignment will get zero credit. In addition, you could fail
the course or be suspended from the University (see Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities). I do

not hesitate to forward potential instances of academic dishonesty to the Department of Student Rights and
Community Standards.
Please reach out to me before submitting a given assignment if you are unsure about integrity standards.
Guidelines for citations and references are provided on the course Moodle site.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
STANDARDS OF BEHAVIOR: Your behavior towards everyone in this class community must be
consistently constructive, kind, respectful, and curious.
Brandeis University is committed to providing its students, faculty and staff with an environment
conducive to learning and working, where all people are treated with respect and dignity. You must refrain
from any behavior toward members of our Brandeis community, including students, faculty, staff, and
guests, that intimidates, threatens, harasses, or bullies. [Brandeis Business Conduct Policy p. 2, 2020. (2)
Student Rights & Responsibilities, p. 11, 2020 ed.]

spans.text: DISABILITIES: I am happy to accommodate documented disabilities. To ensure I can provide such\naccommodation by 26 January 2026 I must receive your accommodation letter from Student Accessibility\nSupport and you must specify your choices for exams (quiet room, etc.).
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
STUDENT SUPPORT: Brandeis University is committed to supporting all our students so they can thrive.
Support resources for you include:
• Support at Brandeis • University Ombuds
• The Care Team • Office of Equal Opportunity.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: RECORDING OF CLASSES: You may not record classes on your own without my express permission.\nIf the class is recorded you may not share the URL and/or password to anyone unaffiliated with this\ncourse.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
PROJECT DETAILS
I. 4-Minute Presentation
You will introduce your classmates to an important type of bank asset, liability, customer
type, or regulatory body. You will provide the following:
 Definition
 Function and importance for the banking industry
 Brief history: Origins and past growth, current size measures
 Something intriguing – Scandal? A recent dramatic change?
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: II. Team Bank Analysis\nYou a partner will choose a bank and analyze it throughout the semester.\n• Everything you need to know is on Moodle under “Guidelines for Bank Analysis.”
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
• EVERYTHING you submit must be your own words. In this course it is not permissible to
use someone else’s words or AI-sourced content. This prohibition holds regardless of
whether quotation marks or citations are provided.
• You must cite your sources and provide complete, verifiable references, including whether
you used AI to polish prose.

• Multiple teams can choose a given bank.

spans.text:
Course Description
Congratulations—you have made it to the next level! Russian 30 will launch you into a much higher
orbit within the language universe and open new horizons for you! While continuing to develop your
ability to communicate effectively and accurately in Russian and to explore Russian culture, you will
expand your vocabulary, sharpen your pragmatic skills, and complete your understanding of the
Russian grammatical system.
Russian 30 is the first semester of the two-semester sequence of Intermediate Russian. Although Brandeis
requires only three semesters of a foreign language, we strongly encourage you to complete this sequence
and take Russian 40 when it is offered next year!
spans.label: DESCRIP
DESCRIP

spans.text:
Required Materials
1

Brandeis University, GRALL Intermediate Russian 30A
Fall 2025
For the first few weeks, we will continue using the textbook Russian Stage One: Live from Moscow:
Volume II (your textbook from Russian 20) and the accompanying workbook, as we still need to finish
chapters 11, 12 and 13. Then we will start working with a new textbook—Этажи: Intermediate Russian
by S. Nazarova and E. Dengub (Georgetown University Press 2021).
You will find all the unit plans, homework assignments, links to instructional videos, readings, study
guides, and other materials related to the course on the Russian 30 course page on Moodle.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Your responsibilities
Statement on Brandeis academic integrity
Academic integrity is central to the mission of educational excellence at Brandeis University. Each
student is expected to turn in work completed independently, except when assignments
specifically authorize collaborative effort. Violations of University policies on academic integrity,
described in Section Three of Rights and Responsibilities, may result in failure of the course or on
the assignment, or in suspension or dismissal from the University. If you are in doubt about the
instructions for any assignment in this course, it is your responsibility to ask your instructor for
clarification.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Engagement, participation, and punctuality\nYou are expected to contribute to a respectful and positive learning environment.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Cell phones and social media
I rely on your maturity to know that any use of cell phones and social media sites during our class
meetings is disrespectful and distracting. Phones should be turned off and out of sight, while class
is in session. Likewise, please close down all social media applications and turn off notifications on
your computers.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Group dynamics
This class relies heavily on all of you working together. Some of you are faster at grasping new
grammatical concepts and memorizing new vocabulary, while others require more time to
process language-related information. Personality types also play a significant role in how people
learn languages: some may be reluctant to speak in front of other people, while others are more
outgoing and will not hesitate to experiment. Please be patient with each other and always remain
polite! It is important to keep in mind that you NEED each other to activate what you have learned
in class and at home—so, please be grateful for having your classmates, who can help you with
this task!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Notice of accommodation
Brandeis seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations as outlined in an accommodations letter, please talk with me and present your
letter of accommodation as soon as you can. In order to provide accommodations during a test, I
must see the letter more than 48 hours in advance. If you have any questions about documenting a
disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support (SAS) at
781.736.3470 or access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text: Communication
spans.label: ADMIN

Please make sure your Brandeis e-mail address is active and check it frequently! I will communicate a lot
of important information about the course to you via the Course Announcement function on our Moodle
page or by directly e-mailing you.
You can always contact me by e-mail with questions related to the course. I will try to respond within 24
hours. Please note that I will not check my e-mail after 10:00 p.m. EST!

spans.text:

Your final grade will be calculated as follows:
• Class participation
o 46 classes out of 50 possible x 5 points each = 230 points
• Recitation participation
o 10 recitations out of 12 possible x 5 points each = 50 points
• Homework = 240 points total
o daily homework: 44 out of 48 possible x 5 points each = 220 points
• Weekly quizzes = 100 points total
o grammar and vocabulary quizzes: 10 out of 13 possible x 10 points each = 100 points
• Unit tests (4) x 50 points each = 200 points
• Final Exam/Task = 100 points
• Final Project = 100 points

Letter Grade Percentage

spans.text:
Components of Course Work Explained
Participation (230 points)
Your participation will be marked on a very simple scale:
5 points – you are on time, prepared (i.e., you have done your homework and are prepared to
work with new material in class), and actively participate in classwork
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
0 points – you are not prepared (i.e., you have not done your homework and, as a result, your
participation in classwork is limited)
Please note that you are allowed to miss up to 4 classes for any reason without penalty—the total
score for participation takes this into account!
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Recitation hour (50 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This course requires that you meet with an instructor for one additional hour per week. It is called the
“Recitation Hour,” which in a language-learning course means that you will practice phonetics, do
selected homework exercises together, practice conversation, prepare for oral exams, and have an extra
opportunity to work with authentic language and culture materials. Recitations will also be reserved for
short weekly vocabulary quizzes.
The exact meeting times for the recitations will be scheduled at the beginning of the semester by common
consent. Recitations will start in the 2nd week of the semester. There will be a total of 12 recitations. You
must participate in at least 10 recitations, in order to receive a full credit for this portion of your final
grade.
Please note that your participation in the Recitation Hour is mandatory! It is as important as your
attendance of our regular class sessions, and it is subject to the same participation and classroom policy!
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Making up for Missed Classes
While you are expected to actively participate in each class and recitation session, we recognize the
inherent uncertainties of real life. If you have to miss a class for a legitimate reason and want to make up
for it, you need to contact your instructor. You will be expected to complete the list of tasks provided by
the instructor.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Textbook/workbook homework must be submitted on the day it is due. If you are not able to make it to
class, you should still submit your homework on time, in order to receive a full credit. The expectations
for your homework are as follows: it has to be complete (i.e., all the assignments should be done), it has to
be at least 90% correct, and it should not contain any systematic errors indicating that re-learning and re-
focusing is required.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Homework (220 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
There are three possible scores you may receive for your homework:
• full credit – 5 points (if your work meets the expectations outlined above)
• no credit – 0 points (if the homework was submitted more than a week after the date it was due or
if it does not meet the expectations of being at least 90% correct and complete)
• partial credit (if your homework does not meet the expectations outlined above, you will have the
option of correcting all circled mistakes and resubmitting the work NO LATER THAN BY THE END
OF THE UNIT. A full credit of 5 points will be given only when all corrections had been made and
the work was resubmitted. If you do not submit your corrections, you will receive a partial credit.
Only one resubmission per homework is allowed.)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Please note:\nIf your homework was submitted more than a week late, it will be accepted and checked for\nerrors, but you will NOT receive any credit for it. No resubmissions will be allowed for late homework.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: Corrections/resubmissions must be received by the last day of the unit (usually, the unit-test day).\nHomework resubmitted after the unit test will be accepted, but you will only receive a partial credit for it.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: This 4-credit-hour course is based on the expectation that students will study on a\nregular basis and spend 10 to 12 hours per week preparing for classes (including\nwritten homework, instructional videos, preparation for quizzes and exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Vocabulary and grammar quizzes (100 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Every week, you will have grammar and vocabulary quizzes. Vocabulary quizzes will mostly take place\nduring the recitations. Grammar quizzes will take place in class. You should expect to be quizzed on any\nlexical and grammatical material that will have been covered until that point.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
There are three possible scores you may receive on a quiz:
• full credit – 10 points (if your work meets the expectations of being at least 90% correct)
• partial credit (if your work is below expectations, but still has some substance and is more
than 80% correct)
• no credit – 0 points (for missed quizzes and for work that has little substance and is less than
80% correct)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Unit tests (200 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Tests will be scheduled at the end of each unit. You will receive specific instructions on how to prepare for\nsuch tests.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Final exam (100 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: There will be a final task due at the end of the semester during the Finals Week. The format of this final\nproject will be determined in collaboration with students.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Final Project (100)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Starting in the middle of September, every Thursday class will be held on zoom—we will be meeting
with students from the Pedagogical University in Yaroslavl, Russia, and chatting with them (in Russian!)
about various subjects. These conversations will be part of our regular class activities—we will prepare
for them together, you will receive homework and other assignments for these meetings, and your final
project will also be based on the information that you will have obtained in the course of these
encounters. You will be provided with detailed instructions for your final project early in the semester.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Please be advised that the Moodle gradebook provides you with a fairly
accurate but still approximate calculation of your grade. The instructor
reserves the right to adjust the total number of points and the grade at the end
of the semester. I invite you to think more about developing your proficiency
in the language rather than focusing on the mechanics of how the grade is
calculated—the ultimate goal for you is to gain an ability to communicate
effectively in Russian!
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
What to Do if You Need Extra Help
Please do not wait until problems start piling up! There are three people in the Russian Language
Program you can turn to for help: your instructor and/or a BUGS tutor. You can come to either of us with
your questions, but it also may be helpful to differentiate your sources of support:
o If you need another explanation of a particular concept or want to practice vocabulary,
pronunciation, conversation, seek help from your instructor (Ирина Евгеньевна
Дубинина) during office hours or by appointment;
o If you would like extra practice of grammar, vocabulary or conversation, sign up to meet
with Matvey Agronovskiy, BUGS tutor for Russian, magranovskiy@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Other Useful Contacts
The Russian Program’s Undergraduate Representatives for the 2024-25 academic year are Zoe Potolsky
(zoepotolsky@brandeis.edu) and Isabella Seemann (isabellaseemann@brandeis.edu) – note that
Isabella is away on a study-abroad in the fall. They can be a wonderful resource not only for your
questions about majoring/minoring in Russian, but also about Russian literature, studying habits, or
successful language-learning strategies.
The undergraduate advisor for the Russian Language Program is Professor David Powelstock
(pstock@brandeis.edu). He will help you plan out your Russian Studies major or minor and guide you in
questions related to study-abroad credits.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Statement on Success
Your academic success in this course depends heavily on your personal health and wellbeing. Stress is a
common part of the college experience, and it often can be compounded by unexpected setbacks or life
changes outside the classroom. Your other professors and I strongly encourage you to view challenges as
unavoidable pathways to success. Reflect on your role in taking care of yourself throughout the academic
year, before the pressure over exams and projects begins to mount. Please feel free to reach out to me
about any difficulty you may be facing, if you think that it may impact your performance in this course.
spans.label: ADMIN
